blocks.html:
EXHIBITION I
PREBASSESby A. Grothendieck and J.L. Verdier
(with an appendix by N. Bourbaki)

In numbers 0 to 5 of this presentation, we present the elementary properties, and the more often well known, categories of prebundles∗. These properties are used constantly in the rest of the seminar and their knowledge is essential for the understanding of the following presentations. The demonstrations are immediate; they are the more often omitted. In numbers 6 to 9 some themes used in different occasions later. The reader in a hurry can omit them first. reading. Number 10 sets the terminology used. Appendix 11 is due to N. Bourbaki.

footnotes.html: (∗) The reader can also consult SGA 3 I §§ 1 to 3.

blocks.html: 0. Universe
A universe is a non-empty set \(\mathcal{U}\) which has the following properties:

1. (\(\mathcal{U}\) 1)

    If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) and if \(y \in x\) then \(y \in \mathcal{U}\).

  2. (\(\mathcal{U}\) 2)

    If \(x, y \in \mathcal{U}\), then \(\{x,y\} \in \mathcal{U}\).

  3. (\(\mathcal{U}\) 3)

    If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), then \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}\).

  4. (\(\mathcal{U}\) 4)

    If \((x_i,\ i \in I \in \mathcal{U})\) is an element family of \(\mathcal{U}\), then \(\bigcup_{i\in I} x_i \in \mathcal{U}\).
From the preceding axioms we easily deduce the properties:
— If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), the set \(\{x\}\) belongs to \(\mathcal{U}\).
—If \(x\) is a subset of \(y \in \mathcal{U}\), then \(x \in \mathcal{U}\).
— If \(x,y \in \mathcal{U}\), the pair \((x,y) = \{\{x,y\}, x\}\) (Kuratowski definition) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
— If \(x,y \in \mathcal{U}\), meeting \(x \cup y\) and product \(x \times y\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\).
— If \((x_i,\ i \in I \in \mathcal{U})\) is an element family of \(\mathcal{U}\), the product \(\prod_{i\in I} x_i\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
— If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), then \(\mathrm{card}(x) < \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\) (strictly). In particular the relation \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{U}\) is not verified.
We can therefore carry out all the usual operations of set theory from elements of a universe without, for that, the final result ceasing to be an element of the universe.

The first interest of the notion of universe is to provide a definition of categories usual: the category of sets belonging to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (\(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens), the category topological spaces belonging to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\), the category of commutative groups belonging to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (\(\mathcal{U}\)-Ab), the category of categories belonging to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) ... .

However, the only known universe is the set of symbols of the type \(\{\{\varnothing\}, \{\{\varnothing\}, \varnothing\}\}\) etc. (all the elements of this universe are finite sets and this universe is countable). In particular, we do not know of any universe which contains an element of infinite cardinal. We are therefore led to add to the axioms of set theory the axiom:
(\(\mathcal{U}\)A) For every set \(x\) there exists a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(x \in \mathcal{U}\).

The intersection of a family of universes being a universe, we immediately deduce that everything together is an element of a smaller universe. It can be shown that the axiom (\(\mathcal{U}\)A) is independent of the axioms of set theory.
We will also add the axiom:
(\(\mathcal{U}\)B) Let \(R\{x\}\) be a relation and \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. If there is an element \(y \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(R\{y\}\), then \(\tau_x R\{x\} \in \mathcal{U}\).
The non-contradiction of the axioms (\(\mathcal{U}\)A) and (\(\mathcal{U}\)B) compared to the other axioms of set theory is neither demonstrated nor provable, it seems.

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(c(\mathcal{U})\) be the upper bound of the cardinals of the elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\(c(\mathcal{U}) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\)). Cardinal \(c(\mathcal{U})\) has the following properties:

1. (FI)

    If \(a < c(\mathcal{U})\), then \(2^a < c(\mathcal{U})\).

  2. (FII)

    If \((a_i, i \in I)\) is a family of cardinals strictly less than \(c(\mathcal{U})\) and if \(\mathrm{card}(I)\) is strictly less than \(c(\mathcal{U})\), \(\sum_{i\in I} a_i < c(\mathcal{U})\).
Cardinals that have the properties (FI) and (FII) are called cardinals highly inaccessible.
Cardinal 0 and countable infinite cardinal are strongly inaccessible.
The axiom (\(\mathcal{U}\)A) implies:
(\(\mathcal{U}\)A') Every cardinal is bounded strictly by a strongly inaccessible cardinal.

We can show (11) that reciprocally the non-contradiction of (\(\mathcal{U}\)A') implies the non-contradiction of (\(\mathcal{U}\)A), and that the non-contradiction of these axioms entails that of the axiom (\(\mathcal{U}\)B).

Let us call artinian set any set \(E\) such that there do not exist infinite families \((x_n, n \in \mathbb{N})\) such as \(x_0 \in E\), \(x_{n+1} \in x_n\). We can then show [loc. cit.] that there is a one-to-one correspondence between the highly inaccessible cardinals and the universes artinians, defined as follows: to any strongly inaccessible cardinal \(c\) we match the unique Artinian universe \(\mathcal{U}_c\) such that \[ \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}_c) = c . \] Let \(c < c'\) be two highly inaccessible cardinals. We have: \[ \mathcal{U}_c \in \mathcal{U}_{c'}, \] In particular the Artinian universes of cardinals lower than a given cardinal form a well-ordered set (for the membership relationship). The axiom (\(\mathcal{U}\)A') is equivalent to the axiom:
(\(\mathcal{U}\)A'') Every Artinian set is an element of an Artinian universe.
Note that all the usual sets (\(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{Q}\), ...) are Artinian sets.
1. \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. Set Pre-Bundles

In the rest of the seminar and unless expressly stated otherwise, the universes considered will have an element of infinite cardinal. Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. It is said that a set is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small (or, when no confusion results, small) if it is isomorphic to an element of \(\mathcal{U}\). We also use the terminology: small group, small ring, small category... We will often assume, without explicit mention, that the diagrams, spaces topological, sets of indices... with which we work are \(\in \mathcal{U}\), where at least have a cardinal \(\in \mathcal{U}\); however, many categories that one will work with will not be \(\in \mathcal{U}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(C\) be a category. We say that \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category if for any pair \((x,y)\) of objects of \(C\), the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(x,y)\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.
Let \(C\) and \(D\) be two categories and \(\mathrm{Fonct}(C,D)\) be the category of functors of \(C\) in \(D\). We immediately check the following assertions:
a) If \(C\) and \(D\) are elements of a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{U}\)-small) the category \(\mathrm{Fonct}(C,D)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) (resp. is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small).
b) If \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and if \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\mathrm{Fonct}(C,D)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.1.2

Let \(D\) be a category with the following properties:

  1. (C1)

    The set \(\mathrm{ob}(D)\) is contained in the universe \(\mathcal{U}\).

  2. (C2)

    For any pair \((x,y)\) of objects of \(D\), the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_D(x,y)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

(The usual categories constructed from a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) have these two properties: \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens, \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ab, ... ). Let \(C\) be a category belonging to \(\mathcal{U}\). So the category \(\mathrm{Fonct}(C,D)\) does not generally have the properties (C1) and (C2). For example the category \(\mathrm{Fonct}(C, \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) does not have any of the properties (C1) and (C2). This is what which justifies the adopted definition of \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, in preference to the more restrictive notion by conditions (C1) and (C2) above.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2

Let \(C\) be a category. We callpre-bundle category of sets on \(C\) relative to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (or, when no confusion results, category of presheaves on \(C\)) the category of contravariant functors on \(C\) to value in the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets.

We designate by \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) (or more simply, when no confusion results, by \(\widehat{C}\)) the category of set pre-bundles on \(C\) relating to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\). The objects of \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) are called \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundles (or more simply prebundles) on \(C\). When \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, category \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. When \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is not necessarily a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

blocks.title: Construction-definition
blocks.html:
Construction-definition 1.3

Let \(x\) be an object of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\). We call\(\mathcal{U}\)-functor represented by \(x\) the functor \(h_{\mathcal{U}}(x) : C^\circ \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) whose construction suit∗. Let \(y\) be an object of \(C\).

a) If \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), then: \[ h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(y) = \mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x) \]

b) Suppose that \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x)\) is not an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) and is \(R(Z,x,y)\) the relation: "The set \(Z\) is goal of an isomorphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Z\)". We pose then: \[ h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(y) = \tau_Z\, R(Z) . \] (By virtue of the axiom (\(\mathcal{U}\)B), \(h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(y)\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\)). Let \(R'(u,x,y)\) be the relation: "\(u\) is a bijection \(u : \mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(y)\)."

We then ask: \[ \varphi(y,x) = \tau_u\, R'(u) . \]

We will notice that we have, in cases (a) and (b), a canonical isomorphism: \[ \varphi(y,x) : \mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(y) . \] (In case a) \(\varphi(y,x)\) is the identity). Let then \(u : y \to y'\) be an arrow of \(C\). The morphism \(u\) defines, by the composition of morphisms, an application: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_C(u,x) : \mathrm{Hom}_C(y',x) \to \mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x) . \]

We then pose \[ h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)(u) = \varphi(y,x)\, \mathrm{Hom}_C(u,x)\, \varphi(y',x)^{-1} . \]

We immediately check that \(h_{\mathcal{U}}(x)\), thus defined, is a functor \(C^\circ \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\).

Similarly, we define, using isomorphisms \(\varphi\), for any morphism \(y : x \to x'\) a morphism of functors: \[ h_{\mathcal{U}}(y) : h_{\mathcal{U}}(x) \to h_{\mathcal{U}}(x') \] and we immediately check that we have thus defined a functor \[ h_{\mathcal{U}} : C \to \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} . \]

Now let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\). We then have a functor fully faithful canon: \[ \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{V}\text{-Ens} \] hence a fully faithful functor: \[ \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} . \]

and the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[rr]^-{h_{\mathcal{U}}} \ar[dr]_-{h_{\mathcal{V}}} & & \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[d] \\ & & \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} } \] is commutative up to canonical isomorphism. When \(C\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), this Canonical isomorphism is the identity.

In practice, the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) is fixed once and for all and is not mentioned. We then use the notations \(\widehat{C}\) (for the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-sheaves sets) and \[ h : C \to \widehat{C} . \] For any object \(x\) of \(C\), the pre-bundle \(h(x)\) is called the prebundle represented by \(x\) and we will always identify the value in \(y \in \mathrm{ob}(C)\) of the pre-bundle \(h(x)\) with \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(y,x)\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(F\) be a pre-bundle on \(C\) and \(X\) be an object from \(C\). There is a functorial isomorphism in \(X\) and in \(F\): \[ i : \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(h(X),F) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } F(X) , \] the application \(i\) being none other, when \(F\) is of the form \(h(Y)\), than the application: \[ h : \mathrm{Hom}(h(X),h(Y)) \longleftarrow \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y) . \] In particular the functor \(h\) is fully faithful.

This proposition justifies the usual abuse of language identifying an object of \(C\) and the corresponding contravariant functor. A pre-beam isomorphic to an image object by \(h\) (or, using the misnomer pointed out above, isomorphic to an object of \(C\)) is called representable prebundle.

Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing a universe \(\mathcal{U}\). The category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-bundles is a full subcategory of the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundles and therefore a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-beam is representable if and only if its image in the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-representable pre-sheaves.

footnotes.html: (∗) \(C^\circ\) will always designate the category opposite to \(C\).

blocks.html: 2. Projective and inductive limits

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \(I\) be a small category. Let us note \(\mathrm{Fonct}(I,C)\) the category of functors from \(I\) in \(C\). Category \(\mathrm{Fonct}(I,C)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. To any object \(X\) of \(C\) we associate the subcategory \(\underline{X}\) of \(C\) having as sole object the object \(X\) and the only arrow is the identity of \(X\). Let us denote by \(i_X : \underline{X} \to C\) the functor of inclusion. There exists one and only one functor \(e_X : I \to \underline{X}\), and we will denote by \(k_X : I \to C\) the functor \(i_X \circ e_X\). (We will say that \(k_X\) is the constant functor of value \(X\)). The correspondence \(X \mapsto k_X\) is visibly functorial in \(X\), which allows us to define, for any functor \(G : I \to C\), the presheaf \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Fonct}(I,C)}(k_X, G). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1

We call projective limit of \(G\) and we note \(\varprojlim_I G\) the pre-beam: \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Fonct}(I,C)}(k_X, G) . \] When the pre-bundle \(\varprojlim_I G\) is representable, we still designate by \(\varprojlim_I G\) an object from \(C\) that represents it. The object \(\varprojlim_I G\) is therefore only defined with isomorphism close. When no confusion results, the abbreviated notation \(\varprojlim G\) is used.
For \(G\) variable, \(\varprojlim G\) is a functor of the category \(\mathrm{Fonct}(I,C)\) with values in \(\widehat{C}\).

We define the same by symmetry (reversal of the direction of the arrows in \(C\)) the inductive limit of a functor: it is a functor covariant on \(C\) with value in the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets. We will use the notations \(\varinjlim_I G\) or \(\varinjlim G\).
Note that products, fiber products, nuclei are projective limits. From even sums, amalgamated sums, cores are inductive limits.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2

Let \(I\) and \(C\) be two categories and \(G : I \to C\) be a functor. It is said that the projective limit of \(G\) is representable if there exists a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that:

1) The category \(I\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.

2) The category \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

3) The pre-sheap \(\varprojlim G\) with values in the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets is representable.

It follows from No. 1 that the object \(\varprojlim G\) representing the pre-beam \(\varprojlim G\) does not does not depend, up to isomorphism, on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\). Note also that there exists a smaller universe \(\mathcal{U}'\) having properties (1) and (2), and that the pre-beam \(\varprojlim G\) is necessarily with values in the category of \(\mathcal{U}'\)-sets.

Let \(I\) and \(C\) be two categories. We say that the\(I\)-projective limits in \(C\) are representable if for any functor \(G : I \to C\), the projective limit of \(G\) is representable. Finally, let \(C\) be a category and \(\mathcal{U}\) a universe. It is said that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits in \(C\) are representable if for any category \(I\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and for any functor \(G : I \to C\), the projective limit of \(G\) is representable.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3

Let \(C\) be a category and \(\mathcal{U}\) be a non-empty universe. The assertions following are equivalent:

i) The \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits in \(C\) are representable.

ii) The products, indexed by a small set, are representable, and the kernels of pairs of arrows are representable.

iii) The products, indexed by a small set, are representable, and the products fibers are representable.

Proof. It is enough to notice that there is a functorial isomorphism in \(G\) \[ \varprojlim G \simeq \mathrm{Ker}\left( \prod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} G(i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{u\in\mathrm{Fl}(I)} G(\mathrm{but}(u))\right), \] the pair of arrows being defined by the morphisms \[ \prod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} G(i) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{pr}_{\mathrm{but}(u)}\ } G(\mathrm{but}(u)) \] \[ \prod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} G(i) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{pr}_{\mathrm{source}(u)}\ } G(\mathrm{source}(u)) \xrightarrow{\ G(u)\ } G(\mathrm{but}(u)) . \] Furthermore, it is clear that \(\mathrm{Ker}\bigl(X \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} Y\bigr) = X \underset{X\times Y}{\times} X\), both morphisms of \(X\) into \(X\times Y\) being \(\mathrm{id}_X\times u\) and \(\mathrm{id}_X\times v\).

There are obviously analogous definitions and assertions for limits inductive which we will not explain. Likewise for projective limits and finite inductive (i.e. relating to a finite \(I\) category).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.2

Let us designate by \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets and by \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ab the category of objects abelian groups of \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens. The \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective limits and inductive in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens and in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ab are representable.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3.3

Let \(I\) be a small category, \(F : I \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) be a functor and \(G \subset \mathrm{ob}\, I\) a small set of objects of \(I\) such that for every object \(X\) of \(I\) there exists an object \(Y \in G\) and a morphism \(Y \to X\) (resp. \(X \to Y\)). Then \(\varprojlim_I F\) (resp. \(\varinjlim_I F\)) is representable and we have \(\mathrm{card}(\varprojlim_I F) \leq \prod_{Y\in G} \mathrm{card}(F(Y))\) (resp. \(\mathrm{card}(\varinjlim_I F) \leq \sum_{Y\in G} \mathrm{card}(F(Y))\)).

Proof. We immediately check that \(\varprojlim_I F\) (resp. \(\varinjlim_I F\)) is isomorphic to a sub-object (resp. a quotient of \(\prod_{Y\in G} F(Y)\) (resp. \(\coprod_{Y\in G} F(Y)\))); hence the proposal.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4.1

Let \(C\) be a category where the projective limits (resp. inductive) finite are representable and \(u : C \to C'\) a functor. We say that \(u\) is exact left (resp. right) if it “switches” to projective limits (resp. inductive) finite. An exact left and right functor is called an exact functor.

It follows from 2.3 that, for a functor to be exact, it is necessary and sufficient that it transforms the final object (= empty product) into the final object, the product of two objects into it product of the two image objects, and the nucleus of the pairs of two arrows in the nucleus of pairs images or even, that it transforms the final object into the final object and the products fibers into fiber products. (We assume that in \(C\) the finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable.)

Let \(I, J\) and \(C\) be three categories, \(G : I \times J \to C\) a functor (i.e. a functor of \(I\) with value in the category of functors of \(J\) in \(C\)). Suppose that the projective limits of functors \[ G_i : J \to C \qquad i \in \mathrm{ob}(I) \] \[ j \mapsto G(i \times j) \] are representable, and that the functor: \[ i \mapsto \varprojlim G_i \] admits a representable projective limit. It is clear that then the functor \(G\) admits a representable projective limit and we have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \varprojlim_{I\times J} G \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_I \varprojlim_J G_i, \] and that as a result we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \varprojlim_I \varprojlim_J G_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_J \varprojlim_I G_j . \] We will subsequently say more briefly that the projective limits commute to the limits projectives. We also see that the inductive limits commute to the inductive limits. But it is not generally true that inductive limits commute to limits projectives.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.5

Let \(C\) be a category with representable fiber products, \(I\) be a category, \(G : I \to C\) a functor, \(g : G \to X\) a morphism of \(G\) into an object of \(C\) (i.e. a morphism of \(G\) in the constant functor associated with \(X\)), \(m : Y \to X\) a morphism of \(C\). Let \(G \times_X Y : I \to C\) be the functor \[ i \mapsto G(i) \times_X Y . \] We say that the inductive limit of \(G\) is universal if for every object \(X\), every morphism \(g : G \to X\), any morphism \(m : Y \to X\),

a) the inductive limit of the functor \(G \times_X Y\) is representable,

b) the canonical morphism \(\varinjlim(G \times_X Y) \to (\varinjlim G) \times_X Y\) is a isomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.6

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. The inductive limits in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens which are representable (in particular, the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits (2.2.1) in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens) are universal.
We will also use another result of switching between projective limits and inductive that we will present now.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.7

A category \(I\) is pseudo-filtering when it has the properties following:

PS 1) Any diagram of the form: \[ \xymatrix{ & j \\ i \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & \\ & j' } \] can be inserted into a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & j \ar[dr] & \\ i \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & & k \\ & j' \ar[ur] & } \]

PS 2) Any diagram of the form: \[ \xymatrix{ i \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v} & j } \] can be inserted into a diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ i \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{u} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{v} & j \ar[r]^-{w} & k } \] such as \[ w \circ u = w \circ v . \]

A category \(I\) is said to be filtering if it is pseudo-filtering, non-empty and connected, i.e. if any two objects of \(I\) can be connected by a series of arrows (we imposes no conditions on the direction of the arrows); this also means, in the presence of PS 2, that \(I \neq \varnothing\) and that for two objects \(a, b\) of \(I\), there always exists an object \(c\) of \(I\) and the arrows \(a \to c\) and \(b \to c\). We also say that a category \(I\) is cofilter if \(I^\circ\) is filtering.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.7.1

If in \(I\) the amalgamated sums (resp. the sums of two objects) and the cokernels of double arrows are representable, then \(I\) is pseudo-filtering (resp. filtering).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.8

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. The \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering inductive limits in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens commute at finite projective limits.

We immediately return to demonstrating that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering limits commute to fiber products. The demonstration is left to the reader. We can use the description of the limit given by the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.8.1

Let \(I\) be a small filtering category, \(i \mapsto X_i\) a functor of \(I\) in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens. On the sum set \(\coprod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} X_i\), i.e. \(R\) the relation:

(R) Two elements \(x_i \in X_i\) and \(x_j \in X_j\) are connected if there is an object \(k \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) and two morphisms \(u : i \to k\) and \(v : j \to k\) such that the images in \(X_k\) of \(x_i\) and \(x_j\) by the transition applications \(u\) and \(v\) respectively are equal.

So:

1) \(R\) is an equivalence relation.

2) The quotient \(\coprod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} X_i / R\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\varinjlim_I X_i\).

3) Two elements \(x_i \in X_i\) and \(x_j \in X_j\) are respectively equivalent according to \(R\) to two elements of the same \(X_k\).

4) For all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), two elements \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) of \(X_i\) are equivalent following \(R\) if and only if there exists a morphism \(u : i \to j\) such that \(u(\alpha) = u(\beta)\).

Assertion 1) results from (PS 1) (2.7). To prove 2), we verify that \(\coprod_{i\in\mathrm{ob}(I)} X_i / R\) has the universal property of the inductive limit (we only uses (PS 1)). Assertion 3) results from the fact that \(I\) is connected. The assertion 4) results from (PS 2).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.9

Let \(\gamma\) be a kind of algebraic structure “defined by limits finite projectives”. (The reader is asked to give a mathematical meaning to the sentence previous. Let us only note that the structures of groups, abelian groups, rings, modules, etc... are such structures). Let us designate by \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) the category of \(\gamma\)-objects from \(\mathcal{U}\)-Set. The functor which to each object of \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) associates the set underlying, switches to \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering limits. As a result the \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering limits in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) commute at finite projective limits.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10

\(\mathcal{U}\)-pseudo-filtering boundaries in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ab commute to boundaries finite projectives.
(We return to the filtering limits by breaking down the category of indices into related components).

Let's now note a result that we will use constantly: Let \(C\) and \(C'\) two \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, \(u\) and \(v\) two functors \(C \underset{u}{\overset{v}{\rightleftarrows}} C'\), \(u\) left adjoint of \(v\). Recall that this means that there is an isomorphism between the bifunctors with a value in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{C'}(u(X),X') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_C(X,v(X')) . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.11

The functor \(u\) commutes at representable inductive limits; the functor \(v\) commutes at representable projective limits.

This assertion means that for any category \(I\) and any functor \(G : I \to C'\) such that the projective limit of \(G\) is representable, the functor \(v \circ G\) admits a limit representable projective and that we have a canonical isomorphism: \[ v(\varprojlim G) \longrightarrow \varprojlim(v \circ G) . \] The reader will explain the assertion concerning the functor \(u\).
Let us finally point out a calculation of projective limits in the case where the category of indices admits products.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.12

Let \(I\) and \(C\) be two categories, \(\mathcal{V}\) a universe. It is assumed that the \(\mathcal{V}\)-projective limits in \(C\) are representable and that there exists in \(I\) a family of objects \((i_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A}\), where \(A\) is an element of \(\mathcal{V}\), such that:

1) the products \(i_\alpha \times i_\beta\) are representable for any couple \((\alpha,\beta) \in A\times A\),

2) any object from \(I\) is sent to at least one of \(i_\alpha\).

Then for any contra-functor \(F\) of \(I\) in \(C\) \[ F : I^\circ \to C, \] the projective limit of \(F\) exists and there is a functorial isomorphism in \(F\): \[ \varprojlim F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ker}\left( \prod_{\alpha\in A} F(i_\alpha) \rightrightarrows \prod_{(\alpha,\beta)\in A\times A} F(i_\alpha \times i_\beta)\right), \] the two arrows being defined by the projections of the products \(i_\alpha \times i_\beta\) on the factors.

pages.title: 3. Accuracy properties of the pre-bundle category

blocks.html: 3. Accuracy properties of the pre-bundle category
Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\widehat{C}\) the category of pre-bundles on \(C\). Properties accuracy of \(\widehat{C}\) are all deduced from the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1

The \(\mathcal{U}\)-projective and inductive limits in \(\widehat{C}\) are representable. For any object \(X\) of \(C\), the functor on \(\widehat{C}\): \[ F \mapsto F(X) \qquad F \in \widehat{C} \] commutes at inductive and projective limits.
In other words, “the inductive and projective limits in \(\widehat{C}\) are calculated argument by argument.” Let us cite some corollaries.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let \(\gamma\) be an algebraic structure defined by projective limits finished. The category of contravariant functors with values in \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\) is equivalent to the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\widehat{C}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

A morphism of \(\widehat{C}\) which is both a monomorphism and a epimorphism is an isomorphism. A morphism of \(\widehat{C}\) factors uniquely into an epimorphism followed by a monomorphism. The inductive limits in \(\widehat{C}\) which are representable, are universal. The \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering inductive limits in \(\widehat{C}\) switch at finite projective limits. The canonical functor \(C \to \widehat{C}\) commutes at limits representable projectives.
More generally we can say that the category \(\widehat{C}\) inherits all the properties of the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets involving inductive and projective limits.

Let \(F\) be an object of \(\widehat{C}\). We designate by \(C/F\) the following category: The objects of \(C/F\) are the pairs formed of an object \(X\) of \(C\) and a morphism \(u\) of \(X\) in \(F\). Let \((X,u)\) and \((Y,v)\) be two objects. A morphism of \((X,u)\) into \((Y,v)\) is a morphism \(g\) from \(X\) into \(Y\) such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[rr]^-{g} \ar[dr]_-{u} & & Y \ar[dl]^-{v} \\ & F & } \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.4

With the notations of (3.4.0), the source functor \(C/F \to \widehat{C}\) admits an inductive limit representable in \(\widehat{C}\). The canonical morphism: \[ \varinjlim_{C/F} \mathrm{source}(.) \to F \] is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \(F\) and \(H\) be two objects of \(\widehat{C}\). There is an isomorphism canonical: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(F,H) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{(X,u)\in\mathrm{ob}(C/F)} H(X) . \]
Proof. The corollary is immediately deduced from 3.4 and 1.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.6

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(i : \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the natural injection functor of prebundle categories corresponding (1.3). The functor \(i\) commutes at inductive and projective limits. From more, for any object \(F\) of \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), any sub-object of \(i(F)\) is isomorphic to the image by \(i\) of a unique sub-object of \(F\) (so that we obtain a bijection of the set of sub-objects of \(F\) with all the sub-objects of \(i(F)\)).
4. Screens

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1

Let \(C\) be a category. We call screen of category \(C\) a full subcategory \(D\) of \(C\) having the following property: any object of \(C\) as it exists a morphism of this object in an object of \(D\) is in \(D\). Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\); we call (by misnomer) screens of \(X\) the screens of the category \(C/X\).

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe such that \(C\) or a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. Let \(\widehat{C}\) be the category of corresponding pre-beams. With any sieve of \(X\) we associate a sub-object of \(X\) in \(\widehat{C}\) in the following way: To any object \(Y\) of \(C\), we match the set of morphisms \(f : Y \to X\) such that the object \((Y,f)\) belongs to the sieve.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2

The application defined above establishes a bijection between the set sifters of \(X\) and the set of sub-objects of \(X\) in \(\widehat{C}\).

Proof. Let us only show that it is the inverse application. To any subfunctor \(R\) of \(X\) we associate the category \(C/R\) of the objects of \(C\) above \(R\) (3.4). We check immediately that \(C/R\) is a sieve of \(X\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.2.1

We also see that the screens of \(C\) are in correspondence canonical one-to-one with the set of sub-functors of the “final functor” on \(C\) (“final” object of \(\widehat{C}\)).

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. By abuse of language we will also call screens of \(X\), the sub-objects of \(X\) in the category \(\widehat{C}\). This abuse of language allows us to any pre-beam \(F\) and any screen \(R\) of \(X\) to define \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F)\) as the set of morphisms of the functor \(R\) in \(F\). We also have an isomorphism canonical functorial in \(F\) (3.5): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(R,F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C/R} F(.) , \] which allows us to give a direct definition. Likewise, proposition 4.2 allows us to transpose to sieves the usual operations on functors. Let's quote:
4.3.1. Base change.

Let \(R\) be a sieve of \(X\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be an object morphism of \(C\). The fiber product \(R \times_X Y\) is a sieve of \(Y\) which is called screen deduced from \(R\) by base change. The corresponding subcategory of \(C/Y\) is the inverse image of the subcategory of \(C/X\) defined by \(R\) by the canonical functor \(C/Y \to C/X\) defined by \(f\).
4.3.2. Order relationship, intersection, meeting.

The inclusion relation on the sub-functors of \(X\) is an order relation. We can define the meeting and the intersection of a family of indexed sieves by a any set as being the upper limit and the lower limit of the family of corresponding sub-prebundles.
4.3.3. Image, generated screen.

Let \((F_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A}\) be a family of pre-sheaves and for each \(\alpha \in A\), a morphism \(f_\alpha : F_\alpha \to X\) where \(X\) is an object of \(C\). We call image of this family of morphisms the union of images of \(f_\alpha\). The image of this family is therefore a sieve by \(X\). In particular if all \(F_\alpha\) are objects of \(C\), the image sieve will be called the screen generated by morphisms \(f_\alpha\). Category \(C/R\) is the full subcategory of \(C/X\) formed from the objects \(X' \to X\) above \(X\) such that there exists a \(X\)-morphism of \(X'\) in one of \(F_\alpha\).

The reader can, as an exercise, translate the categories \(C/R\) into terms relations and operations defined here on the sub-functors. He will then notice that these relations and operations do not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and that they are therefore defined for any category, without one being obliged to specify the universe to which the sets of morphisms belong; what we could moreover plan a priori thanks to 3.6.
5. Functionality of pre-bundle categories

Let \(C, C', D\) be three categories and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor. We will designate by \(u^*\) the functor: \[ u^* : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C'^\circ,D) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,D), \qquad G \mapsto G \circ u, \] obtained by composing with the functor \(u\). The functor \(u^*\) commutes at inductive limits and projectives.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.1

Suppose that \(C\) is small, and that, in \(D\), the \(\mathcal{U}\)-boundaries inductive (resp. projective) are representable. The functor \(u^*\) admits an adjoint to left \(u_!\) (resp. right \(u_*\)). We therefore have an isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,D)}(F, u^*G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C'^\circ,D)}(u_! F, G) \] \[ (\text{resp. } \mathrm{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,D)}(u^*G, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C'^\circ,D)}(G, u_* F) ) . \]

Proof. We will only indicate the demonstration of the existence of the adjoint functor to left. The part resp. of the proposition will then be deduced formally thanks to the isomorphisms: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,D) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,D^\circ)^\circ \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}((C^\circ)^\circ, D^\circ)^\circ . \]

Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C'\). Let us designate by \(I_u^Y\) the following category: The objects of \(I_u^Y\) are the pairs \((X,m)\) where \(X\) is an object of \(C\) and \(m\) a morphism \(Y \to u(X)\). Let \((X,m)\) and \((X',m')\) be two objects of \(I_u^Y\). A morphism of \((X,m)\) into \((X',m')\) is a morphism \(\xi : X \to X'\) such that \(m' = u(\xi)m\). The composition of morphisms is defined in the obvious way.
Let \(f : Y \to Y'\) be a morphism of \(C'\). The morphism \(f\) defines by composition a functor \(I_u^f : I_u^Y \to I_u^{Y'}\).

We also have a functor \(\mathrm{pr}_Y : I_u^Y \to C\) which, to the object \((X,m)\) associates the object \(X\). Note that then the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ I_u^Y \ar[rr]^-{I_u^f} \ar[dr]_-{\mathrm{pr}_Y} & & I_u^{Y'} \ar[dl]^-{\mathrm{pr}_{Y'}} \\ & C & } \tag{\(*\)} \] is commutative.

Now let \(F\) be a pre-bundle on \(C\) and let's set: \[ u_! F(Y) = \varinjlim_{I_u^Y} F\, \mathrm{pr}_Y(.) . \tag{5.1.1} \] The commutativity of the diagram (\(*\)) and the functoriality of the inductive limit make \(u_! F\) a pre-beam on \(C'\). Let us show that the functor \(u_!\) is a left adjoint of the functor \(u^*\). To do this, let us show that for any pre-bundle \(G\) on \(C'\), there exists an isomorphism functorial \(\mathrm{Hom}(u_! F,G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(F,u^*G)\). Or \(\xi \in \mathrm{Hom}(u_! F,G)\). For any object \(X\) of \(C\), we therefore have a morphism: \[ \xi_X : u_! F(u(X)) \to G(u(X)) . \]

But \((u(X), \mathrm{id}_{u(X)})\) is an object of \(I_u^{u(X)}\), and by definition of the limit inductive, we have a canonical morphism: \[ F(X) \to u_! F(u(X)). \] We deduce for any object \(X\) from \(C\) a morphism: \[ \eta_X : F(X) \to G(u(X)) \] which is visibly functional in \(X\). Hence a morphism \[ \eta : F \to u^*G . \]

Conversely, i.e. \(\eta \in \mathrm{Hom}(F,u^*G)\). We deduce, for any object \(Y\) from \(C'\), a functor morphism: \[ \eta_Y : F\,\mathrm{pr}_Y \to (u^*G)\,\mathrm{pr}_Y , \] hence, by composing with the obvious morphism of the functor \((u^*G)\,\mathrm{pr}_Y\) into the functor constant \(G(Y)\), a morphism: \[ \xi_Y : u_! F(Y) \to G(Y) \] which is functorial in \(Y\). Hence a morphism: \[ \xi : u_! F \to G . \]
The reader will verify that the two applications thus defined are inverses of one of the other, and will thus complete the demonstration.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.2

Suppose that in \(D\), the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits are representable, the finite projective limits are representable and the \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits filtering inductives commute at finite projective limits. Let us further assume that in \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable and the functor \(u\) is exact at left (2.3.2). Then the finite projective limits are representable in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ,D)\) and in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C'^\circ,D)\), and the functor \(u_!\) is left exact.

Proof. The first assertion is trivial. Let us demonstrate the second. According to the demonstration of 5.1, for any prebeam \(F\) on \(C\) and any object \(Y\) of \(C'\) we have: \[ u_! F(Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{I_u^Y} F\,\mathrm{pr}_Y . \] It is therefore sufficient to show that the category \((I_u^Y)^\circ\) verifies the axioms (PS 1) and (PS 2) (2.7) and that this category is connected. Verification is left to the reader.

By particularizing these results to the case where \(D\) is the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets, we obtain a sequence of three functors: \[ u_! , \quad u^* , \quad u_* , \] which is a “sequence of adjoint functors” in the sense that for two consecutive functors thereafter the one on the right is added to the right of the other.
Their essential properties are summarized in:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.4

Let \(C\) be a small category, \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor.

1) The functor \(u^* : \widehat{C'} \to \widehat{C}\) commutes at inductive and projective limits.

2) The functor \(u_* : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'}\) commutes at projective limits. For everything pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\) and any object \(Y\) of \(C'\), we have \[ u_* F(Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(u^*(Y),F) . \]

3) The functor \(u_! : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'}\) commutes at inductive limits. The functor \(u_!\) is only defined up to isomorphism, but we can always choose it such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{h} & & C' \ar[d]^-{h'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]_-{u_!} & & \widehat{C'} } \] (\(h\) and \(h'\) are the canonical inclusion functors) or commutative. For any pre-bundle \(F\) on \(C\), we have: \[ u_! F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C/F} h'u . \]

4) If finite projective limits are representable in \(C\) and if \(u\) is exact at left (2.3.2), the functor \(u_!\) is left exact.

Proof. Assertion (1) is trivial. The assertion (2) is deduced from the fact that \(u_*\) is a right-adjoint functor by (2.11) and (1.4). The same is true for assertion (3) but we additionally apply (3.4). Finally, assertion (4) is none other than 5.2 which can be applied thanks to (2.7).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two small categories and \(C \underset{u}{\overset{v}{\rightleftarrows}} C'\) be a couple of functors, where \(v\) is adjoint to the left of \(u\). There then exist isomorphisms, compatible with addition isomorphisms: \[ v^* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u_! \] \[ v_* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u^* . \]

Proof. It is enough to exhibit an isomorphism \(v^* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u_!\); the other isomorphism will be deduced by addition. Let \(F\) be a pre-beam on \(C\) and \(Y\) be an object of \(C'\). We have then: \[ v^* F(Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(v(Y),F) . \] Then using (3.4): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(v(Y),F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C/F} \mathrm{Hom}(v(Y),.) . \] But \(v\) is adjoint to the left of \(u\) and therefore: \[ \varprojlim_{C/F} \mathrm{Hom}(v(Y),.) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C/F} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,u(\,.\,)) . \] Then using 5.4 3), it comes: \[ \varprojlim_{C/F} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,u(\,.\,)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(Y,u_! F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u_! F(Y) . \] We have therefore determined, for any object \(Y\) of \(C'\), an isomorphism \(v^* F(Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u_! F(Y)\) which is visibly functional in \(Y\) and in \(F\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5.1

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor which admits a left adjoint. The functor \(u_! : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'}\) commutes to projective limits (recall that it commutes to inductive limits by (1.4, 3)).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.5.2

We thus find a “sequence of four adjoint functors” (see 5.3): \[ v_! , \quad v^* = u_! , \quad v_* = u^* , \quad u_* , \] of which the first three (resp. last) therefore commute to \(\varinjlim\) (resp. \(\varprojlim\)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.6

The hypotheses are those of 5.4. The following conditions are equivalents:

i) The functor \(u\) is fully faithful.

ii) The functor \(u_!\) is fully faithful.

iii) The addition morphism \(\mathrm{id}_{\widehat{C}} \to u^*u_!\) is an isomorphism.

iv) The functor \(u_*\) is fully faithful.

v) The addition morphism \(u^*u_* \to \mathrm{id}_{\widehat{C}}\) is an isomorphism.

Proof. It is clear that ii) ⟺ iii) and iv) ⟺ v) (general properties of functors deputies) and that (5.4 3)). Let us show that i) ⟹ iii). The functors \(\mathrm{id}_{\widehat{C}}\), \(u^*\) and \(u_!\) commute at inductive limits. According to (3.4), it is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that \(H \to u^*u_! H\) is an isomorphism when \(H\) is representable which is obvious. Let us show that iii) is equivalent to v). For any object \(H\) (resp. \(K\)) of \(\widehat{C}\) let us designate by \(\Phi(H) : H \to u^*u_! H\) (resp. by \(\Psi(K) : u^*u_* \to K\)) the addition morphism. We have then a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(H,u^*u_*K) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{Hom}(H,\Psi(K))} \ar@{-}[d]|-{\wr} & & \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(H,K) \\ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C'}}(u_!H,u_*K) \ar@{-}[d]|-{\wr} & & \\ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(u^*u_!H,K) \ar[uurr]_-{\mathrm{Hom}(\Phi(H),K)} & & } \] Consequently \(\Phi(H)\) is an isomorphism for all \(H\) if and only if \(\Psi(K)\) is a isomorphism for all \(K\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.7

a) The equivalences ii) ⟺ iii) ⟺ iv) ⟺ v) are general results on the adjoint functors.

b) The explicit form of \(u_!\), resp. \(u_*\) given in the 1.4 demo shows as soon as, under the general assumptions of 1.1, if \(u\) is fully faithful, then the adjunction morphism \(\mathrm{id} \to u^*u_!\) (resp. \(u^*u_* \to \mathrm{id}\)) is an isomorphism, i.e. that \(u_!\) (resp. \(u_*\)) is fully faithful.

Let \(\gamma\) be a kind of algebraic structure defined by projective limits finite, \(\mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens}\) the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens, \(\mathrm{esj} : \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens} \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) the underlying set functor (for simplicity we assume that the kind of structure considered has a single basic set). Let \(C\) be a category. Composition with \(\mathrm{esj}\) provides a noted functor \[ \widehat{\mathrm{esj}} : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens}) \to \widehat{C}. \] As in \(\widehat{C}\), the projective limits are calculated argument by argument, the functor \(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}\) factors into an equivalence. \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens}) \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } \widehat{C}_\gamma , \tag{5.8.1} \] where \(\widehat{C}_\gamma\) is the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\widehat{C}\), and a functor further denoted \[ \widehat{\mathrm{esj}} : \widehat{C}_\gamma \to \widehat{C}, \] and called the functor “underlying set pre-sheaf”.

Suppose that the functor \(\mathrm{esj} : \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens} \to \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) admits an adjoint to left \(\mathrm{Lib} : \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens} \to \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens}\)∗ (we can actually show that this condition is always satisfied). Composition with \(\mathrm{Lib}\) provides a functor \[ \widehat{\mathrm{Lib}} : \widehat{C} \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-}\gamma\text{-Ens}) \] and by composing with the equivalence (5.8.1), a functor still denoted \[ \widehat{\mathrm{Lib}} : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}_\gamma , \] and called the functor “pre-sheap of \(\gamma\)-generated free objects”. The functor \(\widehat{\mathrm{Lib}}\) is left adjoint to the functor \(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.8.3

Let \(\gamma\) be a kind of algebraic structure defined by limits finite projectives such that in the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens, the \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits inductive are representable∗∗, \(C\) a category belonging to \(\mathcal{U}\), \(C'\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(u : C \to C'\) a functor. Let us denote by \(\widehat{C}_\gamma\) (resp. \(\widehat{C'}_\gamma\)) the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\widehat{C}\) (resp. \(\widehat{C'}\)) and by \(u^{*\gamma}\) the functor on the \(\gamma\)-objects deduced from the functor \(u^*\). It follows from 5.1 and the equivalence 5.8.1 that there exists a functor left (resp. right) adjoint to the functor \(u^{*\gamma}\). This functor is denoted \(u_{!\gamma}\) (resp. \(u_{*\gamma}\)).

1) The functor \(u^{*\gamma}\) commutes at inductive and projective limits. The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C'}_\gamma \ar[rr]^-{u^{*\gamma}} \ar[d]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}'}} & & \widehat{C}_\gamma \ar[d]^-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}} \\ \widehat{C'} \ar[rr]_-{u^*} & & \widehat{C} } \tag{\(*\)} \] is commutative (\(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}'}\) and \(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}\) denote the “underlying set” functors).

2) The functor \(u_{*\gamma}\) commutes at projective limits. The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C}_\gamma \ar[rr]^-{u_{*\gamma}} \ar[d]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}} & & \widehat{C'}_\gamma \ar[d]^-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}'}} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]_-{u_*} & & \widehat{C'} } \tag{\(**\)} \] is commutative up to isomorphism.

3) The functor \(u_{!\gamma}\) commutes at inductive limits. Suppose that \(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}\) (resp. \(\widehat{\mathrm{esj}'}\)) admits a deputy on the left \(\widehat{\mathrm{Lib}}\) (resp. \(\widehat{\mathrm{Lib}'}\)) (5.8.2). The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]^-{u_!} \ar[d]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{Lib}}} & & \widehat{C'} \ar[d]^-{\widehat{\mathrm{Lib}'}} \\ \widehat{C}_\gamma \ar[rr]_-{u_{!\gamma}} & & \widehat{C'}_\gamma } \tag{\(***\)} \] is commutative up to isomorphism.

Suppose that \(u_!\) commutes at finite projective limits (5.4 4) and 5.6). So the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C}_\gamma \ar[rr]^-{u_{!\gamma}} \ar[d]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}}} & & \widehat{C'}_\gamma \ar[d]^-{\widehat{\mathrm{esj}'}} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]_-{u_!} & & \widehat{C'} } \tag{\(****\)} \] is commutative up to isomorphism, and \(u_{!\gamma}\) commutes at finite projective limits.

Proof. Assertion (1) is obvious. Assertion (2) also because \(u_*\) is an adjoint to right and consequently (2.11) commutes to projective limits and, in particular, to limits finite projectives; hence the commutativity of the diagram (\(**\)). The commutativity of diagram (\(***\)) is deduced from the uniqueness, with isomorphism near the adjoint functor on the left, and finally the commutativity of the diagram (\(****\)) is immediately deduced from the fact that \(u_!\) commutes at finite projective limits.

blocks.title: Rating
blocks.html:
Rating 5.9

By notation abuse, the functors \(u^{*\gamma}\) and \(u_{*\gamma}\) will often be denoted \(u^*\) and \(u_*\), which is not likely to cause confusion under the commutativity of diagrams (\(*\)) and (\(**\)). On the other hand, when \(u_!\) does not switch to finite projective limits, the diagram (\(****\)) is not commutative up to isomorphism, and the notations \(u_!\) and \(u_{!\gamma}\) should be used to avoid confusion.

Let \(C\) be a small category. For any object \(X\) of \(\widehat{C}\), we denote by \(C/X\) the category of arrows of goal \(X\) and source an object of \(C\). The source functor defines a functor \(j_X : C/X \to C\). Let \(m : Y \to X\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). The composition of morphisms defines a functor \(j_m : C/Y \to C/X\). The diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ C/Y \ar[rr]^-{j_m} \ar[dr]_-{j_Y} & & C/X \ar[dl]^-{j_X} \\ & C & } \] is commutative. It follows from 5.1 that for any object \(F\) of \(\widehat{C/X}\) and any object \(Y\) of \(C\), we have: \[ j_{X!} F(Y) = \coprod_{u\in\mathrm{Hom}_C(Y,X)} F(u) . \tag{5.10.1} \]

The formula (5.10.1) allows us to define \(j_{X!}\) when \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and we checks that the functor \(j_{X!}\) thus defined is always appended to the left of the functor \(j_X^* : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C/X}\) (5.0).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.11

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(X\) a pre-bundle on \(C\).

1) The functor \[ j_{X!} : \widehat{C/X} \to \widehat{C} \] is factorized by the category \(\widehat{C}/X\): \[ \widehat{C/X} \xrightarrow{\ e_X\ } \widehat{C}/X \to \widehat{C} . \] The functor \(e_X\) is an equivalence of categories.

2) The functor \(e_X \circ j_X^* : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}/X\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor \(H \mapsto (H\times X \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_2} X)\).

Proof. 1) Let \(f\) be the final object of \(\widehat{C/X}\). We have a canonical isomorphism \(f \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{Y\in\mathrm{ob}\,C/X} Y\) and subsequently \(j_{X!}(f) = \varinjlim_{Y\in\mathrm{ob}\,C/X} j_X(Y)\) (5.4). Gold \(j_{X!}(f) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X\); hence the factorization. To show that \(e_X\) is an equivalence we we will content ourselves with exhibiting a quasi-inverse functor: To any object \(H \to X\) of \(\widehat{C}/X\) we associate the pre-beam on \(C/X\): \[ (Y \to X) \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}/X}((Y \to X), (H \to X)) . \]

2) The functor \(e_X \circ j_X^*\) is right-adjoined to the forgetting functor and consequently the functor \(e_X \circ j_X^*\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor \(H \mapsto (H\times X \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_2} X)\).

Let \(m : Y \to X\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). According to (5.11), the morphism \(m\) is canonically isomorphic to the image by \(e_X\) of an object of \(\widehat{C/X}\) which we will denote \([m]\). The functor \(e_X\) defines, by restriction to subcategories, an equivalence \[ (C/X)/[m] \xrightarrow{\ e_m\ } C/Y . \] The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ (C/X)/[m] \ar[rr]^-{e_m} \ar[dr]_-{j_{[m]}} & & C/Y \ar[dl]^-{j_m} \\ & C/X & } \] is commutative up to canonical isomorphism.

Let us point out a result which will be useful to us in Exp. VI. Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between small categories. For any object \(H\) of \(\widehat{C}\) let us designate by \(u/H : C/H \to C'/u_! H\) the functor which associates with any morphism \(m : X \to H\) the morphism \(u_! X \xrightarrow{\ u_! m\ } u_! H\) (we know (5.4) that we can always put \(u_! X = uX\)). The diagram below is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ C/H \ar[rr]^-{u/H} \ar[d]_-{j_H} & & C'/u_! H \ar[d]^-{j_{u_! H}} \\ C \ar[rr]_-{u} & & C' } \tag{5.13.1} \] We therefore have a commutative diagram up to isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C/H} \ar[rr]^-{(u/H)_!} \ar[d]_-{(j_H)_!} & & \widehat{C'/u_! H} \ar[d]^-{(j_{u_! H})_!} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]_-{u_!} & & \widehat{C'} } \tag{5.13.2} \] and as \((u/H)_!\) transforms the final object of \(\widehat{C/H}\) into the final object of \(\widehat{C'/u_! H}\), the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C/H} \ar[rr]^-{(u/H)_!} \ar[d]_-{e_H} & & \widehat{C'/u_! H} \ar[d]^-{e_{u_! H}} \\ \widehat{C}/H \ar[rr]_-{u_!/H} & & \widehat{C'}/u_! H } \tag{5.13.3} \] is commutative up to isomorphism (6.1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.14

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between small categories. We assume that \(u\) has the following property:

(PPF) For any object \(X\) of \(C\) the functor \(u/X : C/X \to C'/uX\) is fully faithful.

So:

1) Let \(f\) be the final object of \(\widehat{C}\). The functor \(u\) factors into \[ C = C/f \xrightarrow{\ u/f\ } C'/u_! f \xrightarrow{\ \overline{j}_{u_! f}\ } C' . \] The functor \(u/f\) is fully faithful.

2) The functor \(u_! : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'}\) is factorized into \[ \widehat{C} = \widehat{C/f} \xrightarrow{\ (u/f)_!\ } \widehat{C'/u_! f} \xrightarrow{\ e_{u_! f}\ } \widehat{C'}/u_! f \to \widehat{C'} , \] where the functor \((u/f)_!\) is fully faithful, the functor \(e_{u_! f}\) an equivalence, and the functor \(\widehat{C'}/u_! f \to \widehat{C'}\) the forgetting functor.

3) In particular the functor \(u_!\) is faithful and consequently the addition morphism \(\mathrm{id} \xrightarrow{\ \Phi\ } u^*u_!\) is a monomorphism. Moreover, for any morphism \(\alpha : H \to K\) of \(\widehat{C}\), the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ H \ar@{^{(}->}[rr]^-{\Phi(H)} \ar[d]_-{\alpha} & & u^*u_! H \ar[d]^-{u^*u_!(\alpha)} \\ K \ar@{^{(}->}[rr]_-{\Phi(K)} & & u^*u_! K } \] is Cartesian.

Proof. 1) The factorization comes from diagram (5.13.1). The \(u\) functor is faithful. So \(u/f\) is faithful. Let us show that he is fully faithful. Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two objects of \(C\), \(\mathrm{can}_X : uX \to u_! f\) (resp. \(\mathrm{can}_Y : uY \to u_! f\)) canonical morphisms and \[ \xymatrix{ uX \ar[rr]^-{m} \ar[dr]_-{\mathrm{can}_X} & & uY \ar[dl]^-{\mathrm{can}_Y} \\ & u_! f & } \] a morphism of \(C'/u_! f\). We have \(u_! f = \varinjlim_{Z\in\mathrm{ob}\,C} uZ\) and consequently (3.1): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C'}}(uX, u_! f) = \varinjlim_{Z\in\mathrm{ob}\,C} \mathrm{Hom}_{C'}(uX, uZ) . \] By definition of the inductive limit, saying that \(\mathrm{can}_Y\, m = \mathrm{can}_X\) is equivalent to saying that it exists
a) a finite sequence of objects of \(C\), \(X_i\), \(i \in [0,n]\), \(X_0 = X\), \(X_n = Y\),
b) for all \(i\), a morphism \(m_i : uX \to uX_i\) (\(m_0 = \mathrm{id}_{uX}\), \(m_n = m\)),
c) for any pair \((i,i+1)\) a morphism \(f_i : X_i \to X_{i+1}\) or \(f_i : X_{i+1} \to X_i\),

such as diagrams \[ \xymatrix{ & uX \ar[dl]_-{m_i} \ar[dr]^-{m_{i+1}} & \\ uX_i \ar[rr]_-{u(f_i)} & & uX_{i+1} } \qquad \text{ou bien} \qquad \xymatrix{ & uX \ar[dl]_-{m_i} \ar[dr]^-{m_{i+1}} & \\ uX_i & & uX_{i+1} \ar[ll]^-{u(f_i)} } \] are commutative.
We then demonstrate immediately, by induction on \(i\) and using the property (PPF), that \(m_i\) is of the form \(u(p_i)\). In particular \(m = u(p)\) and consequently \(u/f\) is fully faithful.
2) Factorization is immediate. The functor \((u/f)_!\) is fully faithful by virtue of 5.6. The other assertions result from 5.11.

3) The functor \(u_!\) is composed of the forgetting functor which is faithful, and functors fully faithful. He is therefore faithful. It follows, according to the general properties of adjoint functors, that the addition morphism \(\mathrm{id} \to u^*u_!\) is a monomorphism. According to 2) the functor \(u_!\) appears as the compound of a fully faithful functor \(v : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'}/u_! f\) and the forgetting functor. The functor \(u^*\), adjoint to the right of \(u_!\), is therefore the compound of the functor "produced by \(u_! f\)", adjoint to the right of the forgetting functor, and of a functor \(w\) adjoint to the right of \(v\). Moreover, \(v\) being fully faithful, the morphism addition \(\mathrm{id} \to wv\) is an isomorphism. The last assertion follows easily.

footnotes.html: (∗) The Lib functor is the “\(\gamma\)-free spawned objects” functor. Example: free group, free commutative group, free A-module, etc... .
(∗∗) We can show that this condition is always satisfied.

blocks.html: 6. Faithful functors and conservative functors

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

Let \(E\) be a category, \((\varphi_i : E \to F_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of functors \[ \varphi_i : E \to F_i . \] We say that the family of functors \((\varphi_i)\) is faithful if for any pair of objects \(X, Y\) of \(E\), and any pair of arrows \(u, v : X \rightrightarrows Y\), the relation \(\varphi_i(u) = \varphi_i(v)\) for all \(i \in I\) implies \(u = v\) (in other words, if the application \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y) \to \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi_i(X), \varphi_i(Y))\) defined by \((\varphi_i)\) is injective). We say that the family of functors \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative if every arrow \(u\) of \(E\), such that \(\varphi_i(u)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i \in I\), is an isomorphism. We say that \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative for monomorphisms (resp. for epimorphisms, resp. ....) if the previous condition is verified each time \(u\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. ....).

If we introduce the unique functor \[ \varphi : E \to F = \prod_{i\in I} F_i \] defined by the family of functors \((\varphi_i)\), it is clear that it is faithful (resp. conservative, resp. conservative for monomorphisms, resp. ...) if and only if the functor \(\varphi\) is faithful (resp. conservative, resp. ...) (by which we mean that the family reduced to the single object \(\varphi\) is faithful, resp. conservative, resp. ...). We could therefore without major disadvantage we subsequently limit ourselves to the case of a family reduced to a single functor. For the convenience of future references, we will nevertheless give the statements following for families.
The notions of 6.1 are especially useful when the \(\varphi_i\) satisfy properties of suitable accuracy, and in this case have a tendency to coincide:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2

The notations are those of 6.1.

(i) If the cores of double arrows, or the cocores of double arrows, are representable in \(E\), and if the \(\varphi_i\) commute there, then we have the implication \[ (\varphi_i) \text{ conservative} \implies (\varphi_i) \text{ fidèle} . \]

(ii) Suppose that the fiber products (resp. the amalgamated sums) are representable in \(E\), and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there. Suppose \((\varphi_i)\) faithful or conservative; then for any arrow \(u\) of \(E\), \(u\) is a monomorphism (resp. a epimorphism) if and only if for all \(i \in I\), it is thus for \(\varphi_i(u)\).

(iii) Suppose that in \(E\) the fiber products and the amalgamated sums are representable and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there, and that any arrow in \(E\) which is a bimorphism (i.e. a monomorphism and an epimorphism) or an isomorphism. So we have the involvement \[ (\varphi_i) \text{ fidèle} \implies (\varphi_i) \text{ conservative} . \]

(iv) Suppose that in \(E\) the fibered products (resp. the amalgamated sums) are representable, and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there. Then, if \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative for monomorphisms (resp. for epimorphisms) then \((\varphi_i)\) is even conservative.

(v) Let \(D\) be a diagram type, \(F : d \mapsto F(d)\) be a diagram of type \(D\) in \(E\), \(X\) an object of \(E\) and \(u = (u_d)_{d\in D}\) a family of arrows \[ X \to F(d) \qquad (\text{resp. } F(d) \to X) . \] Suppose that \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative, that the projective (resp. inductive) limits of type \(D\) are representable in \(E\), and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there. So, so that \(u\) makes \(X\) a projective (resp. inductive) limit of \(F\) in \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), \(\varphi_i(u)\) makes \(\varphi_i(X)\) a projective (resp. inductive) limit of \(\varphi_i(F)\) in \(F_i\).

Demonstration. (i) For the statement no resp., it is sufficient, for a given double arrow \(u, v : X \rightrightarrows Y\), to express the equality \(u = v\) by the condition that the inclusion \(\mathrm{Ker}(u,v) \to X\) is an isomorphism. Here and subsequently, we dispense with repeating the dual argument for the dual statement.

(ii) If \((\varphi_i)\) is faithful, we express the condition that \(u : X \to Y\) is a monomorphism by the equality \(\mathrm{pr}_1 = \mathrm{pr}_2\) for the fiber product \(X \times_Y X\). If \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative, we express it by the condition that the diagonal morphism \(\delta : X \to X \times_Y X\) or an isomorphism.

(iv) As in this last argument, we see that it was in fact sufficient to suppose \((\varphi_i)\) conservative for monomorphisms. But this then implies that \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative quite simply. Indeed, if \(u \in \mathrm{fl}\, E\) is such that \(\varphi_i(u)\) are isomorphisms, we concludes that these are monomorphisms from the above, therefore isomorphisms according to the hypothesis on \((\varphi_i)\).
(iii) Is a trivial consequence of (ii).
(v) Is a trivial consequence of the definitions.
Note the following consequence of (i) (ii) (iv):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Suppose that in \(E\) the fiber products and the amalgamated sums are representable and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there, and that the double arrow kernels or the cokernels of double arrows are representable and that the \(\varphi_i\) commute there. (Just for example that finite projective limits and finite inductive limits are representable in \(E\), and that the \(\varphi_i\) are exact functors.) Then the conditions following are equivalent:

a) \((\varphi_i)\) is faithful.

b) \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative.

c) \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative for monomorphisms.

c') \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative for epimorphisms.
Also note for the record:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.4

Let \(\varphi : E \to F\) be a functor admitting a right adjoint \(\psi\) (so \(\mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X),Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(X,\psi(Y))\)). For \(\varphi\) (resp. \(\psi\)) to be faithful, it It is necessary and sufficient that for any element \(X\) of \(E\) (resp. any element \(Y\) of \(F\)), the addition morphism \(X \to \psi\varphi(X)\) is a monomorphism. So that \(\varphi\) (resp. \(\psi\)) is fully faithful, it is necessary and sufficient for the previous addition morphism to be a isomorphism.

Indeed, if \(X', X\) are two objects of \(E\), the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X',X) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X'),\varphi(X)) \tag{\(*\)} \] identifies with the application deduced from \[ X \to \psi\varphi(X) \tag{\(**\)} \] by application of the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(X',-)\). So for \((*)\) to be a monomorphism (resp. a isomorphism) for all \(X'\), \(X\) being fixed, it is necessary and sufficient that the morphism of addition \((**)\) is a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5

Let \(p : E' \to E\) be a functor. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(p\) is faithful, conservative and fibrant (SGA 1 VI 6.1).

(ii) \(p\) is a fiber functor of discrete categories.

(iii) For all \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, E'\), the functor \(E'_{/X'} \to E_{/p(X')}\) induced by \(p\) is an equivalence of categories, surjective on objects.

(iv) (When \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category). There is an element \(F \in \mathrm{ob}\, \widehat{E}\) and a equivalence of categories on \(E\) (SGA 1 VI 4.3) \[ E' \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } E_{/F} \] (where \(E_{/F}\) is the full subcategory of \(\widehat{E}_{/F}\) formed by the arrows \(X \to F\) whose source is in \(E\)).

Recall that a category \(C\) is called discrete if it is a groupoid (i.e. any arrow there is invertible) and if it is rigid (i.e. the group of automorphisms of any object is reduced to the unit group); it amounts to the same thing to say that the category is equivalent to the category \(C'\) defined by a set \(I\) (with \(\mathrm{ob}\, C' = I\), and as only arrows the same arrows). When we already assume that \(C\) is a groupoid, then say that \(C\) is discrete amounts to saying that for two objects \(X, Y\) of \(C\), there exists at most one arrow of \(X\) in \(Y\), i.e. that \(C\) is isomorphic to the category defined by a set foreordained.
The equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) of 6.5 is an immediate consequence of the reminders previous and

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.5.2

Let \(p : E' \to E\) be a fibrating functor. So:

(i) For \(p\) to be conservative, it is necessary and sufficient for its fiber categories to be groupoids.

(ii) For \(p\) to be faithful, it is necessary and sufficient for its fiber categories to be ordered categories.

Demonstration of 6.5.2. (i) Assume \(p\) conservative. For any arrow \(u\) of a fiber \(E'_X\), \(p(u) = \mathrm{id}_X\) is an isomorphism, so \(u\) is an isomorphism in \(E'\), so also in \(E'_X\) (because an inverse of \(u\) in \(E'\) will obviously be an inverse in \(E'_X\)). So \(E'_X\) is a groupoid. Conversely, suppose the \(E'_X\) of the groupoids, and let \(u'\) be an arrow of \(E'\) such that \(p(u')\) is an isomorphism, let us prove that \(u\) is an isomorphism. For this we notes that, \(p\) being a fiber, we can factor \(u' : X' \to Y'\) into a compound \(X' \to u^*(Y') \to Y'\), where the first arrow is a \(X\)-morphism (NB \(X = p(X')\), \(u = p(u')\)) and the second is a Cartesian morphism over \(u\). The first arrow is a isomorphism since \(E'_X\) is a groupoid, and the second is, because a morphism Cartesian of a fibered category is obviously an isomorphism as soon as its projection is.

(ii) Suppose \(p\) faithful, and let \(X', Y'\) be two objects of a fiber category \(E'_X\). So two arrows of \(X'\) in \(Y'\) are above the same arrow \(\mathrm{id}_X\) of \(E\), so are identical, so \(E'_X\) is ordered.

Conversely, assume the ordered fiber categories, and prove that \(p\) is faithful. Let then be \(u', v' : X' \rightrightarrows Y'\) arrows of \(E'\) above the same arrow \(u : X \to Y\) from \(E\). They are then factorized into \(X' \rightrightarrows u^*(Y') \to Y'\), where the two arrows \(X' \rightrightarrows u^*(Y')\) are arrows of \(E'_X\) of the same source and same purpose; these are therefore equal, therefore \(u' = v'\), cqfd.

Let's return to the demonstration of 6.5. We have proven (i) ⟺ (ii). On the other hand (ii) ⟺ (iv) is quite clear: in fact, on the one hand the category \(E_{/F}\) is fiberized on \(E\) with categories fibers the discrete categories defined by the sets \(F(X)\), as it immediately follows definitions; on the other hand, if \(p\) is as in (ii), then by virtue of the sorite SGA 1 VI 8 the fibered category \(E'\) on \(E\) is \(E\)-equivalent to the split category on \(E\) defined by the functor \(E \to (\mathrm{Cat})\) defined by the functor \(F : E \to (\mathrm{Ens})\), associating with all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) the set of object isomorphy classes of \(E'_X\). But this category split is \(E\)-isomorphic to the category \(E_{/F}\). As (iv) ⟹ (iii) is clear, it remains to be prove (iii) ⟹ (i). Now it is clear that for \(p\) to be faithful (resp. conservative) it is necessary and it suffices that the induced functors \(E'_{/X'} \to E_{/p(X')}\) are, a fortiori it suffices that these be fully faithful; therefore it remains to prove only that (iii) implies that \(p\) is fibering. But we still see that a functor \(p\) is fibering if and only if the induced functors \(E'_{/X'} \to E_{/p(X')}\) are. This is particularly the case if they are equivalences of surjective categories on objects.
7. Generator and cogenerator subcategories

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\). They say that \(C\) is a subcategory of \(E\) generating by strict epimorphisms (resp. by epimorphisms) if for any object \(X\) of \(E\), the family of arrows of \(E\) of goal \(X\), of source \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), is epimorphic (resp. strict epimorphic) (1.3). We say that \(C\) is a subcategory of \(E\) generator (resp. generator for monomorphisms, resp. generating for strict monomorphisms) if for any arrow \(u : Y \to X\) (resp. all monomorphism of \(E\), resp. any strict monomorphism of \(E\) (10.5)), such that for all \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the corresponding application \(\mathrm{Hom}(X',Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X',X)\) is bijective, \(u\) is an isomorphism. Finally, we say that a family \((X_i)\) of objects of \(E\) isgenerative by strict epimorphisms (resp. .....) if the full subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) generated by this family is generative by strict epimorphisms (resp. .....).

Note that in terms of the family \((h_{X'})_{X'\in\mathrm{ob}\, C}\) of functors \[ h_{X'} : E \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \qquad (X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C) \] represented by the \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), we can express the condition that \(C\) is generating (resp. generator for monomorphisms, resp. generator for monomorphisms strict) by that the family \((h_{X'})\) is conservative (resp. conservative for monomorphisms, resp. conservative for strict monomorphisms) (6.1). It results also immediately definitions that \(C\) is generated by epimorphisms if and only if the family \((h_{X'})_{X'\in\mathrm{ob}\, C}\) is faithful (6.1). We will also give below (7.2 (i)) an analogous interpretation for the condition on \(C\) to be generating by strict epimorphisms.

As for the notions introduced in 6.1, the notions of 7.1 are mainly useful when \(E\) has suitable accuracy properties, in which case the various The notions introduced have a clear tendency to all be equivalent (7.3). This is why the question of which of these notions 7.1 should be considered the most important hardly arises; in the most important cases, these notions coincide and the term “generating subcategory” can therefore be interpreted indifferently as relating to any of the properties considered in 7.1 (for example the first, which is the strongest of all as we will see (7.2 (ii))).

Suppose \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and consider the canonical functor \[ \varphi : E \to \widehat{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C^\circ, \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \tag{7.1.3.1} \] composed of functors \(E \to \widehat{E} \to \widehat{C}\), where the first functor is the canonical functor (1.3.3), and the second the functor restriction to \(C\). Note that it is obvious that it comes down to even to say that the previous functor \(\varphi\) is conservative (resp. faithful), or to say that the family of functors \(h_{X'} : X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X',X) = \varphi(X)(X')\), for \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) variable, is a conservative (resp. faithful) family, that is also to say (7.1.2) that \(C\) is generative (resp. generating by epimorphism). Likewise \(\varphi\) is conservative for monomorphisms (resp. for strict monomorphisms) if and only the family of \(h_{X'}\) (\(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\)) is conservative for monomorphisms (resp. for strict monomorphisms), i.e. if and only if the subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) is generating for monomorphisms (resp. for strict monomorphisms).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.2

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(C\) be a full subcategory.

(i) The following conditions are equivalent:

a) \(C\) is a subcategory generating by strict epimorphisms.

b) For all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), designating by \(C_{/X}\) the full subcategory of \(E_{/X}\) formed from the arrows \(X' \to X\) of source \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the natural arrow of the functor inclusion \(j : C_{/X} \to E\) in the constant functor on \(C_{/X}\) with value \(X\) makes \(X\) a inductive limit of \(j\): \[ X \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} X' . \]

c) The canonical functor \(\varphi\) of (7.1.3.1) is fully faithful.

(ii) We have the following implications between the notions of 7.1:

1) \(C\) generator by epim. strict (\(\varphi\) fully faithful)

2) \(C\) generator by epimorphisms (\(\varphi\) faithful)

3) \(C\) generating (\(\varphi\) conservative)

4) \(C\) generator for monomorphisms (\(\varphi\) conservative for mon.)

5) \(C\) generator for my. strict (\(\varphi\) conservative for mon. strict)

\[ \xymatrix@R=1.2em@C=3em{ 1) \ar@{=>}[dr] & \\ 2) & 3) \ar@{=>}[d] \\ & 4) \ar@{=>}[d] \\ & 5) } \]

(iii) We have the following conditional implications:

a) If in \(E\) the epimorphic families of arrows are strict epimorphic, we have 2) ⟹ 1). If in \(E\) the monomorphisms are strict, we have 5) ⟹ 4).

b) If in \(E\) the kernels of pairs of arrows (resp. the fiber products) are representable, then we have 3) ⟹ 2) (resp. 4) ⟹ 3)).

c) If in \(E\) any family of morphisms \(X_i \to X\) with the same goal \(X\) is factorized into a strict epimorphic family (resp. epimorphic) \(X_i \to Y\) followed by a monomorphism (resp. of a strict monomorphism) \(Y \to X\), then we have 4) ⟹ 1) (resp. 5) ⟹ 2)).
Let’s report it right away

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.3

All the concepts considered in 6.1 (and included in the diagram implications of (ii) above) are equivalent in each of the following two cases:

(i) In \(E\), the kernels of double arrows and the fiber products are representable, monomorphisms are strict and epimorphic families of arrows are strict epimorphic.

(ii) In \(E\), any family \((X_i \to X)_{i\in I}\) of arrows with the same goal \(X\) is factorized into an epimorphic family \((X_i \to Y)\) followed by a monomorphism \(Y \to X\), all monomorphism of \(E\) is strict, and any epimorphic family of arrows of \(E\) is strict.

Indeed, in case (i) we have 4) ⟹ 3) and 3) ⟹ 2) thanks to b), and 5) ⟹ 4) and 2) ⟹ 1) thanks to a). In case (ii) we have thanks to c), the implications 4) ⟹ 1) and 5) ⟹ 2). We therefore concludes thanks to the implication diagram 7.2 (ii).

Demonstration of 7.2. (i) The implication b) ⟹ a) follows immediately from the definitions. Let's prove a) ⟹ b). So under hypothesis a), it is necessary to prove that for all \(X, Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), any arrow system \[ u_{X'} : X' \to Y \] indexed by \(X' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_{/X}\), such that we have \(u_{X'} f = u_{X''}\) for any arrow \(f : X'' \to X'\) in \(C_{/X}\), is factorized by an arrow (necessarily unique by the hypothesis a)) \(X \to Y\). According to hypothesis a), it suffices to verify that for any object \(Z\) of \(E_{/X}\) and any pair of morphisms \(v' : Z \to X'\), \(v'' : Z \to X''\) in \(E_{/X}\), with \(X'\) and \(X''\) in \(C_{/X}\), we have \(u_{X'} v' = u_{X''} v''\). Now, thanks to hypothesis a), the family of arrows \(w : X''' \to Z\), with \(X''' \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), is epimorphic, and it is therefore sufficient to check that for all such \(w\), we have \((u_{X'} v')w = (u_{X''} v'')w\), which is also written \(u_{X'}(v'w) = u_{X''}(v''w)\) and immediately results from the hypothesis made on the family of \(u\).

Let us now prove the equivalence of conditions b) and c). For this we note that for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), the object \(\varphi(X)\) of \(\widehat{C}\) is inductive limit in \(\widehat{C}\) of the functor canonical \(\widehat{C}_{/\varphi(X)} \to \widehat{C}\) (3.4); or \(\widehat{C}_{/\varphi(X)}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(C_{/X}\), so that we have in \(\widehat{C}\) \[ \varphi(X) = \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} X', \] where we identify the object \(X'\) of \(C\) with the functor \(\in \widehat{C}\) that it represents. We subsequently have, for a second object \(Y\) of \(E\), a canonical isomorphism \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X),\varphi(Y)) &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C_{/X}} \mathrm{Hom}(X',\varphi(Y)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C_{/X}} \varphi(Y)(X') \quad (1.4) \\ &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{C_{/X}} \mathrm{Hom}(X',Y) . \end{aligned} \tag{\(*\)} \] This established, the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X),\varphi(Y)) \] defined by \(\varphi\) is none other, via the isomorphism between the extreme members of \((*)\), than the app \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y) \to \varprojlim_{C_{/X}} \mathrm{Hom}(X',Y) \] deduced from the inductive system of arrows \(X' \to X\) indexed by \(C_{/X}\) considered in 7.2 (i) b). So the first map is bijective for all \(Y\) (\(X\) being fixed) if and only if \(X\) is an inductive limit of the inclusion functor \(j : C_{/X} \to E\), which proves the equivalence b) and c).

(ii) The implications 1) ⟹ 2) and 3) ⟹ 4) ⟹ 5) are trivial under the definitions. The implication 1) ⟹ 3) is obtained by interpreting the property 1) by the full fidelity of \(\varphi\) thanks to (i), and observing that a fully faithful functor is conservative. But we already have observed (7.1.1) that 3) means that \(\varphi\) is conservative.

(iii) Assertion a) is a tautology. Assertion b) follows from 6.2 (i) (resp. 6.2 (iv)) applied to the functor \(\varphi\), given that the latter is left-exact. Let us finally prove c). Consider, for a \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), the family of all morphisms \(X_i \to X\), with \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\); by hypothesis on \(E\) it is factorized into an epimorphic \(X_i \to Y\) family effective (resp. epimorphic) followed by a monomorphism (resp. a monomorphism strict) \(Y \to X\). Then hypothesis 4) (resp. 5)) implies that \(Y \to X\) is an isomorphism, therefore the family considered is strict epimorphic (resp. epimorphic), cqfd.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\), \(X\) be a object of \(E\).
