We assume \(C\) generator in \(E\) (resp. \(C\) generator for the monomorphisms, and that the fiber product of two sub-objects of \(X\) on \(X\) is representable in \(E\)). Then a strict subobject (10.11) (resp. a subobject) \(X'\) of \(X\) is known when we know, for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the part of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) image of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\). By continuation, the cardinal of the set of strict sub-objects (resp. of the set of sub-objects) of \(X\) is increased by \(\prod_{T\in\mathrm{ob}\, C} 2^{\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)}\), and if \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), it is increased by \(2^{\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Fl}\, C}\).

Let us first prove the assertion respé. Let \(X', X''\) be two sub-objects of \(X\) such that for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), images of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\) and \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X'')\) in \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) are equal. They are therefore also equal to the image of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X''')\), where \(X'''\) is the product bundled from \(X'\) and \(X''\) to \(X\). As \(C\) is generating for monomorphisms, it follows that the monomorphisms \(X''' \to X'\) and \(X''' \to X''\) are isomorphisms, therefore \(X'\) and \(X''\) are equal, being separately equal to the subobject \(X'''\) of \(X\).

Let's prove the unanswered assertion. By definition of the notion of strict sub-object, it just check that the knowledge of the part \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\) of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) implies knowledge of those of the double arrows \(X \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} T\) such that \(ui = vi\), where \(i : X' \to X\) is the canonical injection. Now as \(C\) is generative, the relation \(ui = vi\) is equivalent to the relation \((ui)f = (vi)f\) for all \(f \in \mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\), i.e. to \(ug = vg\) for all \(g \in \mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) coming from \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\) (i.e. of the form \(if\), with \(f \in \mathrm{Hom}(T,X')\)), which proves our assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.5

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a full generating subcategory, \(X\) an object of \(C\). Then a strict quotient (10.8) \(X'\) of \(X\) is known when we know, for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the part of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)^2\) formed of pairs \((u,v)\) such that \(qu = qv\), where \(q : X \to X'\) is the canonical morphism. So the cardinal of the set of strict quotients of \(X\) is increased by \(\prod_{T\in\mathrm{ob}\, C} 2^{\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)^2}\).

Indeed, by definitions, a strict quotient \(X'\) of \(X\) is known when we know, for any object \(Y\) of \(E\), the part of \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\times\mathrm{Hom}(Y,X)\) formed of pairs \((u,v)\) such that \(qu = qv\), where \(q : X \to X'\) is the canonical morphism. But the relation \(qu = qv\) is equivalent to the relation \((qu)f = (qv)f\) for any morphism \(f : T \to Y\) of source \(T\) in \(C\), since \(C\) is generative. This relation is still written \(q(uf) = q(vf)\), which proves the first assertion of 7.5. The second results immediately.

We can generalize 7.5, by introducing, for a family of objects \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(E\), the notion of strict quotient in \(E\) of the family, by which we mean a family strict epimorphic \((p_i : X_i \to X')_{i\in I}\) of morphisms of \(E\), — being understood, as for ordinary quotients, we identify two such families \((p_i : X_i \to X')\) and \((q_i : X_i \to X'')\) if we can find an isomorphism \(v : X' \to X''\) (necessarily unique) such that we have \(v p_i = q_i\) for all \(i \in I\). With this terminology, the demonstration of 7.5 applies ne varietur to give the

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 7.5.2

Let \(E, C\) as in 7.5, and \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of objects of \(E\). Then a strict quotient \(X'\) of \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(E\) (7.5.1) is known when we know, for any \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), and any pair \((i,j) \in I\times I\), the part of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T,X_i)\times\mathrm{Hom}(T,X_j)\) formed of pairs \((u,v)\) such that \(p_i u = p_j v\), where for all \(i \in I\), \(p_i : X_i \to X'\) denotes the canonical morphism. Consequently, the cardinal of the set strict quotients of \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(E\) is increased by \(\prod_{T\in\mathrm{ob}\, C} \prod_{i,j\in I} 2^{\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X_i)\times\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X_j)}\).

We see immediately that the analogous conclusion is true if we only suppose that \(C\) is generating for strict monomorphisms, provided that we assume that the products \(X_i \times X_j\) are representable and we limit ourselves to the effective quotients of the family \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), i.e. strict quotients \(X'\) such as fiber products \(X_i \times_X X_j\) are representable in \(E\) (which is not a restriction if \(E\) is stable by products fibers).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.6

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\) generator by epimorphisms (7.1), \(\pi_0\) an infinite cardinal \(\geq \mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Fl}\, C\), \(\pi \geq \pi_0\) a cardinal, \((u_i : X_i \to X)_{i\in I}\) a universal epimorphic family (10.3) in \(E\) formed from quarable morphisms (10.7) with sources \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), and such that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\). Then \(\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Fl}\, C_{/X} \leq \pi^{\pi_0}\).

Let \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), it will suffice to prove that we have \[ \mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X) \leq \pi^{\pi_0}, \tag{7.6.1} \] because we will conclude successively \[ \mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{ob}\, C_{/X} \leq (\pi^{\pi_0}) \times \mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{ob}\, C \leq (\pi^{\pi_0})\times\pi_0 = \pi^{\pi_0}, \] finally \[ \mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Fl}\, C_{/X} \leq ((\pi^{\pi_0})^2)\times\pi_0 = \pi^{\pi_0}, \] since for two objects \(S, T\) of \(C_{/X}\), we have \(\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}_{C_{/X}}(S,T) \leq \pi_0\) because \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(S,T) \leq \pi_0\). To prove 7.6.1, we first note the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.6.2

Let \(J\) be a set such that \(\mathrm{card}\, J = \pi_0\). For any object \(T\) of \(C\), and every homomorphism \(f : T \to X\), there exists a family \((v_j : S_j \to T)_{j\in J}\) epimorphic, with sources \(S_j \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), and for all \(j \in J\) a \(i(j) \in I\) and a \(g_j : S_j \to X_{i(j)}\) such that we have \(u_{i(j)} g_j = f v_j\).

Indeed, the family of \(X_i \times_X T \to T\) is epimorphic by hypothesis, on the other hand, as \(C\) is generator by strict epimorphisms, for all \(i\), the family of arrows \(S \to X_i \times_X T\) from source \(S \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) is epimorphic, therefore by transitivity the family arrows \(v : S \to T\) of source in \(\mathrm{ob}\, C\) which are factorized by one of the \(X_i \times_X T\) is epimorphic. Now these are the arrows \(v : S \to T\) of source in \(C\) for which there exists a \(i \in I\) and a \(g : S \to X_i\) such that \(u_i g = fv\). Like all of these arrows \(v : S \to T\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Fl}\, C\), so of cardinal \(\leq \pi_0\), it can be indexed by the set of indices \(J\), hence the lemma.

Let us now note that a morphism \(f : T \to X\) is known when we know the \(f v_j\), which are known when we know the \(g_j\). So \(\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{Hom}(T,X)\) is increased by the cardinal of all families \((i(j), S_j, v_j, g_j)_{j\in J}\); as the cardinal of all applications \(j \to I\) is \(\leq \pi^{\pi_0}\), and as for such application \(j \mapsto i(j)\) fixed, the cardinality of the set of corresponding families \(S_j, f_j, v_j\) is increased by that of the set of applications of \(J\) in \(\mathrm{Fl}\, C \times \mathrm{Fl}\, C\), which is \(\leq \pi_0^{\pi_0}\) since \(\mathrm{card}(\mathrm{Fl}\, C \times \mathrm{Fl}\, C) = \pi_0^2 = \pi_0\), we find that the first member of (7.6.1) is increased by \(\pi^{\pi_0} \times \pi_0^{\pi_0} = \pi^{\pi_0}\), which completes the demonstration of 7.6.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.7

Let \(E\) be a category where fiber products are representable, \((X_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A}\) a family of objects of \(E\) generator, \((\varphi_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of functors \(\varphi_i : E \to E_i\) switching to fiber products. For \((\varphi_i)\) to be conservative (6.1), it It is necessary and sufficient that for all \(\alpha \in A\) and for any sub-object \(X'\) of \(X_\alpha\) distinct from \(X_\alpha\), there exists a \(i \in I\) such that \(\varphi_i(X') \to \varphi_i(X_\alpha)\) is not an isomorphism. In this case, if \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and if \(A\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, there exists a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small part \(J\) of \(I\) such that \((\varphi_j)_{j\in J}\) is already a conservative family of functors.

The necessity of the envisaged condition of conservativity being obvious, let us prove its sufficiency. By virtue of 6.1 (iv), it is sufficient to prove that \((\varphi_i)\) is conservative for monomorphisms. Let \(u : Y' \to Y\) be a monomorphism in \(E\) which is not a isomorphism, it is necessary to prove that there exists \(i \in I\) such that \(\varphi_i(u)\) is not an isomorphism. By hypothesis on \((X_\alpha)\), there exists a \(\alpha\) and a morphism \(X_\alpha \to Y\) which does not factorize by \(Y'\), in other words, such that the inverse image \(X'\) of \(Y'\) is a sub-object of \(X_\alpha\) distinct from \(X_\alpha\). By hypothesis, there exists a \(i \in I\) such that \(\varphi_i(X') \to \varphi_i(X_\alpha)\) is not not an isomorphism. Like \(\varphi_i(X') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varphi_i(X_\alpha) \underset{\varphi_i(Y)}{\times} \varphi_i(Y')\), it It follows that \(\varphi_i(Y') \to \varphi_i(Y)\) is not an isomorphism.
The second assertion of 7.7 follows immediately from the first, taking into account 7.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.7.1

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where fiber products are representable, and admitting a generating family of objects which is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. So for any generating family \((Y_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(E\), there exists a generating subfamily \(\mathcal{U}\)-small \((Y_j)_{j\in J}\) (\(\mathrm{card}\, J \in \mathcal{U}\)).
It is enough to apply 7.7 to the \(\mathcal{U}\)-small generating family \((X_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A}\) of \(E\) and to the family of functors \(\varphi_i\) (\(i \in I\)) represented by \(Y_i\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.8

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a full subcategory generating by strict epimorphisms, \(D\) a category, \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}'(E,D)\) the subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,D)\) formed from functors which commute at inductive limits of the type \(C_{/X}\), where \(X\) is any object in \(E\). Then the functor \(F \mapsto F|C\) induces a functor fully faithful \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}'(E,D) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,D) . \]

As a result, if \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,D)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category (1.1.), for example (1.1.1. b)) if \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,D)\) is also a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

Let \(F, G : E \to D\) be two functors in the first member, and \(u : F \to G\) be one homomorphism. If \(X = \varinjlim X_i\) in \(E\) and if \(F\) and \(G\) commute at the inductive limit considered, then \(u(X) : F(X) \to G(X)\) is identified with the limit of morphisms \(u(X_i) : F(X_i) \to G(X_i)\), and is therefore known when we know the \(u(X_i)\). This shows that the functor envisaged in 7.8 is faithful, given the implication a) ⟹ b) in 7.2 (i). Let us conversely \(v : F|C \to G|C\) be a homomorphism, let us prove that it comes from a homomorphism \(u : F \to G\). We will define, for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), \[ u(X) : F(X) = \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} F(X_i) \to G(X) = \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} G(X_i) \] as the inductive limit of \(v(X_i)\). It is immediate that we obtain a good functorial homomorphism in \(X\), therefore a \(u : F \to G\), and finally that the morphism induced by \(u\) of \(F|C\) in \(G|C\) is \(v\), which completes the demonstration.
7.9. Cogenerator families and subcategories.

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\). We say that \(C\) is cogenerator by strict monomorphisms (resp. cogenerator by monomorphisms, resp. cogenerator, resp. cogenerator for epimorphisms, resp. cogenerator for strict epimorphisms) if the full subcategory \(C^\circ\) of \(E^\circ\) is generating by strict epimorphisms (resp. .....). Analogous terminology for families. All results of this issue concerning the notion of generating subcategory and its variants (7.1), therefore give corresponding results for dual notions, that we leave it to the reader to formulate for their personal satisfaction.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.10

Let \(E\) be a category, \(C\) be a full generating subcategory (7.1), \(D\) a full subcategory of \(E\). For \(D\) to be a cogenerator (7.9), it is necessary and suffices only for any double arrow \(T \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} X\) in \(E\) of source \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), with \(u \neq v\), there exists a goal arrow \(w : X \to I\) \(I \in \mathrm{ob}\, D\), such as \(wu \neq wv\).

By definition, saying that \(D\) is a cogenerator means that for any double arrow \(Y \underset{v}{\overset{u}{ ightrightarrows}} X\) in \(E\) such that \(u eq v\), there is an arrow \(w : X o I\), with \(I \in \mathrm{ob}\, D\), such as \(wu eq wv\). So 7.10 just means just test this property when \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). Now as \(C\) is generative, the hypothesis \(u eq v\) implies that there exists \(f : T o Y\) such that \(uf eq vf\), hence by hypothesis the existence of a \(w : X o I\) of goal \(I \in \mathrm{ob}\, D\) such that \(w(uf) eq w(vf)\), hence \(wu eq wv\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.11

Since the notations are those of 7.10, assume that the objects \(I\) of \(D\) are injective objects of \(E\), i.e. such that for any monomorphism \(X \to Y\) in \(E\), the corresponding \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,I) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X,I)\) application is surjective. Let us further assume that every double arrow \(T \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} X\) in \(E\) factors into an epimorphic double arrow (resp. effective epimorphic) \(T \underset{v'}{\overset{u'}{\rightrightarrows}} X'\) followed by a monomorphism \(i : X' \to X\). So in criterion 7.10 for \(D\) to be a cogenerator, we can limit ourselves to the double arrows \((u,v)\) which are epimorphic (resp. effective epimorphic).
We conclude:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.12

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category admitting a small subcategory generator \(C\), and such that any object of \(E\) is a source of a monomorphism in an object injective of \(E\). Let us further suppose that for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\), the sum \(T \sqcup T\) in \(E\) is representable, and that any source morphism \(T \sqcup T\) is factorized into an epimorphism effective followed by a monomorphism. Then \(E\) admits a full subcategory \(D\) cogenerator. Precisely, we can take \(D\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{ob}\, D \leq \prod_{T,T'\in\mathrm{ob}\, C} 2^{\mathrm{card}(\mathrm{Hom}(T',T\sqcup T))^2}\).

Indeed, by virtue of 7.11, it is sufficient for all \(T \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) and for any effective quotient \(X\) of \(T \sqcup T\), to choose an embedding of \(X\) in an injective object \(I\) of \(E\), and to take for \(D\) the full subcategory of \(E\) generated by these \(I\). The conclusion then results from 7.5.

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 7.13

To construct small cogenerator subcategories in terms of small generating subcategories, we are therefore led to look for conditions so that a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(E\) admits "sufficient injectives", i.e. that any object is immersed in a injective object (by a monomorphism). It is well known [Tohoku] that this condition is satisfied in an \(\mathcal{U}\)-abelian category with (small) exact filtering inductive limits (axiom AB 5 of loc. cit.) admitting a small generating family. Rental construction. cit. is moreover not linked in an essential way to abelian categories, and works also in the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets on a topological space \(X \in \mathcal{U}\). We will not state here the accuracy properties that make the construction in question, and we will limit ourselves to pointing out that in the particular case of the category of sheaves of sets on \(X\), we immediately return to the case of loc. cit. in the following way. Note that if \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a Ring on \(X\), then any injective object \(I\) of the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-Modules is also injective as an object of the category of bundles of sets. Indeed, if \(F\) is a bundle of sets, and \(\mathcal{O}_X[F]\) the “\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-Free module generated by \(F\)”, we have by definition a homomorphism of sheaves of sets \[ F \to \mathcal{O}_X[F] \tag{\(*\)} \] giving rise to an isomorphism, functorial in \(F\) \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_X[F], I) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(F,I) . \] As the functor \(F \mapsto \mathcal{O}_X[F]\) manifestly transforms monomorphism into monomorphism, it immediately follows that if \(I\) is an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-Injective module, it is also an beam of injective sets. It follows that if the morphism \((*)\) is a monomorphism (this which is the case if we take for \(\mathcal{O}_X\) a constant Ring of value a ring \(A \neq 0\)), then an embedding of \(\mathcal{O}_X[F]\) in an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-Injective module \(I\) gives an embedding of \(F\) in the injective set bundle underlying \(I\).

The same argument applies, without change, to the case of the category of bundles of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sets on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, which will be introduced in the following discussion.

The interest in the existence of a small cogeneration subcategory lies above all in the representability criterion 3.12.7 below.
8. Ind-objects and pro-objects
8.1. Cofinal functors and cofinal subcategories

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.1

Either \[ \varphi : I \to I' \tag{8.1.1.1} \] a functor. We say that \(\varphi\) is a cofinal functor if for any category \(C\) and for any functor \(u : I' \to C\), considering \(\varinjlim u\) and \(\varinjlim u\circ\varphi\) as objects of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C, (\mathcal{V}\text{-Ens}))^\circ\) (2.1, 2.3.1) (where \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe such that \(I, I' \in \mathcal{V}\), and \(C\) either a \(\mathcal{V}\)-category), the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim u\circ\varphi \to \varinjlim u \tag{8.1.1.2} \] is an isomorphism. When \(\varphi\) is the inclusion functor of a subcategory of \(I'\), we say that \(I\) is a cofinal subcategory if \(\varphi\) is cofinal.
Note that for \(\varphi, u\) data, the bijectivity of (8.1.1.2) does not depend of the choice of the universe \(\mathcal{V}\).

Returning to the definition of the terms appearing in (8.1.1.2) (2.1, 2.3.1), we see what to say that \(\varphi\) is cofinal also means that for any universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(I\) and \(I'\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, and any functor \[ v : I'^\circ \to (\mathcal{V}\text{-Ens}) , \] canonical homomorphism \[ \varprojlim v \to \varprojlim v\circ\varphi \tag{8.1.2.1} \] is an isomorphism i.e. a bijection. To see that this condition is indeed necessary, we will observe, posing \(u = v^\circ\), that it also means that the condition of definition 8.1.1 is fulfilled when we do \(C = (\mathcal{V}\text{-Ens})^\circ\) (which implies that the inductive limits considered in (8.1.1.2) are representable in \(C\)).
It is immediate on the definition thatthe compound of two cofinal functors is cofinal.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.3

Let \(\varphi : I \to I'\) be a functor.

a) For \(\varphi\) to be cofinal, \(\varphi\) must satisfy the condition

F 1) For every object \(i'\) of \(I'\), there exists an object \(i\) of \(I\) such that \(\varphi(i)\) increases \(i'\) (i.e. such as \(\mathrm{Hom}(i',\varphi(i)) \neq \varnothing\)).

b) Suppose \(I\) filtering. For \(\varphi\) to be cofinal, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\varphi\) satisfies condition F 1) as well as the following condition:

F 2) For any object \(i\) of \(I\) and any double arrow \(i' \underset{g'}{\overset{f'}{\rightrightarrows}} \varphi(i)\) in \(I'\) of but \(\varphi(i)\), there exists an arrow \(h : i \to j\) in \(I\) such that \(\varphi(h)f' = \varphi(h)g'\).

In addition, if \(\varphi\) is cofinal, \(I'\) is filtering.

c) Suppose \(I'\) filtering, and \(\varphi\) fully faithful. For \(\varphi\) to be cofinal, it is necessary and it is enough that it satisfies condition F 1) of a). This implies that \(I\) is filtering.

Demonstration. For the necessity in a) and b), definition 8.1.1 will be used in the only case where \(u\) is the canonical inclusion functor (1.3.3) \[ u : I' \hookrightarrow \widehat{I'} \] (a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(I, I'\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-small being chosen). We can then interpret (8.1.1.2) as an arrow of \(\widehat{I'}\) (3.1), whose goal is the final functor on \(I'\) (3.4). We immediately sees that the condition F 1) expresses that this arrow is an epimorphism of \(\widehat{I'}\), i.e. is surjective on each argument (taking into account that the inductive limits in \(\widehat{I'}\) are calculated "argument by argument"). This proves a). Now suppose \(I\) filtering and \(\varphi\) cofinal. Then \(I'\) is filtering: in fact, the condition that two objects of \(I'\) are increased by a third immediately results from the same condition on \(I\), and from F 1), and it is only necessary to prove the condition PS 2) of 2.7, i.e. that for every double arrow \[ f', g' : i' \rightrightarrows j' \] in \(I'\), there exists an arrow \(h' : j' \to k'\) of \(I'\) such that \(h'f' = h'g'\). But by virtue of F 1) we can assume \(k'\) of the form \(\varphi(i)\). But as by the hypothesis \(\varphi\) cofinal, for all object \(i'\) of \(I'\) \(\varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(i',\varphi(i))\) is the set reduced to one element, it results from the standard description of inductive filtering limits in \((\mathrm{Ens})\) (2.8.1) that this implies the existence of an arrow \(h : i \to j\) in \(I\) such that \(\varphi(h)f' = \varphi(h)g'\). This therefore completes prove that \(I'\) is filtering, and at the same time proves the condition F 2).

To prove that the conditions set out in b) are sufficient for \(\varphi\) to be cofinal, we use form 8.1.2 of the definition. We immediately see that the condition F 1) implies that (8.1.2.1) is a monomorphism i.e. is injective on each argument, while that the condition F 2) (joined to F 1) ensures that it is bijective (taking into account the calculation of projective limits in \(\widehat{I'^\circ}\) argument by argument). This therefore proves b).
Finally, if \(I'\) is filtering and \(\varphi\) fully faithful, then it is immediate that the condition F 1) implies that \(I\) is filtering, and implies condition F 2). So c) results from a) and b).

When \(I'\) is a filtering category, \(I\) a subcategory full of \(I'\), we therefore sees by 8.1.3 c) that the condition that \(I\) is cofinal in \(I'\) only depends on the part \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I\) of the preordered set \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I'\) (for the preorder relation “\(x \leq y\)” ⟺ “\(\mathrm{Hom}(x,y) \neq \varnothing\)”). If \(J'\) is a preordered set, \(J\) a part of \(J'\), we will sometimes say that \(J\) is a cofinal part of \(J'\) when every element of \(J'\) is increased by an element of \(J\). When \(J'\) is filtering, this means that the functor \(J \hookrightarrow J'\) inclusion code for associated categories is cofinal.

In the following, we will only use the notion of cofinal functor in cases where the categories \(I\) and \(I'\) are filtering. Classically, we even limited ourselves to categories associated with preordered sets (i.e. in which there is at most one arrow of given source and purpose). It appears, however, that this restriction is annoying in applications, the “natural” filter categories that are introduced into many applications not being not ordered categories. The following result, due to P. DELIGNE, shows, however, that there is no essential difference between the two points of view:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.6

Let \(I\) be a small filtering category. So there is a small ordered set \(E\), and a cofinal functor \(\varphi : \underline{E} \to I\), where \(\underline{E}\) denotes the category associated with \(E\).

Let us first assume that the preordered set \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I\) has no largest element. Let us call subdiagram of \(I\) a pair \(D = (\underline{O}, F)\) formed of a part \(F\) of \(\mathrm{Fl}(I)\) and of a part \(\underline{O}\) of \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I\), such that for all \(f \in F\), the source and purpose of \(f\) belong to \(\underline{O}\). An element \(e\) of \(\underline{O}\) is called final object of subdiagram \(D\) if for all \(x \in \underline{O}\), the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(x,e) \cap F\) of arrows of \(D\) of source \(x\) and goal \(e\) has exactly one element \(f_x\), if for every arrow \(g : x \to y\) of \(D\), we have \(f_x = f_y \circ g\), and if \(f_e = \mathrm{id}_e\). Let \(E\) be the set of finite subdiagrams \(D\) of \(I\) having a final element unique \(\varphi(D)\), and order \(E\) by inclusion. If \(D, D'\) are two elements of \(E\) and \(D \subset D'\), then there is a single arrow \(\varphi(D',D)\) of \(D'\) of source \(\varphi(D)\), of goal \(\varphi(D')\), and if we have inclusions \(D \subset D' \subset D''\) in \(E\), we obviously have \(\varphi(D'',D) = \varphi(D'',D')\varphi(D',D)\); we also have \(\varphi(D,D) = \mathrm{id}_{\varphi(D)}\). We therefore find a functor \(\varphi : \underline{E} \to I\), and everything comes down to proving that \(E\) is filtering and that \(\varphi\) is cofinal. Taking into account 8.1.3 b), three checks must be made:

1) Condition F 1) of 8.1.3: for all \(i \in I\), we take for \(D\) the subdiagram of \(I\) reduces to the object \(i\) and its identical arrow, then \(i \leq \varphi(D) = i\). This condition F 1) shows at the same time as \(E \neq \varnothing\).

2) Condition F 2): For all \(i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\), all \(D \in E\) and any double arrow \(i \rightrightarrows \varphi(D)\), find a diagram \(D' \in E\), containing \(D\), such that \(\varphi(D',D) : \varphi(D) \to \varphi(D')\) equalizes the double arrow. But as \(I\) is filtering, there exists a object \(j\) of \(I\) and an arrow \(f : \varphi(D) \to j\) which equalizes the double arrow. As \(I\) has no largest element, we can assume that \(j\) is a strict upper bound of \(\varphi(D)\), which implies that it is distinct from all other objects in \(D\). Let then \(D'\) be the subdiagram of \(I\) whose set of objects is \(\underline{O} \cup \{j\}\), and the set of arrows is formed by arrows of \(D\), plus the compounds \(x \xrightarrow{f_x} \varphi(D) \xrightarrow{f} j\), where \(x\) is an object of \(D\) and \(f_x\) is the single arrow of \(D\) with source \(x\) and goal \(\varphi(D)\), and \(\mathrm{id}_j\). It is then clear that \(D'\) is a finite subdiagram of \(I\), which it admits \(j\) as the only final object, therefore \(D' \in E\), and \(D'\) meets the desired condition.

3° Two subdiagrams \(D, D' \in E\) are contained in the same \(D'' \in E\). We can indeed find a strict upper bound \(j\) of \(\varphi(D)\) and \(\varphi(D')\), \[ \xymatrix{ \varphi(D) \ar[dr]^-{f} & \\ & j \\ \varphi(D') \ar[ur]_-{f'} & } \] and we take for \(D''\) the subdiagram whose set of objects is the union of the set of objects of \(D\), of \(D'\) and of \(\{j\}\), and the set of arrows is the union of the set of arrows of \(D\), of \(D'\), of the set of compounds \(f \circ f_x\) (\(x\) object of \(D\)) and \(f' \circ f'_{x'}\) (\(x'\) object of \(D'\)), and \(\{\mathrm{id}_j\}\). We thus obtain a finished subdiagram of \(I\), let's show that, even if we replace \(j, f, f'\) by \(j', gf, gf'\) with suitable \(g : j \to j'\), we can get \(j\) to be a final object of \(D''\). It amounts to the same thing to say that for all \(x\) which is both the object of \(D\) and \(D'\), we have \(f f_x = f' f'_{x'}\). Now this is a finite set of double-arrows that need to be equalized by a \(g : j \to j'\), which is possible since \(I\) is filtering.

This completes the demonstration in the envisaged case. We bring the general case back to this one, by introducing the filtering category \(\underline{\mathbb{N}}\) associated with the ordered set \(\mathbb{N}\) of integers natural, and noting that the category \(\underline{\mathbb{N}} \times I\) is filtering, that it has no more large element, and that the projection \(\underline{\mathbb{N}} \times I \to I\) is a cofinal functor.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.1.7

Let \(I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. So that there exists a small ordered set filtering \(E\), and a cofinal functor \(\underline{E} \to I\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(I\) is filtering and that \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I\) admits a small cofinal part \(I'\) (8.1.4).
It is necessary under 8.1.3 a), and sufficient under 8.1.6 applied to the full subcategory of \(I\) defined by \(I'\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.8

Let \(I\) be a filtering category. It is said that \(I\) isessentially small if \(I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and if it satisfies the equivalent conditions of 8.1.7.
8.2. Ind-objects and ind-representable functors.

We set in the following a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\), which we always consider as diving into the \(\widehat{C}\) category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-presheaves using the canonical functor (1.3.1) \[ h : C \hookrightarrow \widehat{C} . \tag{8.2.1.1} \] We will call ind-object of \(C\) (or inductive system of \(C\)), any functor \[ \varphi : I \to C , \tag{8.2.1.2} \] where \(I\) is a filter category (2.7), called the index category of the ind-object. (\(I\) is otherwise arbitrary, and we do not require, in particular, that \(I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.) It will often be convenient to designate an ind-object \(\varphi\) by the index notation \((X_i)_{i\in\mathrm{ob}\, I}\), or even (by further abuse of notation) \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), or \((X_i)_i\) or \((X_i)\), where \(X_i = \varphi(i)\) for \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\). This notation is neither more nor less abusive than the notation classically used for inductive systems indexed by ordered sets filters (where the mention of transition morphisms is also absent). The \(X_i\) are called the component objects of object-ind \(\underline{X}\). We will pay attention that in the case general considered here, the “transition morphisms” \(\varphi(f)\) of the inductive system are indexed by the set \(\mathrm{Fl}\, I\) of the arrows of \(I\), which is not generally identified with a part of \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I \times \mathrm{Ob}\, I\); in other words, for \(i\) and \(j\) given, \(j\) increasing \(i\), it can exist more than one transition morphism from \(X_i\) to \(X_j\).

The most useful ind-objects \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(C\) are those for which the index category \(I\) is essentially small (8.1.8); such an ind-object is called mostly small. If \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) is so, using the fact that in \(\widehat{C}\) the small inductive limits are representable (3.1), we can consider \[ L(\underline{X}) \overset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \varinjlim h\cdot\underline{X} = \varinjlim_I X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, \widehat{C} , \tag{8.2.2.1} \] where the last limit is taken in \(\widehat{C}\). So \(L(\underline{X})\) is the pre-bundle \[ L(\underline{X}) : Y \mapsto \varinjlim_I \mathrm{Hom}(Y,X_i) \tag{8.2.2.2} \] on \(C\). We say that this prebundle is the ind-prebundle represented by the ind-object \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) from \(C\). A pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\) is called apre-beam ind-representable if it is isomorphic to a pre-sheaf \(L(\underline{X})\) ind-represented by an ind-object essentially small. It also follows from 8.1.6 that in this definition, we can suppose that \(I\) is a small category, and even that \(I\) is associated with an ordered set \(\in \mathcal{U}\).

Consider an ind-object \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\), given by a functor \(\varphi : I \to C\), and either \[ u : I' \to I \] a functor, where \(I'\) is a second filtering category. Then the functor \(\varphi \circ u\) is a ind-object \(\underline{X}'\) of \(C\) of index category \(I'\), which in index notation is written \((X_{u(i')})_{i'\in I'}\), which we call the ind-object of \(C\) deduced from \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) by change of index categoriesusing the functor \(u\). If \(I\) and \(I'\) (i.e. \(\underline{X}\) and \(\underline{X}'\)) are essentially small, we find a canonical morphism \[ L(\underline{X}') = \varinjlim_{i'} X_{u(i')} \to L(\underline{X}) = \varinjlim_i X_i \tag{8.2.3.1} \] between the ind-pre-beams represented by \(\underline{X}'\) and \(\underline{X}\) respectively. When \(u\) is a cofinal functor (8.1.1), then \(\underline{X}\) is essentially small if and only if \(\underline{X}'\) is (8.1.7), and the previous homomorphism (8.2.3.1) is an isomorphism \[ L(\underline{X}') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } L(\underline{X}) . \tag{8.2.3.2} \]

Let \[ \underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I} , \quad \underline{Y} = (Y_j)_{j\in J} \tag{8.2.4.1} \] two essentially small \(C\) ind-objects, indexed by categories (filtering and essentially small) \(I\) and \(J\). We callmorphism of the ind-object \(\underline{X}\) in the ind-object \(\underline{Y}\) any morphism \[ L(\underline{X}) \to L(\underline{Y}) \tag{8.2.4.2} \] between the pre-beams that they ind-represent. We pose \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{ind\text{-}ob}}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(L(\underline{X}),L(\underline{Y})) . \tag{8.2.4.3} \] (We delete the ind-ob index in Hom if we do not fear confusion). We define the composition of the ind-objects of \(C\) like the composition of the morphisms of the pre-sheaves that they ind-represent. If \(\mathcal{V} \supset \mathcal{U}\) is a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\), and if we limit ourselves to the essentially small ind-objects which are \(\in \mathcal{V}\), these therefore form the set of objects of a category, the set of arrows of which is formed by the triples \((\underline{X}, \underline{Y}, u)\), where \(\underline{X}\) and \(\underline{Y}\) are essentially small ind-objects \(\in \mathcal{V}\) and where \(u : \underline{X} \to \underline{Y}\) is a morphism of ind-objects, i.e. a morphism \(L(\underline{X}) \to L(\underline{Y})\). The category thus obtained is noted \[ \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) . \tag{8.2.4.4} \] When \(\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{U}\), we simply note it \[ \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{U}}(C) \quad \text{ou} \quad \mathrm{Ind}(C) , \tag{8.2.4.5} \] and we call it category of ind-objects of \(C\) relative to \(\mathcal{U}\), removing the mention of \(\mathcal{U}\) when no confusion is to be feared.

Obviously, if \(\mathcal{V} \subset \mathcal{V}'\) are two universes containing \(\mathcal{U}\), \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U})\) is a full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}'}(C,\mathcal{U})\); it then follows from 8.2.3 that the functor of inclusion is an equivalence of categories: \[ \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}'}(C,\mathcal{U}) . \tag{8.2.4.6} \] This shows in particular thatany essentially small ind-object of \(C\) defines an element of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), up to unique isomorphism. This observation justifies the abuse of language, quite common in practice, consisting of identifying any essentially small ind-object of \(C\) to a object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

It immediately follows from the definitions that for every universe \(\mathcal{V}\), we have a functor canonical \[ L : \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \to \widehat{C} \tag{8.2.4.7} \] who is fully faithful. These functors are obviously known with unique isomorphism closely, thanks to (8.2.4.6), when we know one of them, and in particular when we know the canonical functor \[ L : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \widehat{C} . \tag{8.2.4.8} \] As this functor is fully faithful, it is frequently used to identify an object of the first member with the functor that it ind-represents, or even to identify \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) with its essential image in \(\widehat{C}\), formed of unrepresentable pre-sheaves. We will do be careful, however, that this identification presents clearly more disadvantages than the analogous identification of \(C\) to a full subcategory of \(\widehat{C}\) by the functor \(h\) (8.2.1.1), because unlike the latter, the functor (8.2.4.8) is not in general injective on objects.

Let us explain the definition (8.2.4.3) in terms of index expressions (8.2.4.1) of the ind-objects considered. We find, taking into account the definition (8.2.2.1): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,\underline{Y}) \overset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \varprojlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i, L(\underline{Y})) = \varprojlim_i \varinjlim_j \mathrm{Hom}_C(X_i,Y_j), \] the last equality coming from (8.2.2.2) applied to \(\underline{Y}\). We therefore find a bijection canonical \[ \mathrm{Hom}((X_i)_{i\in I},(Y_j)_{j\in J}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{i\in I} \varinjlim_{j\in J} \mathrm{Hom}_C(X_i,Y_j) . \tag{8.2.5.1} \] We leave it to the reader to explain the composition of the morphisms of ind-objects on this formula. Note that it immediately follows from this formula that the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y})\) of homomorphisms of ind-objects is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, i.e. that the categories (8.2.4.4) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. It is the same thing to say (by virtue of (8.2.4.6)) that thecategory \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, i.e. the second member of (8.2.5.1) is small when \(I, J \in \mathcal{U}\), which immediately follows from the fact that \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category therefore the \(\mathrm{Hom}_C(X_i,Y_j)\) are small.

Let \(I\) be an essentially small filter category. So we see on (8.2.5.1), or on the functoriality of the inductive limit of a functor \(I \to \widehat{C}\) with respect to the said functor, that we have a canonical functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I,C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) . \tag{8.2.6.1} \] Please note that this functor is not in general fully faithful, or even faithful.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.2.7

The definitions in 8.2.4 make clear the notion of isomorphy of two (mostly small) ind-objects, which also means pre-bundle isomorphy that they ind-represent. We will pay attention that this is a very weak notion of isomorphy, if we compares it to the notion of isomorphy in categories of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I,C)\). Thus, a large number of fairly natural relations that can be imposed on an ind-object (such as that to have its components in a given strictly full subcategory, or that of being strict (8.12 below)) are not stable by isomorphism. It will therefore be necessary, if we want to working with stable notions by isomorphism of ind-objects, “saturating” the notions considered by passing to the strictly full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) generated by the subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) formed of objects satisfying the considered condition. See by example the notion of an essentially constant ind-object, introduced below (8.4).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.2.8

Let \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\) be two universes and \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category which belongs to \(\mathcal{V}\). We denote by \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) the following category:

a) The objects of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) are the functors \(\varphi : I \to C\) where \(I\) is a category filtering essentially \(\mathcal{U}\)-small belonging to \(\mathcal{V}\).

b) Let \((I,\varphi)\) and \((J,\psi)\) be two objects of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\). A morphism of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) of \((I,\varphi)\) in \((J,\psi)\) is a pair \((m,u)\), where \(m : I \to J\) is a functor and \(u : \varphi \to \psi\circ m\) is a morphism of functors. The composition of morphisms in \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) is defined in an obvious way.

Let us denote \(S\) the set of morphisms \((m,u) : (I,\varphi) \to (J,\psi)\) of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) such that \(m\) is cofinal and \(u\) is an isomorphism. Let \((I,\varphi)\) be an object of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\). The category \(\mathrm{Fl}(I)\) of the arrows of \(I\) are sent by two natural functors in \(I\): the source and the goal. Moreover these two functors are linked by the canonical morphism of functors \(v : \mathrm{source} \to \mathrm{but}\). Hence two morphisms in \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\) of \((\mathrm{Fl}(I),\varphi\circ\mathrm{source})\) in \((I,\varphi)\): \(p_1(I,\varphi) = (\mathrm{source}, \mathrm{id})\), \(p_2(I,\varphi) = (\mathrm{but}, \varphi\ast v : \varphi\circ\mathrm{source} \to \varphi\circ\mathrm{but})\). Let us note \(p : \mathrm{SysInd}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) the obvious functor. Let \(B\) be a category. Show that the functor \(F \mapsto F\circ p\): \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Ind}(C),B) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{SysInd}(C),B) \] is fully faithful, and that a functor \(G : \mathrm{SysInd}(C) \to B\) belongs to the essential image if and only if it has the following two properties:

1) For all \(s \in S\), \(G(s)\) is an isomorphism of \(B\).

2) For any object \((I,\varphi)\) of \(\mathrm{SysInd}(C)\), \(G(p_1(I,\varphi)) = G(p_2(I,\varphi))\).
8.3. Characterization of ind-representable functors

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.3.1

Let \(F\) be an ind-representable pre-beam on \(C\). Then \(F\) is exact at left, i.e. (2.3.2) for any finite category \(J\) and any functor \(\varphi : J \to C\) such that \(\varinjlim \varphi\) is representable (i.e. \(\varprojlim \varphi^\circ\) is representable), the canonical application \[ F(\varinjlim \varphi) \to \varprojlim F\circ\varphi \] is bijective.
Indeed, the representable presheaves being obviously exact on the left, it results immediately from 2.8 that it is the same for any filtering inductive limit of such functors, cqfd.

\(F\) being a pre-bundle on \(C\), we will often have to work with the category \[ C_{/F} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}_{/F} , \tag{8.3.2.1} \] which designates the full subcategory of the second member formed by the arrows \(X \to F\) of source in \(C\). It follows from 1.4 that the objects of this category are identified with couples \((X,u)\), where \(X\) is an object of \(C\) and \(u \in F(X)\). A morphism of \((X,u)\) into \((X',u')\) is then interpreted as an arrow \(f : X \to X'\) in \(C\) such that we have \(F(f)(u') = u\). The functor “forgetting the goal” of \(\widehat{C}_{/F}\) in \(\widehat{C}\) induces a functor (qualified as canonical). \[ C_{/F} \to C , \tag{8.3.2.2} \] already considered in 3.4, where we prove that the inductive limit of this functor exists in \(\widehat{C}\) (without smallness condition on \(C_{/F}\)) and is canonically isomorphic to \(F\).

Note that if in \(C\) the finite inductive limits are representable, and if \(F\) is exact on the left (i.e. transforms them into finite projective limits of \((\mathrm{Ens})\)), then it is the same in \(C_{/F}\), and a fortiori (2.7.1) \(C_{/F}\) is filtering. If, more generally, in \(C\) the sums of two objects and the cokernels of double arrows are representable, and if \(F\) transforms them into products resp. in nuclei, then \(C_{/F}\) is stable under the same types of finite inductive limits, therefore it is filtering if and only if it is non-empty, i.e. if and only if the functor \(F\) is not the constant functor of values \(\varnothing\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.3.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and \(F \in \mathrm{ob}\, \widehat{C}\), \(F : C^\circ \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-beam on \(C\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(F\) is unrepresentable (8.2.2).

(ii) Category \(C_{/F}\) (8.3.2) is filtering and essentially small.

(iii) (If in \(C\) the finite inductive limits are representable.) The functor \(F\) is exact left, and ob \(C_{/F}\) admits a small cofinal part (8.1.4).

(iii bis) (If in \(C\) the sums of two objects and the cokernels of double arrows are representable.) The functor \(F\) transforms sum of two objects of \(C\) into products, cokernels of double arrows of \(C\) in kernels, the category \(C_{/F}\) is non-empty i.e. the functor \(F\) is not the constant functor of value \(\varnothing\), finally there exists a small family of objects of \(C_{/F}\) such that any object \(X\) of \(C_{/F}\) is increased by an object \(X_i\), i.e. admits a \(F\)-morphism \(X_i \to X\).

(iv) (If category \(C\) is equivalent to a small category.) Category \(C_{/F}\) is filtering.

(v) (If the category \(C\) is equivalent to a small category, and if the inductive limits filters are representable there.) The functor \(F\) is left exact.

Demonstration. (i) ⟹ (ii). Suppose \(F\) ind-represented by \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), with \(I\) small. Let us prove that \(C_{/F}\) is filtering. Let \(X, X'\) be two objects of \(C_{/F}\), i.e. objects of \(C\) provided with morphisms \(X \to F\), \(X' \to F\), we prove that they are augmented by a third object from \(C_{/F}\). But the morphisms \(X \to F\), \(X' \to F\) come from morphisms \(X \to X_i\), \(X' \to X'_i\), and even if it means replacing \(i, i'\) by a common upper bound in \(\mathrm{Ob}\, I\), we can assume \(i = i'\), and we take as common upper bound of \(X, X'\) the object \(X_i\) with the morphism canonical \(X_i \to F\). Now let \(X \underset{g}{\overset{f}{\rightrightarrows}} X' \xrightarrow{\ h\ } F\) be a double arrow in \(C_{/F}\), let us prove that it is equalized by an arrow \(X' \to X''\) of \(C_{/F}\). But \(h : X' \to F\) is given by a morphism \(h_i : X' \to X_i\), and the condition \(hf = hg\) means that it exists \(\alpha : i \to j\) in \(I\) such as \(\varphi(\alpha)(h_i f) = \varphi(\alpha)(h_i g)\), i.e. even if it means replacing \(h_i\) by \(\varphi(\alpha)h_i\), we equalize \(f\) and \(g\). This proves that \(C_{/F}\) is filtering. It is then immediate that it is essentially small since the objects \((X_i \to F)_{i\in\mathrm{Ob}\, I}\) form a small family in \(\mathrm{Ob}\, C_{/F}\) which is cofinal.

(ii) ⟹ (i). Let us set \(I = C_{/F}\), and consider the canonical functor (8.3.2.2) \[ \varphi : I = C_{/F} \to C . \] We know that the pre-beam represented by this ind-object of \(C\) is \(F\) (3.4), therefore \(F\) is ind-representable by definition (8.2.2).

(ii) ⟺ (iii). Indeed, (ii) ⟹ (iii) because an ind-representable functor is left exact (8.3.1), and (iii) ⟹ (ii) because we pointed out (8.3.1), that \(F\) exact on the left implies that \(C_{/F}\) is filtering, and we apply definition 8.1.8 to conclude that this category is essentially small.
(ii) ⟺ (iii bis) is proven in the same way as (ii) ⟺ (iii).

The equivalences (ii) ⟺ (iv) and (iii) ⟺ (v) are trivial, since for \(C\) equivalent to a small category, \(C_{/F}\) is obviously also equivalent to a small category and has a fortiori it is automatically essentially small as soon as it is filtering. This completes the demonstration of 8.3.3.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.3.4

Let \(F : C' \to C''\) be a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. It appears that the notion of left exactness (2.3.2) is of little interest in practice unless in \(C'\) finite projective limits are representable. In the case where \(C'' = (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\), and where \(C'\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small, writing \(C'\) in the form \(C^\circ\) (hence \(C = C'^\circ\)), it should be considered that the “good notion” which replaces, in the general case, that of exactness on the left is that of ind-representability of \(F\) (considered as pre-sheaf on \(C^\circ\); i.e. that of pro-representability of \(F\), considered as a functor on \(C'\), cf. 8.10). This notion coincides well with that of exactness on the left when in \(C'\) the finite projective limits are representable i.e. in \(C\) the finite inductive limits are representable (8.3.3). When we no longer assume \(C'\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small or at least equivalent to a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category, then the two notions for a functor \(F\), left exactness and pro-representability, no longer necessarily coincide, even if in \(C'\) the finite projective limits are representable (see 8.12.9). In fact, it seems that in this case the exact functors on the left non-indispensable must be considered as being of a pathological nature, the “good” objects remaining the unrepresentable functors; compare 8.13.3. For the case of functors \(F : C' \to C''\), with \(C'\) and \(C''\) again arbitrary \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, we We will develop below (8.11.5), more generally, a notion which “improves” that of exactness on the left, (namely that of a functor admitting a pro-adjoint functor).
8.4. Ind-constant objects, essentially constant

Let's choose a final category \(\underline{e}\), i.e. such that \(\mathrm{Ob}\, \underline{e}\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}\, \underline{e}\) are reduced to one element (for example, we can take for \(\underline{e}\) the full subcategory of \((\mathrm{Ens})\) formed by the empty set, if we want a canonical choice). This is obviously a filtering category and small. If \(X\) is an object of \(C\), we associate with it the constant ind-object indexed by \(\underline{e}\), with value \(X\), or \(c(X)\). It is clear that for \(X\) variable, we thus find a fully faithful functor \[ c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) , \tag{8.4.1} \] moreover injective on objects, and by which we will identify \(C\) to a subcategory full of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). Note that the compound functor \[ C \xrightarrow{\ c\ } \mathrm{Ind}(C) \xrightarrow{\ L\ } \widehat{C} \tag{8.4.2} \] is the canonical functor \(h\) (8.2.1.1).

More generally, let \(I\) be a filtering category. The constant functor on \(I\) of value on an object \(X\) of \(C\) is also called theind-object constant of value \(X\) indexed by \(I\). It is clear, if \(I\) is essentially small, that this ind-object ind-represents the functor \(h(X)\) represented by \(X\), so this ind-object is isomorphic to \(c(X)\).

An ind-object \(\underline{X}\) of \(C\) is said to be essentially constant if it is isomorphic (in a category \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{V}')\), for suitable \(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{V}'\)) to an ind-object of the form \(c(X)\), \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). The object \(X\), which is then determined up to canonical isomorphism, is called the value of the essentially constant ind-object considered. So by definition, the functor \(c\) of (8.4.1) induces an equivalence of \(C\) with the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) formed of ind-objects which are essentially constant, this subcategory being the essential image of the functor \(c\).
Obviously a constant ind-object is essentially constant, the reverse being true only in the only, trivial, case where \(C\) is empty or a one-off category.
8.5. Filtering inductive limits in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\)

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.5.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. In \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), the small limits filtering inductives are representable, and the canonical functor (8.2.4.8) \[ L : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \widehat{C} \] y commutes.
Given that \(L\) is fully faithful, the assertions in the proposition equivalent to the following:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.2

Very small filtering inductive limit (in \(\widehat{C}\)) of pre-beams ind-representables is ind-representable.
This easily follows from criterion 8.3.3 (ii). The details of the verification are left to reader.

The “tautological” case of filtering inductive limits in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is the one where we start from an essentially small ind-object \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(C\). We then have, in \(\widehat{C}\) so also in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) (or in \(\widehat{\widehat{C}}\)): \[ \underline{X} = \varinjlim_I X_i . \tag{8.5.3.1} \] We will pay attention, when writing this formula, that it is not an inductive limit in \(C\), and that even when the latter exists, it is not isomorphic in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) to \(\underline{X}\): indeed, the canonical functor (8.4.1) \(c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) does not commute in general to filtering inductive limits. (See 8.5.5 below.)

To obviate this possibility of confusion in the writing of (8.5.3.1), "some authors” (= P. Deligne) prefer to write it \[ \underline{X} = \text{«}\varinjlim_I\text{»}\, X_i , \tag{8.5.3.2} \] the role of the quotation marks being to indicate that the inductive limit is taken from a category from Ind-objects \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). By extension, it would then be appropriate to denote by “\(\varinjlim\)” any inductive limit operation in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) (without the components of the inductive system envisaged in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) are necessarily in the image, or the essential image, of \(c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\)).

To calculate the inductive or projective limit of a functor \[ \varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) , \tag{8.5.4.1} \] where \(J\) is now any category, it is often convenient to explain \(\varphi\) to using a functor \[ \widehat{\varphi} : J\times I \to C , \tag{8.5.4.2} \] where \(I\) is an essentially small, or preferably small, filter category. Such a \(\widehat{\varphi}\) indeed defines a functor \[ j \mapsto (i \mapsto \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)) : J \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I,C) , \] hence, by composing with the canonical arrow (8.2.6.1) \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I,C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\), a functor \[ j \mapsto (i \mapsto \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)) : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) . \tag{8.5.4.3} \] We can say that \(\widehat{\varphi}\) is an index expression of \(\varphi\), indexed by the category (filtering) \(I\), if the corresponding functor (8.5.4.3) is isomorphic to \(\varphi\). We will study below (8.8) general conditions for the existence of an index expression for a functor \(\varphi\) given, and we limit here from a functor \(\varphi\) given in the form index (8.5.4.3), in the case where \(J\) is a small filtering category, to indicate in this case the calculation of “\(\varinjlim\)” \(\varphi = \text{«}\varinjlim_j\text{»} \varphi(j)\). The formula (8.5.3.2), and the formula for associativity of inductive limits (2.5.0) then immediately gives the “tautological calculation” of “\(\varinjlim\)” \(\varphi\): \[ \text{«}\varinjlim_J\text{»} \varphi = \text{«}\varinjlim_{J\times I}\text{»} \widehat{\varphi} , \tag{8.5.4.4} \] i.e. \[ \text{«}\varinjlim_J\text{»} (i \mapsto \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)) = \text{«}\varinjlim_{J\times I}\text{»} \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) . \tag{8.5.4.5} \] Thus, the inductive system sought is none other than \(\widehat{\varphi}\) itself (the category of indices being \(J\times I\), which is well filtered since \(J\) and \(I\) are).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.5.5

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category

a) Prove that the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) In \(C\) the small inductive limits are representable, and the functor \(c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) switches there.

(iii) The functor \(c\) is an equivalence of categories.

(ii) In \(C\) small inductive limits are representable, and for all \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), the functor \(Y \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) commutes at said limits.

(iv) The small filtering inductive limits are representable in \(C\), and the functor \(\varinjlim c : \mathrm{Ind}\, C \to C\) (see 8.7.1.5) is fully faithful (or even an equivalence).

b) If \(C\) is a finite category, prove that the preceding conditions are equivalent to the following: for any projector in \(C\) (10.6), the image is representable in \(C\).
8.6. Extending a functor to ind-objects

Either \[ f : C \to C' \tag{8.6.1.1} \] a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. For any ind-object \[ \varphi : I \to C \] of \(C\) (\(I\) a filtering category), the compound functor \(f\circ\varphi\) is an ind-object of \(C'\), also noted \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)(\varphi)\). In index notations, if \(\varphi\) is written in the form \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\), we obtain \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f)(X_i)_{i\in I} = (f(X_i))_{i\in I} . \tag{8.6.1.2} \] Let \(\underline{Y} = (Y_j)_{j\in J}\) be a second inductive system of \(C\). We then find an application obvious, also noted \(u \mapsto \mathrm{Ind}(f)(u)\): \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) = \varprojlim_i \varinjlim_j \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Y_j) &\to \\ \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{Ind}(f)(\underline{X}), \mathrm{Ind}(f)(\underline{Y})) &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_i \varinjlim_j \mathrm{Hom}(f(X_i),f(Y_j)) . \end{aligned} \tag{8.6.1.3} \] We immediately see that these applications are compatible with the composition of the morphisms of ind-objects, referring to the unwritten formula for composition of morphisms of ind-objects (8.2.5). We thus obtained, for any universe \(\mathcal{V}\), a functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \to \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C',\mathcal{U}) \tag{8.6.1.4} \] (notations of (8.2.4.5)), and in particular a functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C') . \tag{8.6.1.5} \]

If we have a second functor \[ g : C' \to C'' , \] we obviously have an identity of functors between Ind categories (or \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}\), as desired): \[ \mathrm{Ind}(gf) = \mathrm{Ind}(g)\circ\mathrm{Ind}(f) , \tag{8.6.1.6} \] and for \(C' = C\), we have the eternal formula \[ \mathrm{Ind}(\mathrm{id}_C) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C)} . \tag{8.6.1.7} \] Pictorially, we can therefore say that the category \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) functionally depends on \(C\) (covariantly)∗. We leave it to the reader to explain even a 2-functional dependence, by defining, for any pair of \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories \(C, C'\), a canonical functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,C') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Ind}(C), \mathrm{Ind}(C')) , \tag{8.6.1.8} \] and by specifying the functorial nature of the identical isomorphisms (8.6.1.6) and (8.6.1.7).

Let's take the functor (8.6.1.1), and consider the corresponding functor (5.1) \[ f_! : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C'} , \tag{8.6.2.1} \] and the functor diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Ind}(C) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{Ind}(f)} \ar[d]_-{L_C} & & \mathrm{Ind}(C') \ar[d]^-{L_{C'}} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[rr]_-{f_!} & & \widehat{C'} } \tag{8.6.2.2} \] where the vertical arrows denote the canonical functors \(L\) of (8.2.4.8). Like the functor \(f_!\) “extends \(f\)” and commutes to inductive limits (5.4.3), and that functors \(L_C\) and \(L_{C'}\) commute at small filtering inductive limits (8.5.1), it results from (8.5.3.2) that for any ind-object \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(C\), we have in \(\widehat{C'}\) an isomorphism canonical \[ f_!(L_C((X_i)_{i\in I})) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_i f_! L_C(X_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_i L_{C'} f(X_i) = L_{C'}(f(X_i)_{i\in I}) \] i.e. a canonical isomorphism \[ f_! L_C(\underline{X}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } L_{C'}(\mathrm{Ind}(f)(\underline{X})) . \] We leave it to the reader to verify that the latter is functional, i.e. that it corresponds to a canonical isomorphism \[ f_! L_C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } L_{C'}\circ\mathrm{Ind}(f) . \tag{8.6.2.3} \] In other words, the square (8.6.2.2) is commutative up to canonical isomorphism. Taking into account the fact that \(f_!\) commutes at small inductive limits, and 8.5.1, we conclude from this:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.6.3

Let \(f : C \to C'\) be a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. Then the functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C') \] switches to filtering inductive limits. Furthermore, it makes the following diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[rr]^-{f} \ar[d]_-{c_C} & & C' \ar[d]^-{c_{C'}} \\ \mathrm{Ind}(C) \ar[rr]_-{\mathrm{Ind}(f)} & & \mathrm{Ind}(C') } \] where the vertical arrows \(c_C, c_{C'}\) are the canonical functors (8.4.1).

The last assertion is trivial about definitions, and was put for the convenience of references. Note also that the properties stated in 8.6.3 characterize the functor \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) up to a single isomorphism, as being induced by the functor \(f_!\) (8.6.2.1), as it results from the demonstration that we have just given of (8.6.2.3).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.6.4

The notations are those of 8.6.3.

a) For \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) to be faithful (resp. fully faithful), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) the either.

b) For \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) to be an equivalence of categories, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) be fully faithful, and that any object of \(C'\) is isomorphic to a direct factor (10.6) of a object in the image of \(f\).

Demonstration. a) The necessity obviously results from the fact that \(f\) is induced by \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\). For sufficiency, just use the form (8.6.1.3) of \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) on sets \(\mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y})\), remembering that the filtering inductive limits of sets, and the limits any projectives, transform monomorphisms into monomorphisms, isomorphisms in isomorphisms.

b) We can already assume \(f\) therefore \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) fully faithful. Like any object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C')\) is a small filtering inductive limit of objects of \(C'\), the full fidelity of \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) implies that for this functor to be essentially surjective, it amounts to the same as all object \(X'\) of \(C'\) is in the essential image. But if we have an isomorphism \[ X' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim\text{»} f(X_i) , \] this isomorphism is factorized by one of \(f(X_i)\), which shows that \(X'\) is a direct factor of \(f(X_i)\), which proves the necessity in b). For sufficiency, using full faithfulness of \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\), it immediately results from

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.6.5

In \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) projector images (10.6) are representable, and the functor \(\mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C')\) switches to the formation of said images.

This in fact results from the fact that in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) the small filtering inductive limits are representable (8.5.1) and that \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) commutes there (8.6.3), given that the image of a projector \(p : X \to X\) is interpreted as the inductive limit of an inductive filtering system indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) \[ X \xrightarrow{\ p\ } X \xrightarrow{\ p\ } X \to \ldots \] or as desired, as an inductive limit of the functor that we guess on the filtering category \(P\) having a single object, and a non-identical arrow \(\pi\) such as \(\pi^2 = \pi\).
8.7. The functor \(\varinjlim_C : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to C\). Universal characterizations of category \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

Let's take a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\), and the canonical functor \[ c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) . \tag{8.7.1.1} \] Let \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). It immediately follows from the definition of representable inductive limits (2.1, 2.1.1) that \(\varinjlim_i X_i\) is representable in \(C\) if and only if the adjoint to the left of \(c\) is defined in \(\underline{X}\), and that in the case \(\varinjlim_i X_i\) (calculated in \(C\)) is none other than the value in \(\underline{X}\) of said left adjoint: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_C(\varinjlim_i X_i, Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C)}((X_i)_{i\in I}, c(Y)) . \tag{8.7.1.2} \]

If we designate by \[ \mathrm{Ind}(C)' \subset \mathrm{Ind}(C) \tag{8.7.1.3} \] the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) formed of the ind-objects of \(C\) which admit a limit inductive in \(C\), it follows from the previous observation that this subcategory is strictly full, and that we have a canonical functor \[ \varinjlim_C : \mathrm{Ind}(C)' \to C , \tag{8.7.1.4} \] whose value in each object \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)'\) is its inductive limit in \(C\). In the particular case where in \(C\) the small filtering inductive limits are representable, we therefore obtains a natural functor \[ \varinjlim_C : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to C . \tag{8.7.1.5} \]

Of course, we can also define the previous functors on categories of type \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U})'\), \(\mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U})\), but, taking into account (8.2.4.6), they are already determined (up to a single isomorphism) by the knowledge of the previous functors, corresponding to the case \(\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{U}\).

From the previous construction of \(\varinjlim_C\) as a left adjoint functor, it immediately results that this functor commutes to any inductive limits, and in particular to small filtering inductive limits (the latter being representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) (8.5.1)). Note also that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)'\) always contains the essential image of \(c\) (formed by essentially constant ind-objects), and that we have a canonical isomorphism functorial in \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Ind}(C)'\): \[ \varinjlim_C c(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X , \tag{8.7.1.8} \] i.e., in the favorable case where \(C\) is stable by small inductive limits, we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \varinjlim_C \circ\, c \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{id}_C . \tag{8.7.1.9} \]

Now consider a functor \[ f : C \to E , \tag{8.7.2.1} \] where \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where small inductive limits are representable, so that we have a functor \[ \varinjlim_E : \mathrm{Ind}(E) \to E . \] As we also defined (8.6) \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(E) , \] we can consider the compound \[ \overline{f} = \varinjlim_E \circ\, \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E , \tag{8.7.2.2} \] which is sometimes called the canonical extension of \(f\) to ind-objects (but we will not be confused with \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\)!). As composed of two functors commuting to small filtering inductive limits (8.6.3, 8.7.1.7), this functor itself commutes to small filtering inductive limits. Moreover, it results from the isomorphism (8.7.1.9) applied to \(E\), and of (8.6.2.3), that \(\overline{f}\) extends \(f\) up to isomorphism, i.e. we have an isomorphism canonical \[ \overline{f} \circ c_C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f . \tag{8.7.2.3} \]

It is quite clear by the way, taking into account the fact that any object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is small filtering inductive limit of objects of \(C\), which the two previous properties characterize again \(\overline{f}\), up to a single isomorphism. We can specify this point to obtain at the same time time a universal characterization, up to equivalence, of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) among the \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories \(E\) where the small filtering inductive limits are representable. If \(E\) and \(F\) are two such \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, let's denote by \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)' \subset \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F) \tag{8.7.2.4} \] the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\) formed of functors which commute to small filtering inductive limits. We then have:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.7.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and use the notation above (8.7.2.4). Then the canonical functor \[ c_C : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) \] is 2-universal among source \(C\) and goal functors a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category with small limits representable filtering inductive forces. In other words, \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is such a category (8.2.5, 8.5.1), and if \(E\) is such a category, the functor \[ g \mapsto g\circ c_C : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Ind}(C),E)' \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,E) \tag{8.7.3.1} \] is an equivalence of categories.

To prove this last point, simply check that the application \(f \mapsto \overline{f}\) defined by (8.7.2.2) can be specified by a functor \[ f \mapsto \overline{f} = \varinjlim_E \circ\, \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,E) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Ind}(C),E)' , \tag{8.7.3.2} \] and that the latter is quasi-inverse of (8.7.3.1). The details of the verification, essentially trivial, is left to the reader. We can also invoke 7.8 to first conclude that (8.7.3.1) is fully faithful, and (8.7.2.3) to conclude that it is essentially surjective.

Let's take a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories \[ f : C \to E , \tag{8.7.4.1} \] where \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where small filtering inductive limits are representable, hence a functor \[ \overline{f} : (X_i)_{i\in I} \mapsto \varinjlim_i f(X_i) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E . \tag{8.7.4.2} \] We propose to study conditions on \(f\) which ensure that \(\overline{f}\) is fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories. As \(f\) is isomorphic to the compound \(\overline{f}\circ c_C\), and that \(c_C : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is fully faithful, we see that for \(\overline{f}\) to be fully faithful, it must \(f\) be. Even if it means replacing \(C\) by its essential image in \(E\), we therefore see that we do not lose in general, essentially, assuming that \(f\) is the inclusion functor of a subcategory \(C\) of \(E\), which we will assume subsequently, to simplify the ratings.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.7.5

The notations are those of 8.7.4.

a) For the functor \(\overline{f}\) to be fully faithful, it is necessary and sufficient that we have:

(i) Any object \(X\) of \(C\) satisfies the following condition:

(PF) For any small inductive filtering system \((Y_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(C\), with inductive limit \(\varinjlim_i Y_i\) in \(E\), the canonical application \[ \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X, \varinjlim_i Y_i) \tag{8.7.5.1} \] is bijective.

b) For the functor \(\overline{f}\) to be an equivalent of categories, it is necessary and sufficient that \(C\) satisfies condition (i), and the following two conditions:

(ii) \(C\) is a subcategory of \(E\) generating by strict epimorphisms (7.1).

(iii) For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the full subcategory \(C_{/X}\) of \(E_{/X}\) formed of objects \(X'\) above source \(X\) in \(\mathrm{Ob}\, C\), is filtering and essentially small.

Condition (iii) is verified in particular if \(C\) is equivalent to a small category, and if the finite inductive limits in \(C\) are representable and the inclusion functor \(f : C \to E\) commutes there (i.e. for any finite category \(J\) and any functor \(\varphi : J \to C\) the inductive limit of \(f\varphi\) is representable and is isomorphic in \(E\) to an object of \(C\)).

Demonstration. a) With the notations of condition (i), if \(\underline{Y}\) denotes the ind-object \((Y_i)\), \(\underline{X}\) the constant ind-object defined by \(X\), then (8.7.5.1) is none other than the application canonical \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\overline{f}(\underline{X}),\overline{f}(\underline{Y})) , \] therefore if \(\overline{f}\) is fully faithful this application is indeed bijective. Conversely, if \(\underline{X} = (X_j)_{j\in J}\) and \(\underline{Y} = (Y_i)_{i\in I}\) are any two ind-objects of \(E\), then the application \(u : \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\overline{f}(\underline{X}),\overline{f}(\underline{Y}))\) is the projective limit on \(j\) of apps \[ u_j : \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X}_j,\underline{Y}) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\overline{f}(\underline{X}_j),\overline{f}(\underline{Y})) , \] where \(\underline{X}_j\) is the ind-object constant with value \(X_j\); so \(u\) is bijective if the \(u_j\) are, and (i) therefore implies that \(\overline{f}\) is fully faithful.

b) Let (i), (ii), (iii) hold, and prove that \(\overline{f}\) is an equivalence. It remains to prove that it is essentially surjective, so that everything \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) is in the image essential. Now hypothesis (ii) means that \(\varinjlim_{C_{/X}} Z \to X\) is an isomorphism, and (iii) that the category \(C_{/X}\) is filtering and essentially small, so \(X\) is the image of the ind-object of \(E\) defined by the natural functor \(C_{/X} \to E\). Conversely, suppose that \(\overline{f}\) is an equivalence, so by virtue of a), it remains to verify (ii) and (iii). It can be assumed that \(E = \mathrm{Ind}(C)\), \(C\) being identified with the subcategory \(c(C)\) of \(E\). But we know (8.3.3 (ii)) that for all \(X \in \mathrm{Ind}(C)\), identified if desired with the functor \(F\) on \(C^\circ\) that it ind-represents, \(C_{/X} = C_{/F}\) is an essentially small filtering category (which proves (iii)) and that the inductive limit in \(\widehat{C}\) of the functor \(C_{/F} \to \widehat{C}\) is \(F\). A fortiori, it is thus in the full subcategory \(E\) of \(\widehat{C}\), since \(F = X\) is in \(E\), which proves (ii). It remains to prove the last assertion of b). Now the hypothesis made on \(C\) obviously implies that in the category \(C_{/X}\), the finite inductive limits are representable, a fortiori the category \(C_{/X}\) is filtering.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.7.5.2

The reasoning we have just given shows more generally that when condition (i) is verified, then the essential image of the fully faithful functor \(\overline{f}\) (8.7.4.2) is formed from \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) such that the category \(C_{/X}\) is filtering and essentially small (condition automatically satisfied if \(C\) satisfies the conditions stated at the end of b)), and that the inductive limit of the canonical functor \(C_{/X} \to E\) is \(X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.6

Let \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) be the full subcategory of \(E\) formed by objects satisfying condition (PF) of 8.7.5 a). Then for any functor \(J \to E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), \(J\) a finite category, which admits an inductive limit \(X\) in \(E\), we have \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E_{\mathrm{PF}}\). The functor canonical \((X_i)_{i\in I} \mapsto \varinjlim_{i,\,E} X_i\) inferred from inclusion \(g : E_{\mathrm{PF}} \hookrightarrow E\) \[ \overline{g} : \mathrm{Ind}(E_{\mathrm{PF}}) \to E \tag{8.7.6.1} \] is fully faithful, and if in \(E\) the finite inductive limits are representable and if \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is equivalent to a small category, then the essential image of the functor \(\overline{g}\) is formed from the objects \(X\) of \(E\) such that the canonical morphism \(\varinjlim_{(E_{\mathrm{PF}})_{/X}} Y \to X\) is an isomorphism. If we further assume that the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) of \(E\) is generating by strict epimorphisms (7.1), then the functor (8.7.6.1) is an equivalence of categories.
All facts are obvious, in the order they are given, taking into account 8.7.5, in using 2.8 for the first assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.7

Suppose that in \(E\) the finite inductive limits are representable. Let \(C\) be a full subcategory of \(E\), equivalent to a small category, and consider the functor \((X_i)_{i\in I} \mapsto \varinjlim_{i,\,E} X_i\): \[ \overline{f} : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E . \tag{8.7.7.1} \] Let \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) as in 8.7.6. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The functor \(\overline{f} : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E\) is an equivalence.

(ii) The category \(C\) in \(E\) is generator by strict epimorphisms, contained in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and any object of \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is isomorphic in \(E\) (or in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), this amounts to same (8.7.6)) to a direct factor of an object of \(C\).

When the subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) is stable by direct factors (10.6), the conditions previous ones still equivalent to the following ones:

(ii bis) \(C = E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and \(C\) is generator in \(E\) by strict epimorphism.

(iii) The subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) is generator by strict epimorphisms, contained in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and the finite inductive limits are representable there.

When moreover in \(E\) the finite inductive limits are representable, these conditions also equivalent to

(iii bis) The subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) is generator by strict epimorphisms, contained in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and stable in \(E\) by finite inductive limits (or what amounts to the same thing, by are finite, and by cokernels of double arrows).

We already know (8.7.5) that condition (i) implies that \(C \subset E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and that \(C\) is generator by strict epimorphisms; we also prove that then any object \(X\) of \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is isomorphic to a direct factor of an object of \(C\). Indeed we know that \(X\) is a limit inductive filtering \(\varinjlim_i X_i\) in \(E\) of objects of \(C\), and by definition of \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), we see that for suitable \(i\) the canonical homomorphism \(X_i \to X\) admits a left inverse, of so that \(X\) is indeed a direct factor of the object \(X_i\) of \(C\). This proves that (i) ⟹ (ii) (without hypothesis about \(C\) nor \(E\), for that matter). Let us prove (ii) ⟹ (i). As \(C\) is equivalent to a small category and every object of \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is a direct factor of an object of \(C\), we see that \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is also equivalent to a small category, so under 8.7.6 the functor (8.7.6.1) is an equivalence of categories, and we conclude thanks to 8.6.4 b) applied to the inclusion \(C \hookrightarrow E_{\mathrm{PF}}\).

When any direct factor in \(E\) of an object of \(C\) is in \(C\), it is clear that (ii) ⟺ (ii bis). On the other hand (ii bis) implies (iii) resp. (iii bis) under 8.7.6. Finally, (iii) (and a fortiori (iii bis)) implies (i) under 8.7.5. This completes the demonstration.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.7.8

(Karoubi envelopes). Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, \(E = \mathrm{Ind}(C)\), and \(E_{\mathrm{PF}} \supset C\) the full subcategory of \(E\) defined in 8.7.6.

a) Prove that in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) the projector images are representable, and that all object of \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is direct factor of an object of \(C\).

b) Let's call karoubienne a category \(F\) in which the projector images are representable. Either then \[ \varphi : C \to K \] a fully faithful functor, such that \(K\) is Karoubian and any object of \(K\) is direct factor of an object in the image of \(C\). Prove that \(\varphi\) is 2-universal among functors of \(C\) with values in Karoubian categories, precisely: for any karoubian category \(F\), the functor \[ g \mapsto g\circ\varphi : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(K,F) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F) \] is an equivalence of categories. (We will use the fact that any functor commutes to images of projectors.) The category \(K\) provided with \(\varphi\), determined to the nearest equivalence (itself determined up to a single isomorphism) by the previous properties, is called the Karoubi envelope \(\widetilde{C}\) of \(C\). (Compare also IV 7.5.)

c) Deduce from a) and b) that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)_{\mathrm{PF}}\), provided with the functor \(C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)_{\mathrm{PF}}\) induced by \(c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\), makes \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)_{\mathrm{PF}}\) a Karoubi husk of \(C\).

d) Show that any functor \(f : C \to C'\) extends in an essentially unique way in a functor \(\widetilde{f} : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C'}\) of Karoubi envelopes, and that if we take these envelopes of Karoubi as in c), \(\widetilde{f}\) is the functor induced by \(\mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C')\). Show that the conditions of 8.6.4 b) are still equivalent to the following: \(\widetilde{f}\) is a equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.7.9

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

a) Show that the conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(E\) is equivalent to a category of the form \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), with \(C\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

(i bis) Like (i), with additional \(C\) karoubian (8.7.8 b)).
