(ii) The subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) of \(E\) (8.7.6) is generator by strict epimorphisms, and for any object \(X\) of \(E\), \((E_{\mathrm{PF}})_{/X}\) is an essentially small filter category.

Show that if \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category karoubian such that \(E\) is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), then \(C\) is equivalent to \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) (by an equivalence determined by isomorphism close to only one). Establish a 2-equivalence between the 2-category formed by the categories \(E\) satisfying the previous conditions and the functors between themcommuting to small inductive filtering limits, and the 2-category formed of \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories karoubian and the any functors between them.

b) Show that the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(E\) is equivalent to a category of the form \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where finite inductive limits are representable.

(i bis) Like (i), but with additional \(C\) karoubian.

(ii) The finite inductive limits in \(E\) are representable, the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) of \(E\) generating by strict epimorphisms, and for any object \(X\) of \(E\), there exists a small part \(I\) of \(\mathrm{Ob}\,(E_{\mathrm{PF}})_{/X}\) such that any object of \((E_{\mathrm{PF}})_{/X}\) is increased by an object of \(I\). (Use 8.9.5 b.)
8.8. Index representation of a functor \(J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\)

Either \[ \varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) \quad \text{i.e.} \quad \varphi \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C)) \] a functor, \(C\) being a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. Recall (8.5.4) that anindex representation of \(\varphi\) is by definition an ind-object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\) \[ \widehat{\varphi} : I \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C) , \] \(I\) essentially small filter category, whose inductive limit in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\) is isomorphic to \(\varphi\) by a given isomorphism. So \(\varphi\) admits a indexical representation if and only if it is isomorphic to a filtering inductive limit essentially small in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\) of objects of the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\). We will say that the category \(J\) is admissible for \(C\) if any functor \(\varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) admits an index representation.

As in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) the small filtering inductive limits are representable (8.5.1), it is the same in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\), and they are calculated "argument by argument". Consequently, the inclusion functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C) \hookrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C)) \tag{8.8.1.1} \] extends canonically into a functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C)) \tag{8.8.1.2} \] (8.7.2). The elements of the essential image of this functor are then precisely the \(\varphi\) admitting an indexical representation; so saying that \(J\) is admissible for \(C\) means also that the functor (8.8.1.2) is essentially surjective. Let us note in this regard:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.8.2

If the category \(J\) is equivalent to a finite category, the functor canonical (8.8.1.2) is fully faithful; therefore \(J\) is admissible for \(C\) if and only if it is an equivalence of categories.

Under 8.7.5 a) everything comes down to proving that for any small inductive filtering system \((\varphi_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\) and any object \(\varphi\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\), the canonical application \[ \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi,\varphi_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi, \varinjlim_i \varphi_i) \tag{8.8.2.1} \] is bijective, where \(\varinjlim_i \varphi_i\) denotes the inductive limit taken in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\). However, if \(\varphi, \psi\) are two functors \(J \to C\), we have an exact diagram of sets, functorial in \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi,\psi) \to \prod_{X\in\mathrm{Ob}\, J} \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X),\psi(X)) \mathrel{\substack{f\in\mathrm{Fl}\, J \\ \textstyle\rightrightarrows \\ f:X\to Y}} \prod \mathrm{Hom}(\varphi(X),\psi(Y)) . \] As the filtering inductive limits commute to the double arrow cores and to the finite products, the bijectivity of (8.8.2.1) follows when \(J\) is finite. The case where \(J\) is equivalent to a finite category immediately returns to the previous case.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.8.3

Let \(\varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) be a functor, with \(J\) equivalent to a small category. For \(\varphi\) to admit an index representation, it is sufficient for it to satisfy the following two conditions, and this is also necessary when \(J\) is equivalent to a finished category:

a) The category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi}\) (made up of the arrows of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\) of goal \(\varphi\) and source in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\)) is filtering.

b) For any object \(j\) of \(J\), the functor \[ \psi \mapsto \psi(j) : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi} \to C_{/\varphi(j)} \tag{8.8.3.1} \] is cofinal, i.e. satisfying the conditions F 1), F 2) of 8.1.3.

Demonstration. Suppose a) and b) are verified, we prove that \(\varphi\) admits a representation index. We can obviously assume \(J\) small. Either \[ I = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi} , \tag{8.8.3.2} \] which is a filtering category by hypothesis, and first assume \(I\) essentially small, so that "including" \(I\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\) defines an ind-object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\), \(i \mapsto \psi_i\). Furthermore, we have a canonical homomorphism \[ \varinjlim_i \psi_i \to \varphi , \tag{8.8.3.3} \] given argument by argument by homomorphism \[ \varinjlim_I \psi_i(j) \to \varphi(j) = \varinjlim_{C_{/\varphi(j)}} X \] deduced from the functor (8.8.3.1). As last is cofinal, we conclude that (8.8.3.3) is a isomorphism, hence the conclusion. In the case where we do not assume \(I\) essentially small, just construct an essentially small full subcategory \(I'\), such that (8.8.3.3) remains an isomorphism by taking \(\varinjlim_{I'}\) instead of \(\varinjlim_I\). For this, it is enough that the compound functors \[ I' \to I \to C_{/\varphi(j)} \] induced by the functors (8.8.3.1) are all cofinal. We then conclude with the following lemma, the demonstration of which is immediate thanks to criterion (8.1.3 b)), and is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.8.3.4

Let \(I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering category, and \[ f_j : I \to I_j \quad (j \in J) \] a small family of cofinal functors of \(I\) in essentially filtering categories small. Then there exists a full subcategory \(I'\) of \(I\) which is filtering and essentially small, and such that the functors induced by the \(f_j\) are cofinal.

Let's finally prove the necessity in 8.8.3 when we assume \(J\) equivalent to a category finished. An index representation of \(\varphi\) using a filtering index category essentially small \(I\) defines a functor \(\psi\) \[ \xymatrix{ I \ar[rr]^-{\psi} \ar@{-->}[dr]_-{\psi_j} & & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi} \ar@{-->}[d] \\ & & C_{/\varphi(j)} } \tag{8.8.3.5} \] and it suffices to prove that \(\psi\) and each of the functors \(\psi_j\) which is deduced from it are cofinal: by virtue of 8.1.3 b) it will indeed follow that \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi}\) is filtering, and by definition 8.1.1 that the vertical arrow of (8.8.3.5) is also cofinal. Under 8.8.2 one can identify \(\varphi\) to an object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(E)\), where \(E = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\), and the fact that the functor \[ \psi : I \to E_{/\varphi} , \] deduced from the ind-object \(\varphi\) of \(E\) indexed by \(I\), is cofinal is a general fact, which is verified immediately using criteria F 1) and F 2) of 8.1.3. The same reason (where \(E, \varphi\) are replaced by \(C, \varphi(j)\)) shows that \(\psi_j\) is cofinal, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.8.4

a) In the case where \(J\) is equivalent to a finite category, if \(\varphi\) admits a index representation, we saw that (8.8.3.5) is a cofinal functor, which implies that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi}\) is itself essentially small.

b) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite inductive limits are representable. So he is the same in \(E = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)\), and these are calculated argument by argument, and this are also inductive limits in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\widehat{C})\) and a fortiori in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,\mathrm{Ind}(C))\). It immediately follows that condition a) of 8.8.3 is then automatically satisfied, and it all comes down to looking at condition b). I don't know if it is automatically satisfied when in addition \(J\) is assumed to be small.
We arrive at the main result of this issue:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.8.5

Let \(J\) be a category equivalent to a finite category, and suppose moreover \(J\) rigid i.e. that for all \(j \in \mathrm{Ob}\, J\), any endomorphism of \(j\) is the identity. Then \(J\) is admissible for \(C\) (whatever the \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\)), i.e. any functor \(J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) admits an index representation.

Even if it means replacing \(J\) by an equivalent category, we can assume that \(J\) is reduced i.e. that two isomorphic objects of \(J\) are identical. Then \(J\) is even finished. We let's proceed by induction on \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Ob}\, J\), the case where this number is \(\leq 0\) being trivial; we will therefore assume \(\geq 1\). Let \(j_0\) be a maximal object of \(J\), i.e. such that for all arrow \(j_0 \to j\), there is arrow \(j \to j_0\); given the fact that \(J\) is rigid, this implies that \(j_0 \to j\) is an isomorphism, and since \(J\) is reduced, this implies \(j_0 = j\). Let therefore be \(J'\) the full subcategory deduced from \(J\) by removing the object \(j_0\), and \(J''\) the full subcategory reduced to object \(j_0\). The proposition then results from

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.8.5.1

Let \(J\) be a finite category, \(J'\) and \(J''\) two full subcategories such that \(\mathrm{Ob}\, J = \mathrm{Ob}\, J' \cup \mathrm{Ob}\, J''\), and that for all \(j' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, J'\) and \(j'' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, J''\), we has \(\mathrm{Hom}(j'',j') = \varnothing\). If \(J'\) and \(J''\) are admissible for \(C\), the same is true for \(J\).

It all comes down to proving criteria a) and b) of 8.8.3, which amounts to doing six elementary checks, namely the last two conditions of the filtering categories for \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi}\), and the conditions F 1) and F 2) for the functors (8.8.3.1), in the case \(j \in \mathrm{Ob}\, J'\) and \(j \in \mathrm{Ob}\, J''\) successively; these latter conditions implying moreover that \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J,C)_{/\varphi}\) is non-empty. The rather tedious verification does not offer any difficulty, and is left to the reader. (The editor searched in vain for an elegant demonstration which would bypass these unpleasant checks.)

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.8.6

a) Let \(J, J'\) be two admissible categories for \(C\), one of them being finite. Prove that \(J\times J'\) is admissible for \(C\). (Use 8.8.2.)

b) Prove that a small discrete category is admissible for any category \(C\).

c) Let \(J\) be a category satisfying the following conditions: (i) \(J\) is rigid, (ii) \(\mathrm{Ob}\, J\) is countable, (iii) For any two objects \(j, j'\) of \(J\), \(\mathrm{Hom}(j,j')\) is finite, (iv) For any object \(j\) of \(J\), the set of objects of \(J\) which are increased by \(j\) i.e. which are source of a goal arrow \(j\), is finished. Show that \(J\) is admissible for all \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\). (First show that we can write \(J\) as a filtering meeting of finite full subcategories \(J_n\), such that any object of \(J\) augmented by an object of \(J_n\) i.e. in \(J_n\). Then check criteria a), b) of 8.8.3.)

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.8.7

We identify in the notations an ordered set and the category it defines.

a) Let \(C\) be an ordered set. Let \(\widetilde{C}\) be the set of parts \(A\) of \(C\) which are filtering and such that \(x \in A\) and \(y \leq x\) implies \(y \in A\). Let \(L_0 : C \to \widetilde{C}\) be the application which associates with all \(x \in C\) the set \(L_0(x)\) of \(y \in C\) such that \(y \leq x\). Show that \(L\) is an injective application and that the order of \(C\) is induced by that of \(\widetilde{C}\). For everything \(A \in \widetilde{C}\), consider the include \(f(A) : A \to C\), it is an ind-object of \(C\); for everything ind-object \(\varphi : I \to C\) of \(C\), i.e. \(g(\varphi) \in \widetilde{C}\) the part of \(C\) formed from the elements of \(C\) increased by an element of the form \(\varphi(i)\). Show that we thus obtain two equivalences almost opposites of each other \[ \mathrm{Ind}(C) \underset{f}{\overset{g}{\rightleftarrows}} \widetilde{C} , \] transforming the functor \(L_0\) into the canonical functor \(L : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

b) Suppose that for all \(x \in C\), the set of upper bound (resp. lower bound) elements \(x\) is finished. Show that every ind-object (resp. pro-object) of \(C\) is essentially constant, and same as for every such \(\varphi : I \to C\) (resp. \(\varphi : I^\circ \to C\)) there exists a \(i_0 \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\) such that the functor induced on \(I_{/i_0}\) is constant (i.e. transforms any arrow into an isomorphism).

c) Let us take \(C \subset \mathbb{N}^\circ \times \mathbb{N}\) formed from pairs of natural integers \((j,i)\) such that \(i \geq j\). Show that \(\widetilde{\mathbb{N}}\) is isomorphic to the ordered set deduced from \(\mathbb{N}\) (identified with a ordered subset of \(\widetilde{\mathbb{N}}\) as in a)) by adding a larger element \(\infty\). Show that \(\widetilde{C}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{N}^\circ \times \widetilde{\mathbb{N}}\). Consider the functor \[ \varphi : \mathbb{N}^\circ \to \widetilde{C} , \quad \varphi(j) = (j,\infty) . \] Show that: 1) the projective limit of \(\varphi\) is not representable in \(\widetilde{C}\), although the filtering projective limits in \(C\) are representable (and are essentially constants, under b)). 2) For any functor \(\psi : \mathbb{N}^\circ \to C\), we have \(\mathrm{Hom}(\psi,\varphi) = \varnothing\), and a fortiori the functor \(\varphi\) does not support representation in index form.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.8.8

Let \(X = \mathbb{N} \sqcup \mathbb{N}\) be the sum set of two copies of \(\mathbb{N}\), \(s\) the symmetry of \(X\), and for all \(i \in \mathbb{N}\), that is \(X_i\) the part \(\Delta_{i+1} \sqcup \Delta_i\) of \(X\) (where \(\Delta_i\) designates the part of \(\mathbb{N}\) formed from \(j\) such as \(j \leq i\)). Let \(C\) be the subcategory of the category of subsets of \(X\), whose objects are \(X_i\), and the arrows are the applications between the \(X_i\) which are induced by the identity of \(X\) or by its symmetry \(s\), and \(\widetilde{C}\) the category defined in an analogous way, but where we also admit the object \(X\).

a) Show that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is equivalent to \(\widetilde{C}\), the canonical functor \(C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) corresponding to inclusion \(C \to \widetilde{C}\). Show that there does not exist a pair \((Y,f)\) of an object \(Y\) of \(C\) and an endomorphism \(f\) of \(Y\), and morphism of objects with endomorphism of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) from \((Y,f)\) in \((X,s)\).

b) Conclude that if \(J\) is the category having a single object, and in addition to the arrow identical a single arrow of order 2, then the functor \(J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) defined by \((X,s)\) does not admit no index representation.
8.9. Correctness properties of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\)

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.9.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

a) The canonical functors \(L\) (8.2.4.8) and \(c\) (8.4.1) \[ C \xrightarrow{\ c\ } \mathrm{Ind}(C) \xrightarrow{\ L\ } \widehat{C} \] commute to projective limits.

b) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite inductive limits are representable, and that \(C\) is equivalent to a small category. Then in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) the small projective limits are representable.

c) If in \(C\) the small projective limits (resp. the finite projective limits) are representable, then it is the same in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). Let \(J\) be a category which is finite and rigid, or discrete; if projective limits of type \(J\) are representable in \(C\), this same type of projective limits is representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

d) The small filtering inductive limits in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) (they are representable in by virtue of 8.5.1) are left exact, i.e. commute to finite projective limits.

Demonstration. a) The fact that \(L\) commutes to projective limits results from the fact that it is fully faithful and the calculation of projective limits in \(\widehat{C}\) "argument by argument", which implies that to check that a projective system of morphisms \(F \to F_i\) (\(i \in I\)) in \(\widehat{C}\) makes \(F\) a projective limit of \(F_i\), it suffices to verify that the analogous assertion is true for projective systems of set applications \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,F) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X,F_i)\), for all \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\). Or \(C \subset \mathrm{Ind}(C)\).
The fact that \(c\) commutes to projective limits results formally from the fact that \(L\) y commutes, as well as the compound \(L\circ c : C \to \widehat{C}\).

b) Taking into account a), the assertion amounts to saying that any projective limit of unrepresentable presheaves is unrepresentable, which immediately follows from the criterion 8.3.3 (v), given that a projective limit of left-exact functors is exact at left (which results from the fact that "the projective limits commute to the limits projectives" (2.5.0)).

c) Formally results from the analogous property of \(\widehat{C}\) (3.3), and from the fact that \(L\) is conservative (being fully faithful) and commutes at the limits of the considered type (a) and 8.5.1).

d) It is well known (cf. 2.3) that the representability of finite projective limits is equivalent to that of the projective limits of types \[ \text{vide}\ , \quad \cdot\quad\cdot\ , \quad \cdot \to \cdot \leftarrow \cdot \] (corresponding to particular finite ordered sets), and that of small limits projectives returns to that of products and finite projective limits. So the second assertion made in c) implies the first.

Let's first assume \(I\) finished. Using result 8.8.5 on the representability of functors \(\varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) in index form (8.5.4) \[ \widehat{\varphi} : J\times I \to C , \tag{8.9.1.1} \] the existence of \(\varprojlim \varphi\) is therefore a special case of the more precise and more precise result general:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.9.2

Consider a functor \(\varphi : J \to \mathrm{Ind}(C)\) given in index form \(\widehat{\varphi}\) (8.9.1.1), \(J\) being a finite category. If for all \(i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\), the partial functor \(j \mapsto \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)\) has a projective (resp. inductive) limit representable in \(C\), then \(\varphi\) has a projective (resp. inductive) limit representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), and we have an isomorphism canonical \[ \varprojlim_j \varphi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} \varprojlim_j \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) \tag{8.9.2.1} \] (resp. \[ \varinjlim_i \varphi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} \varinjlim_j \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) ) , \tag{8.9.2.2} \] where \(\varprojlim_j\) (resp. \(\varinjlim_j\)) is calculated in \(C\).

For the first formula, we simply use the inductive limits in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) filters switch to \(\varprojlim_j\), and that \(c\) switches to \(\varprojlim_j\) (8.9.1 d) and a)): \[ \varprojlim_j \varphi = \varprojlim_j \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»}\,\varprojlim_j^{\mathrm{Ind}(C)} \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»}\,\varprojlim_j^{C} \widehat{\varphi}(j,i) . \] The second is proved in the same way, using the switching of inductive limits of type \(I\) to the inductive limits of type \(J\) (2.5.0) and the fact that \(c\) switches to the inductive limits of type \(J\), proven in 8.9.4 a) below.
Case \(J\) discrete. This case is contained in the following more precise assertion:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.9.3

Let \(I_\alpha\) be essentially small indexed filter categories by a small set \(A\), and for all \(\alpha \in A\), i.e. \(X(\alpha) = (X(\alpha)_{i_\alpha})_{i_\alpha\in I_\alpha}\) a ind-object of \(C\) indexed by \(I_\alpha\). Let \(I\) be the product category of \(I_\alpha\), and suppose that for all \(i = (i_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A} \in I\), the product \(\prod_{\alpha\in A} X(\alpha)_{i_\alpha}\) is representable in \(C\). Then the product \(\prod_{\alpha\in A} X(\alpha)\) is representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), and it is canonically isomorphic to the ind-object indexed by \(I\) given by the formula \[ \prod_{\alpha\in A} \text{«}\varinjlim_{i_\alpha\in I_\alpha}\text{»} X(\alpha)_{i_\alpha} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varinjlim_{i=(i_\alpha)_{\alpha\in A}\in I}\text{»} \Bigl(\prod_{\alpha\in A} X(\alpha)_{i_\alpha}\Bigr) , \tag{8.9.3.1} \] where the product on the right hand side is the product calculated in \(C\). (Note that \(I\) is filtering and essentially small, the \(I_\alpha\) being so.)

Indeed, it is well known (and immediate by reduction in case we work in the category of sets) that the formula considered is valid when we calculate the limits in \(\widehat{C}\). The conclusion then results from the fact that \(L\) commutes at the considered limits (8.9.1 a) and 8.5.1).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.9.4

The demonstration given in 8.9.2, 8.9.3 shows, more generally, that if for all \(i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\), \(\varprojlim_j \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)\) (resp. \(\varinjlim_j \widehat{\varphi}(j,i)\)) calculated in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is representable, then it is the same for \(\varprojlim_j \varphi\) (resp. of \(\varinjlim_j \varphi\)). This and the argument of c) shows that for a given category \(J\) coming from an ordered set finite or discrete (or more generally, which is \(C\)-admissible (8.8.1)), projective limits (resp. inductive) of type \(J\) are representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) if (and only if) for all functor \(\varphi : J \to C\), the projective (resp. inductive) limit of \(\varphi\) calculated in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is representable. In the other case, this also means, by virtue of a), that any limit projective of type \(J\) of representable pre-sheaves on \(C\) is ind-representable.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.9.5

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

a) The canonical functor \[ c : C \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) \] is exact to the right (therefore exact, taking into account 8.9.1 a)).

b) If finite inductive limits (resp. finite sums) are representable in \(C\), then the small inductive limits (resp. the small sums) are representable in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). Let \(J\) be a finite or discrete preordered set; if the inductive limits of type \(J\) are representable in \(C\), it is the same in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

Demonstration. a) Suppose that in \(C\) we have \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_j X_j\), where \(J\) is a finite category, \(X\) and the \(X_j\) in \(C\). We then have, for any ind-object \(\underline{Y} = (Y_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(C\): \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}(X,\underline{Y}) &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X,Y_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_i \varprojlim_j \mathrm{Hom}(X_j,Y_i) \\ &\overset{2.8}{\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }} \varprojlim_j \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_j,Y_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_j \mathrm{Hom}(X_j,\underline{Y}) , \end{aligned} \] and the desired conclusion results from the comparison of the extreme terms.

b) It has already been noted in 8.9.4 that the assertions made result from a) and 8.9.2, from less for finite inductive limits. To prove the conclusions made in the cases infinite, we are brought back to the case of sums, which can be interpreted as an inductive limit filtering finite sums, and we therefore conclude using 8.5.1.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.9.6

We have already observed that the functor \(c\) does not generally commute to filtering inductive limits, therefore not to infinite sums either, contrary to what takes place for \(L : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \widehat{C}\). On the other hand, with the exception of switching at the limits inductive filtering (8.5.1), \(L\) practically never has switching properties at some other type of inductive limits (initial object, sum of two objects, cokernels of double arrows). Thus, if \(C\) admits an initial object \(\varnothing_C\), which is therefore the initial object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) by virtue of a), \(\varnothing_C\) is never the initial object of \(\widehat{C}\) (i.e. identical to the prebundle constant with value \(\varnothing\)), since \(\mathrm{Hom}(\varnothing_C,\varnothing_C) \neq \varnothing\)!

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.9.7

Either \[ f : C \to C' \] a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, and let \(J\) be a finite and rigid category (8.8.5). If the limits inductive (resp. projective) of type \(J\) are representable in \(C\) and if \(f\) commutes there, then \(\mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C')\) also switches to this type of limits.
This immediately follows from 8.8.5 and the calculation 8.9.2 of the finite limits in a category of ind-objects, for a functor represented in index form.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.9.8

If in \(C\) the finite inductive (resp. projective) limits are representable, and if \(f\) is right (resp. left) exact then the same is true of \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\); in the other case, \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) switches even at any small inductive limits.
The first assertion follows from 8.9.7. The second results from the first, taking into account that \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) switches to the filtering inductive limits (8.6.3).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.9.9

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

a) Suppose that in \(C\) finite sums are representable. Show that if in \(C\) the finite sums are disjoint (resp. universal) (cf. II 4.5), then in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) the small sums are disjoint (resp. universal).

b) Suppose that in \(C\) every morphism factors into an epimorphism followed by an monomorphism (resp. into an effective epimorphism followed by a monomorphism, resp. into a epimorphism followed by effective monomorphism, resp. an effective epimorphism followed by a effective monomorphism). Show that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) satisfies the same property. In the last respective case, we therefore conclude that any epimorphism of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is effective, any monomorphism of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is effective, any bimorphism of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is an isomorphism; show that in this case any epimorphism (resp. monomorphism) of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) can be represented by a inductive system of epimorphisms (resp. monomorphisms) of \(C\). If we assume more that in \(C\) any epimorphism (resp. any monomorphism) is universal, the same property is true in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\).

c) Suppose \(C\) is additive (resp. abelian), then \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is also additive.

d) Let \(X = \text{«}\varinjlim_I\text{»} X_i\) be an ind-object of \(C\), \(R \rightrightarrows X\) a relation of equivalence in \(X\). For all \(i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\), let \(R_i \rightrightarrows X_i\) be the equivalence relation in \(X_i\) (considered object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\)) induced by \(R\) via \(X_i \to X\). (We assume that the fiber product \(R_i = R \times_{X\times X} (X_i\times X_i)\) in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) is representable by an object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), which is the case if in \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable.) Show that for \(R\) to be effective, it is enough for the \(R_i\) to be effective.

e) Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\), \(R\) an equivalence relation in \(c(X)\) i.e. in \(X\) considered as object of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). Suppose that in \(C\) the fiber products are representable. For \(R\) to be effective, \(R\) must be of the form \(\text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} R_i\), where the \(R_i\) are equivalence relations in \(X\) (considered as an object of \(C\)), and this condition is sufficient if we assume that in \(C\) the equivalence relations are effective.

f) Suppose that in \(C\) every morphism factors into an effective epimorphism followed of an effective monomorphism, and that the finite projective limits are representable. Either \(X\) an object of \(C\), and \(R\) a sub-object of \(X\times X\), considered as an element of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\). Let us write \(R\) in the form \(\text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} Z_i\), where \((Z_i)_{i\in I}\) is a filter family increasing number of sub-objects of \(X\times X\) in \(C\) (this is possible thanks to b)). Show that for that \(R\) is an equivalence relation in \(X\), it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\), exists a \(j \in \mathrm{Ob}\, I\) which contains \(s(Z_i)\) and \(Z_i \circ Z_i\), where \(s\) is the symmetry of \(X\times X\).

g) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite projective limits as well as the finite sums are representable, that any equivalence relation is effective there, and any morphism is there factors into an effective epimorphism followed by an effective monomorphism. Show that in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) equivalence relations are universal if and only if \(C\) satisfies the following condition:

(ST) For any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any sub-object \(Z\) of \(X\times X\) in \(C\), if we define by induction the sequence of sub-objects \(Z_n\) (\(n \geq 0\)) of \(X\times X\) in \(C\) by \(Z_0 = Z\), \(Z_{n+1} = \mathrm{Sup}(Z_n, s(Z_n), Z_n \circ Z_n)\) (the Sup taken from all the sub-objects of \(X\times X\), which exists thanks to the hypotheses made on \(C\)), then the sequence \((Z_n)_{n\geq 0}\) is stationary.

k) Show that in \(\mathrm{Ind}((\mathrm{Ens}))\), the equivalence relations are not necessarily effective.

NB. For criteria for \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) to be a topos, cf. VI 8.9.9.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.9.10

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. Let's set \(\mathrm{Pro}(C) = (\mathrm{Ind}(C^\circ))^\circ\) (see 8.11).

a) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite sums (resp. small sums) are representable. Show that if they are disjoint (cf. II 4.5), then \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) satisfies the same condition.

b) Let \(J\) be a small set, such that the sums indexed by \(J\) are representable in \(C\), therefore also in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\). Let \((X(\alpha))_{\alpha\in J}\) be a family of elements of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\), with \(X(\alpha) = \text{«}\varprojlim_{I_\alpha}\text{»} X_{i_\alpha}\). Show that so that the sum of \(X(\alpha)\) in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) is universal, it suffices that it is the same for each of the families \(\varprojlim_J X_{i_\alpha}\), where \((i_\alpha)_{\alpha\in J} \in \prod_{\alpha\in J} I_\alpha\). Conclude that if in \(C\) the sums of type \(J\) are universal, for it to be the same in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\), it is necessary and sufficient that for all family \((X(\alpha))_{\alpha\in J}\) as above, with \(I_\alpha = I\) for all \(\alpha\in J\), homomorphism canonical in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) \[ \text{«}\varprojlim_I\text{»} \coprod_\alpha X(\alpha)_i \leftarrow \text{«}\varprojlim_{I^J}\text{»} \coprod_{\alpha\in J} X(\alpha)_{i_\alpha} \] or an isomorphism. Conclude that in \(\mathrm{Pro}((\mathrm{Ens}))\) the sums of type \(J\) are universal if and only if \(J\) is finite.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.9.11

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

a) Let \((X_i \to X)_{i\in I}\) be a family of morphisms in \(C\). Show that so that she is epimorphic in \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\), it suffices that there exists a finite subfamily which is epimorphic in \(C\). Prove that this condition is also necessary when we assume that in \(C\) every finite family of goal morphisms \(X_i\) factorizes into a family epimorphic, followed by an effective monomorphism (10.5). (For this last assertion, either \(P\) the set of finite parts of \(I\), ordered by inclusion, and let \(X' = (X_J')_{J\in P}\) be the ind-object formed of images of the finite subfamilies of the given family, finally either \(X'' = X \sqcup_{X'} X = \text{«}\varinjlim\text{»} X \sqcup_{X_J'} X\). Show that the two canonical morphisms \(X \rightrightarrows X''\) are distinct, but coincide on \(X_J'\).)

b) Suppose that in \(C\) every finite family of morphisms with the same goal is factorized into an epimorphic family, followed by an effective monomorphism. Prove that \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) admits a small subcategory generating by epimorphisms (7.1) if and only if \(C\) admits a small subcategory \(C'\) such that any object of \(C\) is the goal of an epimorphic family finished from source in \(C'\). When we assume that \(C\) admits a small subcategory generator (7.1), then \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) admits a small generating subcategory by epimorphisms if and only if \(C\) is equivalent to a small category. (For this last statement, use 7.5.2).

c) \(\mathrm{Ind}((\mathrm{Ens}))\) does not admit a small generating subcategory.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.9.12

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and consider \(\mathrm{Pro}(C) = \mathrm{Ind}(C^\circ)^\circ\).

a) Show that if \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) admits a small subcategory \(P'\) generating by epimorphisms (7.1), then there exists a small subcategory \(C'\) of \(C\) which is generative by epimorphisms in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\). (Take object component category from \(P'\).)

b) Show that the category \(\mathrm{Pro}((\mathrm{Ens}))\) does not have a small generating subcategory by epimorphisms. (If \(C'\) is like in a), choose a set \(X\) whose cardinal majors strictly the cardinals of the elements of \(C'\), and consider the pro-set \(\underline{X}\) formed by the complements in \(X\) parts of cardinal \(< \mathrm{card}(X)\). Show that for everything together \(Y\) not empty of cardinal \(< \mathrm{card}(X)\), we have \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y,\underline{X}) = \varnothing\), but that \(\underline{X}\) is not isomorphic to the constant proset \(\varnothing\).)
8.10. Dual notions: proobjects, pro-representable functors

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. We call pro-object of \(C\) any functor \[ \varphi : I^\circ \to C , \tag{8.10.1} \] where \(I\) is an essentially small filter category (called the index category), and as usual \(I^\circ\) denotes the opposite category. Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{V} \supset \mathcal{U}\). We will pay attention that the pro-objects of \(C\) indexed by \(I \in \mathcal{V}\) are in correspondence one-to-one with the ind-objects of \(C^\circ\) indexed by \(I \in \mathcal{V}\), associating with any such ind-object \(\psi : I \to C^\circ\) the pro-object \(\varphi = \psi^\circ : I^\circ \to C\). Inspired by this correspondence, we define “by transport of structure and reversal of the arrows” the notion of morphism between pro-objects of \(C\) from the analogous notion (8.2.4.2), (8.2.5.1) for in-objects, and we consequently define the category of pro-objects of \(C\) indexed by of \(I \in \mathcal{V}\), and a canonical isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Pro}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ind}_{\mathcal{V}}(C^\circ,\mathcal{U})^\circ ; \tag{8.10.2} \] for \(\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{U}\) we simply speak of the category of pro-objects of \(C\), denoted \(\mathrm{Pro}_{\mathcal{U}}(C)\) or simply \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\), which is therefore defined by \[ \mathrm{Pro}(C) = \mathrm{Pro}_{\mathcal{U}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ind}(C^\circ)^\circ . \tag{8.10.3} \] If two pro-objects are given in index form \[ \underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I} , \quad \underline{Y} = (Y_j)_{j\in J} , \tag{8.10.4} \] the formula (8.2.5.1) here takes the form \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{X},\underline{Y}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_j \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,Y_j) . \tag{8.10.5} \] The functor (8.4.1) applied to \(C^\circ\) gives, by passing to opposite categories, a functor canonical (denoted by the same letter \(c\) if there is no risk of confusion), allowing to identify \(C\) to a full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\): \[ c : C \to \mathrm{Pro}(C) ; \tag{8.10.6} \] it is to have such a functor, and not \(C \to \mathrm{Pro}(C)^\circ\), that we “reversed the arrows” in the definition of morphisms of pro-objects from the analogous definition for ind-objects. The pro-objects in the essential image of (8.10.6) are still calledpro-objects essentially constant.

Let’s ask \[ \check{C} = (C^\circ)^\wedge = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,(\mathrm{Ens})) , \tag{8.10.7} \] then the functor (8.2.4.7) can be considered as a fully faithful canonical functor \[ L : \mathrm{Pro}_{\mathcal{V}}(C,\mathcal{U}) \to \check{C}^\circ , \tag{8.10.8} \] and we find in particular \[ L : \mathrm{Pro}(C) \hookrightarrow \check{C}^\circ . \tag{8.10.9} \]

We will leave it to the reader to translate, as needed, the results of the previous and following sections of the language of ind-objects in that of pro-objects. Depending on the mathematical context, one or the other language is the most useful, without counting the cases where the two notions are introduced simultaneously, for example when it is necessary to consider complex categories like \(\mathrm{Pro}(\mathrm{Ind}(C))\) or \(\mathrm{Ind}(\mathrm{Pro}(C))\). We are content to give some additional indications, to fix terminology and notations, and specify “yoga”.

A covariant functor \(F : C \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) is said to be a pro-representable functor if it is in the essential image of (8.10.9) (or 8.10.8, it comes to the same thing); the subcategory full of \(\check{C}\) formed from these functors is therefore equivalent to \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)^\circ\). We will prefer usually work in the opposite category, essential image as subcategory of (8.10.9), which is therefore equivalent to \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) itself. In agreement with this usage, it is often better to look at covariant functors \[ F : C \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] as objects of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,(\mathrm{Ens}))^\circ = \check{C}^\circ\), and write the category accordingly homomorphisms \[ X \to F \quad \text{dans} \quad \check{C}^\circ , \] i.e. pairs \((X,u)\) of an object \(X\) of \(C\) and of a \(u \in F(X)\), like \[ {}_F\backslash C . \tag{8.10.11} \] With this convention, we will therefore find a covariant functor (source functor) \[ {}_F\backslash C \to C , \tag{8.10.12} \] and 3.4 is rewritten in the form \[ F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \text{«}\varprojlim\text{»}_{({}_F\backslash C)^\circ} X . \tag{8.10.13} \]

Criterion 8.3.3 applied to \(C^\circ\) becomes a pro-representability criterion for \(F\): it is necessary and sufficient that \(({}_F\backslash C)^\circ\) be filtering and essentially small, this last condition being superfluous if \(C\) is equivalent to a small category; if more in \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable, it amounts to the same thing to say that \(F\) y commutes i.e. is left exact.

In \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) the small filtering projective limits are representable and the functor (8.10.9) commutes there; in other words, this functor transforms projective limits in \(\mathrm{Pro}(C)\) in inductive limits of \(\check{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,(\mathrm{Ens}))\), just like its compound \(C \to \check{C}^\circ\) with (8.10.6), which shares with it the unfortunate property of being contravariant when considered at values in \(\check{C}\). We will pay attention that the functors pro-representables of \(C\) in \((\mathrm{Ens})\) are the functors which are small limits inductive filters of representable functors (and not projective limits, like the terminology could possibly suggest this).
8.11. Ind-deputies and pro-deputies

Either \[ f : C \to C' \tag{8.11.1.1} \] a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories, hence a functor \(F \mapsto F\circ f\) \[ f^* : \widehat{C'} \to \widehat{C} . \tag{8.11.1.2} \] We say that \(f\) admits an ind-adjoint if the previous functor transforms functors ind-representable into ind-representable functors. As \(f^*\) commutes at limits inductive, and that the full subcategory of \(\widehat{C'}\) formed of ind-representable functors is stable by small filtering inductive limits, it amounts to the same thing to say that \(f\) admits an ind-adjoint, or that \(f^*\) applies objects of \(C\) (identified to a full subcategory of \(\widehat{C}\)) in ind-representable functors on \(C\), i.e. \[ X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(f(X),Y') \text{ est ind-représentable pour tout } Y' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C'. \tag{8.11.1.3} \] Another way of expressing the condition that \(f\) admits an ind-adjoint is to say that there exists a functor \[ g : \mathrm{Ind}(C') \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) \tag{8.11.1.4} \] which is essentially induced by \(f^*\), i.e. such that we have an isomorphism of bifunctors \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C)}(X,g(\underline{Y'})) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C')}(f(X),\underline{Y'}) , \tag{8.11.1.5} \] where \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\) and \(\underline{Y'} \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \mathrm{Ind}(C')\). In fact, it is even enough to have a functor \[ g_0 : C' \to \mathrm{Ind}(C) \tag{8.11.1.6} \] and an isomorphism of bifunctors \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C)}(X,g_0(Y')) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{C'}(f(X),Y') \tag{8.11.1.7} \] in \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), \(Y' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C'\). The functor \(g\) (resp. \(g_0\)) is obviously determined to unique isomorphism close to \(f\), and conversely we reconstitute \(f\) with canonical isomorphism close by the knowledge of this \(g\) (resp. \(g_0\)). It is clear from (8.11.1.5) that the functor \(g\) commutes at inductive limits, and that it “extends” the functor \(g_0\); this therefore determines it up to a single isomorphism in terms of \(g_0\) (8.7.2). The functor \(g\), and sometimes also the functor \(g_0\) that it extends, is called the ind-adjoint functor of \(f\) (no need here to specify: on the right, because the other, if it exists, will be called the pro-adjoint, cf. 8.11.5 below).

Of course, when \(f\) admits a deputy on the right \[ f^{\mathrm{ad}} : C' \to C , \] it admits an ind-adjoint \(g\), and this is canonically isomorphic to the extension canonical from \(f\) to ind-objects: \[ g \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Ind}(f^{\mathrm{ad}}) . \tag{8.11.1.8} \] The notion of ind-adjoint is therefore a natural generalization of the notion of right-adjoint.
Let us now consider the canonical extension \[ \mathrm{Ind}(f) : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to \mathrm{Ind}(C') . \tag{8.11.1.9} \] We then have:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.11.2

For the functor \(f : C \to C'\) to admit an ind-adjoint, it is necessary and it is enough that the functor \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) (8.11.1.9) admits a right adjoint. The latter is canonically isomorphic to ind-adjoint (8.11.1.4).

Sufficiency and the last assertion are trivial on the ind-adjunction formula (8.11.1.5). For necessity, we note that by passing to the projective limit on this formula on \(X_i\), we deduce an isomorphism of addition into the ind-objects \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) and \(\underline{Y'}\): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C)}(\underline{X}, g(\underline{Y'})) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Ind}(C')}(\mathrm{Ind}(f)(\underline{X}),\underline{Y'}) , \tag{8.11.2.1} \] cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.11.3

If \(f\) admits an ind-adjoint, then \(\mathrm{Ind}(f)\) commutes at the limits inductive, and the ind-adjoint \(g\) commutes to projective limits.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.11.4

For the functor \(f : C \to C'\) to admit an ind-adjoint, it is necessary that \(f\) is exact on the left, and this condition is sufficient when \(C\) is equivalent to a small category.
This follows from criterion (8.11.1.3), and from 8.3.1 and 8.3.3 (iv).
For another criterion in terms of the notion of accessible functor, cf. 8.13.3.

Now consider the functor \(F \mapsto F\circ f\) \[ f^{\circ *} : \check{C'} \to \check{C} \tag{8.11.5.1} \] induced by \(F\). We will say that \(f\) admits a pro-adjoint if the previous functor applies pro-representable functor into pro-representable functor, i.e. if there exists a functor (called pro-adjoint functor of \(f\)) \[ g : \mathrm{Pro}(C') \to \mathrm{Pro}(C) , \tag{8.11.5.2} \] and an isomorphism of bifunctors \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(C)}(g(\underline{Y'}),X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(C')}(\underline{Y'},f(X)) . \tag{8.11.5.3} \] Of course, saying that \(f\) admits a pro-deputy means that \(f^\circ\) admits an ind-deputy, of so that the notions and results for the ind-adjoints are trivially translated into terms of pro-deputies. Let us only point out that \(f\) admits a pro-deputy if and only if \(\mathrm{Pro}(f) : \mathrm{Pro}(C) \to \mathrm{Pro}(C')\) admits a right adjoint, and in this case the functor \(g\) precedent is such an adjoint to the right of \(\mathrm{Pro}(f)\); and that for this it is necessary that \(f\) be exact at left, this condition also being sufficient when \(C\) is equivalent to a small category. In this case, \(f\) is therefore exact if and only if it admits both an ind-adjoint and a pro-deputy.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 8.11.6

Consider the case of a functor \[ f : C \to (\mathrm{Ens}) . \] If this functor admits a pro-adjoint, it is pro-representable, and the converse is true if and only if the full subcategory of \(\check{C}\) formed by pro-representable functors is stable by small products; this is the case in particular if \(C\) is equivalent to a small category (8.10.14) or if in \(C\) small products are representable (8.9.5 b) applied to \(C^\circ\)). Roughly speaking, we can therefore say that for a functor \(f : C \to C'\), the notion of existence of a pro-adjoint is thenatural generalization of the notion of pro-representability of \(f\), which is defined when \(C' = (\mathrm{Ens})\).
8.12. Strict ind-objects and pro-objects. Application to a representability criterion

Either \[ \underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I} \] an ind-object of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C\), and let \(F\) be the pre-bundle that it ind-represents. We see then as soon as it amounts to the same thing to say that the canonical morphisms \[ X_i \to F = \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} X_i \] are monomorphisms of \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) (or, which amounts to the same thing, of \(\widehat{C}\), i.e. monomorphisms of functors "argument by argument"), or to say that for any arrow \(i \to j\) of \(I\), the corresponding transition arrow \[ X_i \to X_j \] is a monomorphism. When these conditions are met, and if in addition \(I\) is a ordered category, we say that \(\underline{X}\) is a strict ind-object. Note that this condition is not invariant under isomorphism of ind-objects; an ind-object will be called essentially strict if it is isomorphic to a strict ind-object. A pre-bundle will be called strictly ind-representable if it is ind-representable by a strict ind-object; so \(F = \text{«}\varinjlim_i\text{»} X_i\) is strictly unrepresentable if and only if \(\underline{X} = (X_i)_{i\in I}\) is essentially strict.

Let \(F\) be a presheaf on \(C\), and consider the full subcategory of \(C_{/F}\) formed from the arrows \(X \to F\) of source in \(C\) which are monomorphisms. We will call it the category of representable subfunctors of \(F\); this is the category associated with the set ordered of the representable subfunctors of \(F\), ordered by the order induced from that of the set of sub-objects of \(F\). It then immediately results in the definitions:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.12.2

For the pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\) to be strictly ind-representable, it It is necessary and sufficient that the (ordinate) category \(I\) of the representable subfunctors of \(F\) be filtering and essentially small and that we have \[ \varinjlim_I X_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F , \tag{8.12.2.1} \]

When for any object \(X\) of \(C\), the set of sub-objects of \(X\) in \(C\) is small (by example if \(C\) admits a small generating subcategory (7.4)), this implies that the category of representable sub-functors is even small.

If \(F\) is strictly unrepresentable, there is therefore a preferred way to do it ind-represented by an ind-object, and the latter is even a strict ind-object: we take the representation (8.12.2.1). A strict ind-object \(\underline{Y}\) is said to be saturated if it is isomorphic to a ind-object of the form \((X_i)\) appearing in (8.12.2.1), for \(F\) suitable; therefore it exists at unique isomorphism, a single saturated strict ind-object isomorphic to the strict ind-object given, namely that envisaged in 8.12.2, taking \(F = \varinjlim \underline{Y}\) (limit in \(\widehat{C}\)).

Suppose we already know that we can find a small cofinal part in the set \(\mathrm{Ob}\, C_{/F}\), which is the case in particular if \(F\) is unrepresentable; then he follows that the same condition is verified in the full subcategory \(I\) considered in 8.12.2, so we can in criterion 8.12.2 omit the condition that \(I\) is essentially small.

Let \(F\) be a pre-beam on \(C\), and consider an object \[ u : X \to F \] from \(C_{/F}\). We say that \(u\) (or the pair \((X,u)\)) is minimal if for any factorization of \(u\) into \[ X \xrightarrow{\ p\ } X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } F , \tag{8.12.3.1} \] with \(p\) a strict epimorphism (10.2), \(p\) is an isomorphism. Considering \(u\) as an object of \(F(X)\) (1.4), saying that \(u\) is minimal therefore means that any strict epimorphism \(p : X \to X'\) such that \(u \in \mathrm{Im}(F(p) : F(X') \to F(X))\) is an isomorphism. This notion is clarified by part a) of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.12.4

Let \(F\) be a pre-beam on \(C\).

a) Suppose that \(F\) transforms cokernels into nuclei and consider a morphism \[ u : X \to F , \] with \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\). For \(u\) to be a monomorphism, it is sufficient, when in \(C\) the cokernels of double arrows are representable, whether \(u\) is minimal; this condition is also necessary if in \(C\) the fiber products are representable.

b) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable. So that the subcategory of representable subfunctors of \(F\) (8.12.1.1) is filtering (not necessarily small) and has as inductive limit \(F\), it suffices that \(F\) is exact on the left and that any morphism \(u : X \to F\), with \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), is factorized into \[ X \to X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } F , \] with \(u'\) minimal; this condition is also necessary if in \(C\) the fiber products are representable.

c) Suppose that \(C\) admits a small generating subcategory (7.1), and that \(I\) the category of representable subfunctors of \(F\) is filtering, then \(I\) is small.

Demonstration. a) Suppose \(u\) minimal, prove that \(u\) is a monomorphism, i.e. that for any double-arrow \(v, v' : Y \rightrightarrows X\) such that \(uv = uv'\), we have \(v = v'\). Indeed, if \(X' = \mathrm{Coker}(v,v')\), then \(u\) factors into \(X \xrightarrow{\ p\ } X' \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } F\) (\(F\) transforming cokernels to nuclei), and since \(p\) is a strict epimorphism by construction, it follows that \(p\) is an isomorphism i.e. \(v = v'\). Suppose that \(u\) is a monomorphism, let's prove that it is minimal. Consider a factorization (8.12.3.1); as \(p\) is a strict epimorphism, it is the core of the canonical double arrow \(v, v' : X\times_{X'} X \rightrightarrows X\), and as \((u'p)v = (u'p)v'\) and \(u'p = u\) is a monomorphism, we have \(v = v'\) therefore \(p\) is a isomorphism.

b) Sufficiency: As \(F\) is left exact, \(C_{/F}\) is filtering. As we know that \(F = \varinjlim_{C_{/F}} X\), we are brought back by 8.1.3 c) to prove that the full subcategory \(I\) of \(C_{/F}\) of subobjects of \(F\) is cofinal in \(C_{/F}\); but by virtue of “it is enough” in a), this is ensured by the hypothesis that every object of \(C_{/F}\) is augmented by a “minimal” object. Necessity: As any filtering inductive limit of exact functors on the right is itou, the first condition is trivially necessary. The second then results from “it must” in a).

c) A representable subfunctor \(X \hookrightarrow F\) of \(F\) is known when we know the full subcategory \(C'_{/X}\) of \(C'_{/F}\), where \(C'\) is a small fixed generating subcategory from \(C\). (Use the filtering hypothesis \(I\).) As \(C'_{/F}\) is small, the set of its full subcategories is small, hence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.12.5

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where the finite inductive limits are representable, and admitting a small generating subcategory (7.1). Let \(F\) be a pre-beam on \(C\). For \(F\) to be strictly unrepresentable, it suffices for it to satisfy the following two conditions, and these are also necessary if in \(C\) the products fibers are representable:

a) \(F\) is left-exact.

b) Any pair \((X,u)\), with \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\) and \(u \in F(X)\), is increased in \(C_{/F}\) by a minimal torque (8.12.3), i.e. there exists a minimal torque \((X',u')\) (\(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), \(u' \in F(X')\)) and a morphism \(f : X \to X'\) such that \(u = F(f)(u')\).
Sufficiency results from 8.12.2 and 8.12.4 b), c), necessity from 8.3.1 and 8.12.4 a).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.12.6

When \(F\) transforms amalgamated sums of \(C\) into fiber products, we immediately sees that for a couple \((X,u)\) given as in b), the set of strict quotients \(X'\) of \(X\) such that \(u \in \mathrm{Im}(F(X') \to F(X))\) is decreasing filtering, which implies that if the set of strict quotients of \(X\) is Artinian, then condition b) of 8.12.5 is automatically satisfied. If therefore the previous condition on \(X\) is satisfied for all object \(X\) of \(C\) (we also say, sometimes, that the objects of \(C^\circ\) are artinian), then it follows from 8.12.5 that \(F\) is strictly unrepresentable if and only if \(F\) is exact at left; in this case, \(F\) is therefore strictly unrepresentable as soon as it is unrepresentable (8.3.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.12.7

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where the small projective limits are representable, and admitting a small cogenerator subcategory (7.9, 7.13). So a functor \(F : C \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) is representable if and only if \(F\) commutes to small limits projectives.

The need is clear. For sufficiency, we apply 8.12.5 to the opposite category \(C^\circ\). To first prove that \(F\) is (strictly) pro-representable, we are reduced to prove that any pair \((X,u)\), \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\) and \(u \in F(X)\), is increased by a pair “minimal.” Now the family \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) of strict sub-objects of \(X\) such that \(u \in \mathrm{Im}(F(X_i) \to F(X))\) is small (7.5 in dual form). Thanks to the fact that \(F\) is exact left, it is \(\infty\)-filtering (8.12.6), and thanks to the fact that \(F\) switches to small limits projectives we see that \(X' = \varprojlim_I X_i\) is a smaller object in this family. (Use the fact that, \(F\) being exact on the left, transforms monomorphisms into monomorphisms.) If \(u' \in F(X')\) is the only element whose image in \(F(X)\) is \(u\), we then see that \((X',u')\) is a minimum increasing torque \((X,u)\). This proves that \(F\) is pro-representable, and the conclusion then results from

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.12.8.1

Let \(F : C \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) be a functor, where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category where the small projective limits are representable. For \(F\) to be representable, it is necessary and it is enough that it is pro-representable and that it commutes to small projective limits.

The necessity is clear, let us prove the sufficiency. Saying that \(F\) is representable means obviously that \({}_F\backslash C\) admits an initial element (in fact, the initial objects of \({}_F\backslash C\) are precisely the isomorphisms \(F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X\), i.e. the data of representation for \(F\)). Now \(F\) being pro-representable, \(({}_F\backslash C)^\circ\) is filtering and equivalent to a small category, so \(X = \varprojlim_{({}_F\backslash C)^\circ} X\) is representable in \(C\), and since \(F\) commutes at the considered limit, it follows \(X\) is an initial object of \({}_F\backslash C\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.12.8

The assumptions on \(C\) being those of 8.12.7, i.e. \(f : C \to C'\) a functor of \(C\) in a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(C'\). For \(f\) to admit a deputy on the left, it is necessary and it is enough that \(f\) commutes to small projective limits.
This in fact reduces trivially to 8.12.7, by applying this statement to the functors compounds of the form \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(Y',f(X))\).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 8.12.9

As noted in 7.13, the assumptions on \(C\) of 8.12.7 and 8.12.8 are verified if \(C\) is the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves of sets on a space topological \(X \in \mathcal{U}\) (or more generally, on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (II 3.0.2)). Let's give an example instructive∗ which shows that the hypothesis of the existence of a small subcategory cogenerator \(D\) of \(C\) is not overabundant in 8.12.7. Let us take for \(C\) the category of groups elements of \(\mathcal{U}\). Let \(J\) be the set of isomorphy classes of simple groups \(\in C\), let us choose for all \(j \in J\) a simple group \(G_j\) in the class of \(j\), and let \(I\) the ordered set filtering the \(\mathcal{U}\)-small parts of \(J\), and for \(i \in I\), i.e. \(X_i = \coprod_{j\in i} G_j\). The \(X_i\) then form a projective system \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(C\), at transition morphisms of epimorphisms, and the corresponding functor \[ F(X) = \varinjlim_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i,X) \tag{\(*\)} \] takes its values in \((\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) (although the index set \(I\) obviously does not have a cardinal \(\in \mathcal{U}\)); more precisely, let us show that for any small full subcategory \(C_0\) of \(C\), there exists a \(i_0 \in I\) such that the restriction of \(F\) to \(C_0\) is representable by \(X_{i_0}\), which will prove both that \(F\) has values in \(\mathcal{U}\)-Ens, and that it commutes to small projective limits. To prove our assertion, it suffices to note that for all \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C_0\), the cardinality of the set \(J(X)\) of \(j \in J\) such that there exists a homomorphism non-trivial of \(G_j\) in \(X\) is necessarily small, since such a morphism is necessarily a monomorphism (\(G_j\) being simple); consequently, if \(i_0\) is the part of \(J\) meeting of \(J(X)\) for \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_0\), \(i_0\) is small i.e. \(i_0 \in I\), and it does the trick. Else on the other hand it is clear that \(F\) is not representable, since we have card \(I \notin \mathcal{U}\). Of this and of 8.12.7 we therefore conclude that the category \(C\) of groups \(\in \mathcal{U}\) does not admit a small full cogenerator subcategory. As the object \(\mathbb{Z}\) of \(C\) is on the other hand a generator, it then follows from the demonstration of 7.12 that there exists a group \(G\) with two generators which does not delve into an injective object of the category \(C\) of the groups \(\in \mathcal{U}\). It seems moreover, it is plausible that \(C\) does not admit any other injective object than unit groups.
8.13. Prorepresentable functors and accessible functors

In this issue, we use some concepts and results from the paragraph following, and in particular 9.11 and 9.13, to obtain a criterion of pro-representability that we will use (incidentally) in IV 9.16. \(C\) subsequently designates a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category satisfying condition L of 9.1 b), this condition being met for example if in \(C\) the small filtering inductive limits are representable.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.13.2

Let \(C\) be as above, and \[ f : C \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] a functor.

a) Suppose that each object of \(C\) is accessible (9.3). If \(f\) is pro-representable, \(f\) is accessible (9.2) and exact on the left.

b) Suppose that in \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable, and that \(C\) admits cardinal filtration (9.12). If \(f\) is reachable and left-exact, then \(f\) is prorepresentable.

Demonstration. a) The assumption on \(C\) means that representable covariant functors of \(C\) in \((\mathrm{Ens})\) are accessible. It is therefore the same for any small inductive limit of such functors (9.6 (i)), hence also of any pro-representable functor.

b) Under 8.3.3 (iii), it remains to be proven that in \(\mathrm{Ob}({}_F\backslash C)^\circ\) there is a small cofinal subcategory. Now by hypothesis there exists a cardinal \(\pi\) such that \(f\) is \(\pi\)-accessible. Let \((X,u)\), \(u \in F(X)\), be an object of \({}_F\backslash C\). With the notations of 9.12 c) we then have \(X = \varinjlim_i X_i\), with \(I\) filtering large in front of \(\pi\) and the \(X_i\) in \(C' = \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(C)\), hence \(F(X) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_i F(X_i)\). This shows that the small subcategory \(({}_F\backslash C')^\circ\) is cofinal in \(({}_F\backslash C)^\circ\), and completes the demonstration.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.13.3

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category satisfying the following conditions:

a) In \(C\) finite projective limits are representable.

b) In \(C\) the small filtering inductive limits are representable.

c) The functor \(\mathrm{Ker}\) on the category of double arrows of \(C\) is accessible (by example, it switches to small \(\varinjlim\) filters).

d) Any strict epimorphism of \(C\) is strict universal (10.2).

e) There is a small subcategory of \(C\) generating by strict epimorphisms (7.1).

Under these conditions, a functor \(f : C \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) is prorepresentable if and only if it is exact on the left and accessible (9.2).
Indeed, the conditions of 8.13.2 a) and b) on \(C\) are verified, by virtue of 9.11 and 9.13 respectively.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.13.4

Let \(f : C \to C'\) be a functor between \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories satisfying the conditions a) to e) of 8.13.3. For \(f\) to admit a pro-deputy, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) is exact on the left and accessible.

Like representable functors \[ h_{Y'} : X' \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(Y',X') : C' \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] are left-exact and accessible (9.11), if \(f\) has these same properties, it is the same of its compounds with the preceding functors, which are therefore pro-representable by 8.13.3, i.e. \(f\) admits a prodeputy. Conversely, suppose that \(f\) admits a prodeputy, and are \(C'_1\) a small subcategory generating \(C\), \(\pi\) a cardinal such as the \(Y' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C'\) are \(\pi\)-accessible; to prove that \(f\) is \(\pi\)-accessible, it is therefore enough to prove that it so are its compounds with \(h_{Y'}\), \(Y' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C'\); or by hypothesis these compounds are pro-representable, therefore they are accessible (8.13.3), therefore \(\pi\)-accessible provided that we take \(\pi\) big enough, cqfd.

footnotes.html: (∗) For a contravariant dependence, under certain conditions, cf. 8.11.

pages.title: 9. Accessible functors, cardinal filtrations and construction of small generating subcategories.

blocks.html: 9. Accessible functors, cardinal filtrations and construction of small generating subcategories.

This paragraph, of a more technical nature than the other paragraphs of this exposed, will only be used in this seminar in IV 9 and in VI 4, which are not used elsewhere in the Seminary. It is therefore necessary to omit reading this paragraph, the less on first reading!

All categories considered in this issue are assumed to be \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories. Except for the small categories of indices \(I, J \ldots\) that we will have to use, the following developments will mainly apply to “large” categories \(E, F \ldots\) which are stable by small filtering inductive limits. However, it will most often suffice that a slightly weaker condition is verified (condition L in 9.1 below). All cardinals considered in this issue are assumed \(\in \mathcal{U}\).

Following a suggestion from P. DELIGNE, we will study, for a functor \(f : E \to F\) between large categories, a switching condition from \(f\) to certain types of limits filtering inductive, remarkably stable condition, and which will be verified for the functors the most important ones found in nature. The applications we have in mind, for our seminar, are 9.13.3, 9.13.4 (used in VI 4) and especially 9.25, which gives, in a non-trivial case, the existence of a small generating family in a category of sections of a fibered category; this result will be used in IV 9.16.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.1

a) Let \(I\) be a preordered set, \(\pi\) be a cardinal. We say that \(I\) islarge in front of \(\pi\) if \(I\) is filtering, and if any part of \(I\) of cardinal \(\leq \pi\) admits an upper bound in \(I\).

b) Let \(E\) be a category. If \(\pi\) is a cardinal, we say that \(E\) satisfies the condition \(L_\pi\) if for any small ordered set \(I\) large in front of \(\pi\), \(E\) is stable by the limits inductive of type \(I\). We say that \(E\) satisfies the condition \(L\) if there exists a cardinal \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(E\) satisfies the condition \(L_\pi\).

When in 9.1 a) we have \(\pi \geq 2\), the second condition stated already implies that \(I\) is filtering, and if \(\pi\) is finite, \(I\) large in front of \(\pi\) simply means that \(I\) is filtering. We will only be interested in the case where \(\pi\) is infinite. Note that if \(\pi, \pi'\) are two cardinals such that \(\pi' \geq \pi\), then \(I\) big in front of \(\pi'\) implies obviously \(I\) big in front \(\pi\).

As announced in 9.0, the conditions \(L_\pi\), \(L\) should be considered as technical variants of the stronger condition of stability by small inductive limits filtering. It is clear that if \(\pi, \pi'\) are cardinals such that \(\pi' \geq \pi\), then the condition \(L_\pi\) implies the condition \(L_{\pi'}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.2

Let \(f : E \to F\) be a functor. If \(\pi\) is a cardinal, we say that \(f\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible) if \(E\) satisfies \(L_\pi\) (9.1) and if for all set ordered \(I \in \mathcal{U}\) large in front of \(\pi\), and any inductive system \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(E\) of type \(I\), the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim_i f(X_i) \to f(\varinjlim_i X_i) \] is a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism). We say that \(f\) is preaccessible (resp. accessible) (relative to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\)) if there exists a cardinal \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(f\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible).
The category of \(\pi\)-accessible (resp. accessible) functors of \(E\) in \(F\) will be noted \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi\) resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_{\mathrm{acc}}\).
Obviously, a functor commuting to small filtering \(\varinjlim\) (e.g. a functor admitting a deputy on the right) is \(\pi\)-accessible for any cardinal \(\pi \geq 2\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.3

Let \(E\) be a category, \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \[ h_X^\circ : E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}) \] the covariant functor it represents, \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) a cardinal. We say that \(X\) is an object \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible) of \(E\) if the functor \(h_X^\circ\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible); we say that \(X\) is preaccessible (resp. accessible) if there exists a cardinal \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(X\) is a \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible) object of \(E\).
We designate by \(E_\pi\) the full subcategory of \(E\) formed by \(\pi\)-accessible objects by \(E\).

When we apply definition 9.3 to a category of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F)\), the terminology introduced presents a priori an ambiguity with analogous terminology introduced in 9.2, when interpreting the objects of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F)\) as functors; he doesn't However, it does not appear that there is a serious risk of confusion.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.4

Let \(E\) be a category, \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) be a cardinal. We say that \(E\) is a category \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible) if there exists in \(E\) a small full subcategory \(C\) which is generator (7.1) and whose objects are \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible) (9.3). We say that \(E\) is a pre-accessible category (resp. accessible) if there exists a cardinal \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(E\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible (resp. \(\pi\)-accessible).
For important examples, cf. 9.11.3 below.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.5

Let \(f : E \to F\) be a functor between categories such that \(F\) or accessible and that any object of \(E\) is accessible. So, if \(f\) admits an assistant on the left, \(f\) is accessible.

Indeed, by hypothesis, \(F\) admits a small conservative family of functors representable \(F \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) which are accessible, so we are immediately brought back to showing that the compound of \(f\) with each of the preceding functors is accessible. But as \(f\) admits a left adjoint, these compounds are representable \(E \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) functors, therefore accessible according to the hypothesis on \(E\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.6

Let \(E\) and \(F\) be two categories, \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) be a cardinal and let us consider the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\) formed by \(\pi\)-accessible functors (9.2) from \(E\) in \(F\).

(i) This subcategory is stable by any type of \(\varinjlim\) which is representable in \(F\).

(ii) Suppose \(F\) \(\pi\)-accessible (9.4), and let \(J\) be a category such that \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, J \leq \pi\) and that the \(\varprojlim\) of type \(J\) are representable in \(F\), therefore the \(\varprojlim\) of type \(J\) are representable in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\). So the subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi\) is stable by \(\varprojlim\) of type \(J\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.7

Let \(E\) and \(F\) be two categories, with \(F\) accessible (9.4). So the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_{\mathrm{acc}}\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\) formed by accessible functors is stable by any type of inductive or projective limit, relative to a small category of indices \(J\), which is representable in \(F\) (therefore in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\)).

Proof of 9.6. The assertion (i) results trivially from the commutation of the functor \(\varinjlim\) at any inductive limits. For (ii), let \((f_j)_{j\in J}\) be a projective system of functors \(\pi\)-accessible \(E \to F\), \(f = \varprojlim f_j\) its projective limit, which is calculated “argument by argument”, let us prove that \(f\) is \(\pi\)-accessible, i.e. that for every set ordered \(I \in \mathcal{U}\) large in front of \(\pi\), and any inductive system \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) in \(E\), the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim_i ((\varprojlim_j f_j)(X_i)) \to (\varprojlim_j f_j)(\varinjlim_i X_i) \] is an isomorphism. However, the “argument by argument” calculation of the functor \(\varprojlim_j f_j\) gives us allows you to identify the previous canonical morphism with the canonical morphism \[ \varinjlim_i \varprojlim_j f_j(X_i) \to \varprojlim_j \varinjlim_i f_j(X_i) \] associated with the bifunctor \[ (i,j) \mapsto f_j(X_i) : I\times J \to F . \] So 9.6 is a consequence of the more general assertion:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.8

Let \(F\) be a \(\pi\)-accessible category, \(I\) be a large ordered set in front of \(\pi\), \(J\) a small category such as \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, J \leq \pi\), and that the \(\varprojlim\) of type \(J\) be representable in \(F\), \(h : I\times J \to F\) a functor; then the morphism canonical \[ \varinjlim_i \varprojlim_j h(i,j) \to \varprojlim_j \varinjlim_i h(i,j) \tag{9.8.1} \] is an isomorphism. In other words, the functor \[ \varinjlim_i : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I,F) \to F \tag{9.8.2} \] commutes to projective limits of type \(J\) (for any small category \(J\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}(J) \leq \pi\) and such that projective limits of type \(J\) are representable in \(F\)). Or again, for any \(J\) as above, the functor \[ \varprojlim_j : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(J, F) \to F \tag{9.8.3} \] is \(\pi\)-accessible.

As by hypothesis \(F\) admits a conservative family of covariant functors representable \(\pi\)-accessible \(g : F \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\), we immediately see that we are brought back to prove 9.8 in the case where \(F = \mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\). We will then prove the assertion in the form of switching from (9.8.2) to \(\varprojlim\) of type \(J\), with \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, J \leq \pi\). As \(I\) is filtering, we know that (9.8.2) commutes at finite projective limits (2.8). We are therefore brought back by a standard argument (cf. 2.3) to prove that it commutes to products indexed by a set \(J\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, J \leq \pi\). This brings us back to proving the bijectivity of (9.8.1) when \(J\) is discrete. Let's prove the injectivity: consider two elements \(a, b\) of the first member, they therefore come from \(\prod_j h(i_0,j)\) for \(i_0 \in I\) suitable, be \(\prod_j a(i_0,j)\) and \(\prod_j b(i_0,j)\); suppose that the elements \(\prod_j h(\infty,j)\) of the second member that they define are equal, i.e. that for all \(j\), there exists \(i(j) \geq i_0\) such that \(a(i_0,j)\) and \(b(i_0,j)\) have same image in \(h(i,j)\). As \(I\) is big in front of \(\mathrm{card}\, J\), he follows that there exists a common upper bound \(i_1 \in I\) of all \(i(j)\), which implies that the two considered elements of \(\prod_j h(i_0,j)\) have same image in \(\prod_j h(i_1,j)\), so define the same element of the first member of (9.8.1), which establishes the injectivity. For the surjectivity, let \(\prod_j a(\infty,j)\) be an element of the right hand side; so for all \(j \in J\), \(a(\infty,j)\) comes from an element of \(h(i(j),j)\), for a suitable \(i(j) \in I\). Like previously, we can find a common upper bound \(i \in I\) of \(i(j)\), therefore \(\prod_j a(\infty,j)\) comes from an element \(\prod_j a(i,j)\) of \(\prod_j h(i,j)\), so is in the image of (9.8.1). This completes the demonstration.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.9

Let \(F\) be a \(\pi\)-accessible (resp. accessible) category, then for any category \(J\) such as \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, J \leq \pi\) (resp. any small category \(J\)) and for all functor \((X_j)_{j\in J} : J \to F\) whose inductive limit in \(F\) is representable, if the \(X_j\) are \(\pi\)-accessible (resp. accessible) it is the same for \(\varinjlim X_j\).

Indeed, the functor \(F \to (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\) represented by \(\varinjlim X_j\) is the projective limit functors represented by \(X_j\), and we apply 9.6 (ii) to the projective system formed by these functors.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.10

In statements 9.6, 9.7, 9.8 and 9.9 we can replace the words “\(\pi\)-accessible”, “accessible” by “\(\pi\)-preaccessible”, “preaccessible”. The given demonstration indeed also proves this variant of the previous statements.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.11

Let \(E\) be a category, satisfying the condition L (9.1), \(C\) a small full generator subcategory (7.1). We assume the condition is satisfied:

a) \(E\) is stable by kernels of double arrows, and the functor \(\mathrm{Ker}\) on the category of double arrows of \(E\) is accessible (for example, it switches at small inductive limits filters).

Then any object of \(E\) is preaccessible (9.3), a fortiori \(E\) is preaccessible. Suppose that \(C\) is even generating by strict epimorphisms (7.1), and suppose that \(E\) satisfies under the conditions:

b) \(E\) is stable by fiber products.

c) Any small strict epimorphic family in \(E\) is universal strict epimorphic (10.3), or in \(E\) small direct sums are representable, and any epimorphism strict of \(E\) is a universal strict epimorphism.

Then any object of \(E\) is accessible, a fortiori \(E\) is accessible.
The fact that, given a), any object of \(E\) is accessible, results from the fact that for all object \(X\) of \(E\), the set of strict subobjects of \(X\) is small (7.4), and of

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.11.1

Under the conditions of 9.11 a), let \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) and \(\pi\) be a cardinal such that \(E\) satisfies \(L_\pi\) (9.1), that the functor \(\mathrm{Ker}\) in 9.11 a) is \(\pi\)-accessible, and that the set of strict sub-objects of \(X\) or of cardinal increased by \(\pi\). Then \(X\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible.

Indeed, if \(I\) is a large set in front of \(\pi\), \((Y_i)_{i\in I}\) an inductive system in \(E\) inductive limit \(Y\), and \((u_i, v_i : X \rightrightarrows Y_i)\) a double arrow such as the double arrow composed \(X \underset{v}{\overset{u}{\rightrightarrows}} Y\) satisfies \(u = v\), we prove that there exists \(j \geq i\) in \(I\) such that \(u_j = v_j\). For this, consider the inductive system of double arrows \((u_j, v_j : X \rightrightarrows Y_j)_{j\geq i}\), whose inductive limit is \((u,v)\). By hypothesis we have \(X = \mathrm{Ker}(u,v) = \varinjlim_j \mathrm{Ker}(u_j,v_j) = \varinjlim_j X_j\), where \(X_j = \mathrm{Ker}(u_j,v_j)\). But the \(X_j\) are strict sub-objects of \(X\), so there exists a part \(I'\) of \(I\) formed of indices \(j \geq i\), such that \(\mathrm{card}\, J \leq \pi\) and that all \(X_j\) is equal to one of \(X_{i'}\), (\(i' \in I'\)). How big \(I\) is in front of \(\pi\), there is an upper bound \(j\) of \(I'\) in \(I\). Then \(X_j\) contains all \(X_{j'}\), for \(j' \geq i\), therefore \(X_j \to X\) is an epimorphism (since the family of \(X_{j'} \to X\) is epimorphic), therefore an isomorphism since it is a strict monomorphism. So we have \(u_j = v_j\), which proves 9.11.1.
The second assertion in 9.11 follows from the first, and

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.11.2

Under the conditions of 9.11 a), b), c), let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), and \(\pi\) an infinite cardinal such that \(E\) satisfies \(L_\pi\) (9.1), that we have \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{ob}\, C_{/X} \leq \pi\), that for two objects \(X' \to X\) and \(X'' \to X\) of \(C_{/X}\), \(X'\times_X X''\) be \(\pi\)-preaccessible, finally that \(X\) or \(\pi\)-preaccessible. Then \(X\) is an \(\pi\)-accessible.

With the notations of the proof of 9.11.1, it suffices to prove that any morphism \(u : X \to Y\) comes from a morphism \(u_i : X \to Y_i\). Now consider the family of morphisms \(Y_i \to Y\), which is strict epimorphic; thanks to b) and c), the family of \(Y_i\times_Y X \to X\) is also strict epimorphic; on the other hand, for all \(i\), as \(C\) is generating by strict epimorphisms, the arrow family \[ T \to Y_i\times_Y X \] of source in \(C\) is strict epimorphic.
