In the second alternative considered in c), one can find such a strict epimorphic arrow, from source, a (small) sum of objects from \(C\). By virtue of the transitivity of the notion of universal strict epimorphic family (II 2.5) it then follows that the family of arrows \(T_\alpha \xrightarrow{\ f_\alpha\ } X\) of source in \(C\) which is factor by one of \(Y_i\times_Y X\) is strict epimorphic. Let \(J\) be the set of indices of this family, which is of cardinal increased by \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{ob}\, C_{/X} \leq \pi\). Let's choose for everything \(\alpha \in J\) a \(i = i(\alpha) \in I\) and a \(X\)-morphism \(T_\alpha \to Y_i\times_Y X\), or what amounts to even, a \(v_\alpha : T_\alpha \to Y_i\) such that \(p_i v_\alpha = u f_\alpha\), where \(p_i : Y_i \to Y\) is the morphism canonical. As \(I\) is large in front of \(\pi\), we can choose \(i(\alpha)\) independent of \(\alpha\), i.e. \(i\). For any pair of indices \(\alpha, \beta \in J\), consider the compounds \[ \mathrm{pr}_1 v_\alpha, \mathrm{pr}_2 v_\beta : T_\alpha\times_X T_\beta \rightrightarrows Y_i . \] Their compounds with \(Y_i \to Y\) are equal, therefore, as \(T_\alpha\times_X T_\beta\) is \(\pi\)-preaccessible by hypothesis, there exists an index \(i' = i(\alpha,\beta) \geq i\) such that the compounds of the arrows considered with \(Y_i \to Y_{i'}\), are equal. As the set of couples \(\alpha, \beta\) is cardinal \(\leq \pi^2 = \pi\) (\(\pi\) being infinite), it still follows that we can choose \(i'\) independent of \(\alpha, \beta\). We can obviously assume \(i' = i\). But then, the family \((f_\alpha : T_\alpha \to X)\) being strict epimorphic, we can find a morphism \(u_i : X \to Y_i\) such that we have \(u_i f_\alpha = v_\alpha\). We then have \(p_i u_i = p\), because for all \(\alpha\) we have \((p_i u_i)f_\alpha = p_i(u_i f_\alpha) = p_i v_\alpha = u f_\alpha\), and the family of \(f_\alpha\) is epimorphic. This completes the demonstration of 9.11.2.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.11.3

We see, as a special case of 9.11, that in the category \(E\) everything object is accessible, in each of the following two cases: 1) \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category abelian with exact filtering inductive limits and admitting a small subcategory generator (here, it is the second alternative of c), which applies). 2) \(E\) is the category sheaves of sets on a topological space \(X \in \mathcal{U}\). More generally, it is sufficient that \(E\) is the category of bundles of sets on a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (II 2.1), or again, that \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (IV 1.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.12

Let \(E\) be a category. We call cardinal filtration of \(E\) a filtration increasing \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\) of \(E\) by strictly full subcategories \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), indexed by cardinals \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\) such as \(\pi \geq \pi_0\) (where \(\pi_0\) is a cardinal fixed infinity, depending on the cardinal filtration envisaged), and satisfying the conditions following:

a) For all \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) is equivalent to a small category.

b) \(E\) satisfies \(L_{\pi_0}\) (9.3), and for all \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) is stable in \(E\) for filtering inductive limits indexed by ordered sets \(I\) large in front \(\pi_0\) such as \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\).

c) For all \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), and all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), we can find an isomorphism \[ X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X_i , \] where \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) is an inductive system in \(E\) indexed by a large ordered set \(I\) in front of \(\pi\) (9.1), and where the \(X_i\) are in \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\). Additionally, if \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), \(\pi' \geq \pi\), we can take \(I\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi'^\pi\).
Note that it follows from b) and c) that all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) belongs to a \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), for \(\pi\) big enough.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 9.12.2

Let's take \(E = (\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens})\), \(\pi_0 = \aleph_0\), \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E) = \) full subcategory of \(E\) formed from sets such as \(\mathrm{card}\, X \leq \pi\). More generally:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.13

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. We assume that \(E\) is stable by small \(\varinjlim\) filtering, by sum of two objects and by cores of double arrows, that \(E\) is stable by fiber products, and that the epimorphic morphisms in \(E\) are epimorphic strict universals. Let \(C\) be a small subcategory full of \(E\) which is generating by strict epimorphisms. Let \(\pi_0\) be an infinite cardinal \(\geq \mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, C\). For any cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), i.e. \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) the strictly full subcategory of \(E\) formed by the objects \(X\) of \(E\) such that there exists a strict epimorphic family \((X_i \to X)_{i\in I}\) of goal \(X\), such that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\) and that \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\) for all \(i \in I\). Then \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\) is a cardinal filtration of \(E\). Moreover, for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), the cardinality of the set of the arrows of \(C_{/X}\) (and a fortiori, of all the objects of \(C_{/X}\)) is increased by \(\pi^{\pi_0}\).
This last assertion is none other than 7.6.

To show that we have cardinal filtration, we must prove the conditions a), b) and c) of 9.12. Condition a) follows immediately from 7.5.2. For b), suppose we have \(X = \varinjlim_I X_i\), with \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\) and the \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\). So the family of canonical morphisms \(X_i \to X\) is strict epimorphic, and by hypothesis on \(X_i\), it exists for all \(i \in I\) a strict epimorphic family \((X_{ij} \to X_i)_{j\in J_i}\), with \(\mathrm{card}\, J_i \leq \pi\). Moving on to are \(\coprod_i X_i\) and \(\coprod_{i,j} X_{ij}\), we see by transitivity strict epimorphisms universal (II 2.5) that the family of compounds \(X_{ij} \to X_i \to X\) (indexed by the set sum of \(J_i\), for \(i\in I\)) is strict epimorphic. But we have a card \(J \leq \pi^2 = \pi\), hence the conclusion. (NB. we only used the fact that \(\varinjlim_I X_i\) exists, and not the fact that \(I\) is large in front \(\pi_0\) nor even filtering.) Let us finally prove c). Note that for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\), the family of arrows \(X_i \to X\) from source in \(\mathrm{ob}\, C\) is strictly epimorphic, since \(C\) is generating by strict epimorphisms, and obviously small, so there exists a cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_0\) such as \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\). It remains to be proven that if we have cardinals \(\pi' \geq \pi \geq \pi_0\), and if \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^{\pi'}(E)\), then we have an isomorphism \[ X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X_i , \] with \(I\) large in front of \(\pi\), \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi'^\pi\), the \(X_i\) in \(\mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\). Now we have, \(C\) being generator by strict epimorphisms, \[ X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} T . \tag{\(*\)} \] Note also that by successively adding to \(C\) sums and cokernels of doubles arrows of \(C\), and the same for the category thus obtained etc, we return to the case where \(C\) is stable by sum of two objects in \(E\) and by cokernels of double arrows in \(E\), and this without destroying the hypothesis \(\pi_0 \geq \mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, C\). We can further assume that, if \(E\) contains a fixed initial object \(\varnothing_E\), we have \(\varnothing_E \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). This implies that the category \(C_{/X}\) is stable by sum of two objects and cores of double arrows. It is then filtering, because it is non-empty, since if it were empty, the relation (*) would show that \(X\) is an object initial, so \(C_{/X}\) contains the arrow \(\varnothing_E \to X\), a contradiction. Let then \(I\) be the set, ordered by inclusion, full subcategories \(i\) of \(C_{/X}\) which are filtering and such that \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{ob}\, i \leq \pi\). For all \(i \in I\), let \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\) be the inductive limit of the functor compound \(i \to C_{/X} \to E\), which exists by hypothesis. So we obviously have \[ \varinjlim_I X_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{C_{/X}} T \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X . \] On the other hand, we have already noted that card ob \(C_{/X} \leq \pi'^{\pi_0}\). It follows that \(I\) is large before \(\pi\), and that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq (\pi'^{\pi_0})^\pi = \pi'^{\pi_0\pi} = \pi'^\pi\) by virtue of the following lemma, the demonstration of which is left to the reader (where we will do \(I = C_{/X}\), \(c = \pi'^{\pi_0}\)):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.13.1

Let \(J\) be a filtering category, \(c\) and \(\pi\) two cardinals such that \(c \geq \mathrm{Sup}(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, J, \pi)\), and such that \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Hom}(j,j') \leq \pi_0\) for any pair of objects \(j, j'\) from \(J\). Let \(I\) be the set of full filtering subcategories \(i\) of \(J\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{ob}\, i \leq \pi\). Then, ordered by inclusion, \(I\) is large in front of \(\pi\), and we have \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq c^\pi\).
This completes the demonstration of 9.13.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 9.13.2

Slight extra effort should replace in 9.13 the hypothesis that \(E\) is stable by small inductive limits filtering by the hypothesis that \(E\) satisfies L (9.1), if we assume \(E\) stable by small sums, or that in \(E\) any family epimorphic is universal epimorphic. It is then necessary, in the demonstration of c), to choose \(\pi_0\) such that \(E\) satisfies \(L_{\pi_0}\), and limit ourselves to the full subcategories \(i\) of \(C_{/X}\) which are not only filtering, but such that the preordered set ob \(i\) is large in front of \(\pi_0\). Using 8. and the hypothesis that \(E\) satisfies \(L_{\pi_0}\), we then find that the \(X_i\) exist, and we should conclude with a suitable variant of 9.13.1, that the drafter did not not verified.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.14

Let \(f : E \to F\) be an accessible functor (9.2) between two categories equipped with cardinal filtrations \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\) and \((\mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(F))_{\pi\geq\pi'_0}\). So he exists a cardinal \(\pi_1 \geq \mathrm{Sup}(\pi_0,\pi'_0)\) such that for every cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_1\), we have \[ f(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)) \subset \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi^{\pi_1}}(F) . \tag{9.14.1} \] In particular, if we have \(\pi = 2^c\) with \(c \geq \pi_1\), hence \(\pi^{\pi_1} = 2^{c\pi_1} = 2^c = \pi\), we have \[ f(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)) \subset \mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(F) . \tag{9.14.2} \]
Let’s report it right away

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.15

Let \(E\) be a category, provided with two cardinal filtrations \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\) and \((\mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi'_0}\). So there exists a cardinal \(\pi_1 \geq \mathrm{Sup}(\pi_0,\pi'_0)\) such that, for any cardinal \(c \geq \pi_1\), posing \(\pi = 2^c\), we have \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E) = \mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(E)\).

Proof of 9.14. Let \(c\) be a cardinal such that \(c \geq \mathrm{Sup}(\pi_0,\pi'_0)\), and such that \(f\) be \(c\)-admissible. Or on the other hand \(\pi_1 \geq c\) such that we have \[ f(\mathrm{Filt}^c(E)) \subset \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi_1}(F) . \tag{\(*\)} \] There exists such a \(\pi_1\), thanks to the fact that \(\mathrm{Filt}^c(E)\) is equivalent to a small category (9.12 a)), so \(f(\mathrm{Filt}^c(E))\) is also, so that we can apply 9.12.1 to the objects of the latter to find a \(\mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(F)\) which contains them all (taking into account that the \(\mathrm{Filt}'^\pi(F)\) are full subcategories). So let \(\pi \geq \pi_1\), and \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), we prove that \(f(X) \in \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi^{\pi_1}}(F)\). Let us indeed write \[ X = \varinjlim_I X_i , \] with the \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^c(E)\), \(I\) large in front \(c\), \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi^c \leq \pi^{\pi_1}\) (9.12 c)). Like \(f\) is \(c\)-admissible, \(I\) large in front of \(c\), we have \[ f(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I f(X_i) , \] and like \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi^{\pi_1}\) and \(f(X_i) \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi_1}(F) \subset \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi^{\pi_1}}(F)\) by (*), we have \(f(X) \in \mathrm{Filt}'^{\pi^{\pi_1}}(F)\) by 9.12 b), cqfd.

The notion of cardinal filtration 9.12 is of little interest only when the objects of \(E\) are accessible. Note that it follows from 9.11 that this condition is satisfied when, in addition to the hypotheses of 9.13, we suppose that the functor \(\mathrm{Ker}\) on the category of doubles arrows of \(E\) is accessible. Let us also point out:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.16

Let \(E\) be a category with cardinal filtration \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\). Suppose the elements of \(\mathrm{Filt}^{\pi_0}(E)\) are objects accessible from \(E\); then there exists a cardinal \(\pi_1 \geq \pi_0\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) such that the objects of \(\mathrm{Filt}^{\pi_0}(E)\) are \(\pi_1\)-accessible; if \(\pi_1\) is chosen thus, then for any cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_1\), we have (with the notations of 9.3.1): \[ E_\pi \subset \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E) \subset E_{(\pi^{\pi_0})} . \tag{9.16.1} \]

The existence of \(\pi_1\) immediately results from the fact that \(\mathrm{Filt}^{\pi_0}(E)\) is equivalent to a small category (9.12 a)). Let then be \(\pi \geq \pi_1\). If \(X\) is in \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), writing \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X_i\) with \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi^{\pi_0}\), \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^{\pi_0}(E)\) for all \(i\) (9.12 c)), then it follows from 9.9 that \(X\) is \(\pi^{\pi_0}\)-accessible, hence the second inclusion (9.16.1). Suppose \(X\) is \(\pi\)-accessible, and write \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X_i\), with \(I\) large in front \(\pi\) and the \(X_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) (9.12 c)). By hypothesis on \(X\), the isomorphism given \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X_i\) is factorized by one of \(X_i\), so \(X\) is isomorphic to one direct factor of this \(X_i\). We conclude that \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), therefore the first inclusion (9.16.1), thanks to

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.16.2

Any object of \(E\) that is a direct factor of an object of \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) is in \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\).

Indeed, if \(X\) is the image of a projector \(p\) in the object \(Y\) of \(E\) (i.e. of a endomorphism \(p\) such that \(p^2 = p\)), and if \(I\) is a filtering ordered set, \(X\) is limit inductive of the inductive filtering system \((Y_i)_{i\in I}\) defined by \(Y_i = Y\) for all \(i \in I\), \(p : Y_i \to Y_j\) if \(i < j\). Taking \(I\) large in front of \(\pi_0\) and card \(I \leq \pi\), we therefore see that if \(Y \in \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), the same applies to \(X\) under 9.12 b).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.17

Under the conditions of 9.16, for any cardinal \(c \geq \pi_1\), posing \(\pi = 2^c\), \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) is identical to the strictly full subcategory of \(E_\pi\) of \(E\) formed from \(\pi\)-accessible objects.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.18

Let \(E\) be a category satisfying the condition \(L_\pi\) (9.1), where \(\pi\) is a cardinal, and \(C\) a full subcategory of \(E\). We denote by \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)_\pi\) the subcategory full of the category \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)\) of ind-objects of \(C\) (8.2) formed of ind-objects of the form \((X_i)_{i\in I}\), where \(I\) is a large ordered set in front of \(\pi\). Consider the functor canonical \[ (X_i)_{i\in I} \mapsto \varinjlim_I X_i : \mathrm{Ind}(C)_\pi \to E . \tag{9.18.1} \]

a) For this functor to be fully faithful, it is necessary and sufficient that any object \(X\) of \(C\) either a \(\pi\)-accessible object (9.3) of \(E\).

b) Let us place ourselves under the conditions of 9.17, in particular \(\pi = 2^c\), and take \(C = \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\). Then the functor (9.18.1) is an equivalence of categories.

Assertion a) is an immediate generalization of 8.7.5 a), and is proved in the same way way. Then b) results from 9.17 and condition 9.12 c) from cardinal filtrations, which implies that the considered functor is essentially surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.19

Under the conditions of 9.17, let \(C = \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), \(F\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category, and consider the functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F) \tag{9.19.1} \] induced by the functor “restriction to \(C\)” \(f \mapsto f|C\), where the source of (9.19.1) is the category of \(\pi\)-accessible functors from \(E\) in \(F\) (9.2). The previous functor is fully faithful. If \(F\) satisfies the condition \(L_\pi\) (9.1), then the functor (9.19.1) is a equivalence of categories.

The first assertion is proved as 7.8, using 9.18 b). The second is obtained by constructing a quasi-inverse functor of (9.19.1), by associating with any functor \(f : C \to F\) the functor \((X_i)_{i\in I} \mapsto \varinjlim_i f(X_i)\) of \(\mathrm{Ind}(E_\pi)\) in \(F\), and using 9.18 b) to deduce a functor \(\overline{f} : E \to F\). It all comes down to showing that the latter is \(\pi\)-accessible. Now this is proven like the analogous assertion 8.7.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.20

Let \(E\) be a category admitting cardinal filtration and such that any object of \(E\) is accessible (see 9.16), \(F\) a category. So:

a) The category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_{\mathrm{acc}}\) of accessible functors of \(E\) in \(F\) (9.2) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. (NB we recall (9.0) that the given categories \(E, F\) are supposed to be of \(\mathcal{U}\)-categories).

b) Suppose that \(F\) is stable by small filtering inductive limits. For all full subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) equivalent to a small category, with inclusion functor \(i : C \to E\), consider the corresponding functor \[ i_! : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F) \] (5.1). For a functor \(f : E \to F\) to be accessible, it is necessary and sufficient that there exists a small subcategory \(C\) of \(E\), such that \(f\) is in the essential image of the previous functor \(i_!\).

Demonstration. a) It suffices to prove that for any cardinal \(\pi\) such that \(E\) satisfies \(L_\pi\), the full subcategory \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_{\mathrm{acc}}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category. It is obviously enough to check it for cardinals of the form \(2^c\), with \(c\) quite large. But then this follows from 9.19, since (\(C = \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) being essentially small) \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,F)\) is obviously a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category.

b) By transitivity of the formation of functors \(i_!\), we can in the statement limit ourselves to the subcategories \(C\) of the form \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), where \(\pi\) is as in 9.17. We then see easily as the compound functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E),F) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)_\pi \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,F)\), where the first arrow is quasi-inverse of (9.19.1), and the second is the inclusion, is none other than the functor \(i_!\), up to isomorphism. So assertion b) follows from 9.19.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 9.20.1

(This exercise uses the notions of site and topos, developed in lectures II and IV.) Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(\mathcal{V}\) a universe such that \(\mathcal{U}\in\mathcal{V}\), \(C\) a small full generating subcategory of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), \(\pi_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) an infinite cardinal such that \(\pi_0 \geq \mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, C\). For any cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), \(\pi \in \mathcal{U}\), let \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\) be strictly full subcategory of \(E\) formed of objects \(X\) such that there exists a family strict epimorphic \((X_i \to X)_{i\in I}\) with goal \(X\), such that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\) and that \(X_i \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\) for all \(i \in I\).

a) Show that \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E))_{\pi\geq\pi_0}\) is a cardinal filtration of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-category \(E\), and that we can choose \(\pi_0\) such that for all \(\pi \geq \pi_0\), we have the inclusions \[ E_\pi \subset \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E) \subset E_{\pi^{\pi_0}} , \] where for any cardinal \(c\), \(E_c\) designates the strictly full subcategory of \(E\) formed of \(c\)-accessible objects.

b) Choosing a cardinal \(\pi \geq \pi_0\) such that \(\pi = \pi^{\pi_0}\) (for example \(\pi\) of the form \(2^c\), with \(c > \pi_0\)), and setting \(C = \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E)\), show that we have an equivalence of categories \[ \mathrm{Ind}(C)_\pi \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } E , \] (rating \(\mathrm{Ind}(C)_\pi\) of 9.18).

c) Let us keep the notations of b), and let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\), \(\widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}}\) the \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos of \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles on the site \(E\) (provided with its canonical topology). Let us designate by \(\mathrm{Ind}(C,\mathcal{V})_\pi\) the category of \(\mathcal{V}\)-Ind-objects of \(C\) indexed by sets of indices preordained large in front of \(\pi\). Show that we have an equivalence of categories \[ \mathrm{Ind}(C,\mathcal{V})_\pi \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}} . \]

d) Let \(c \in \mathcal{V}\) be a cardinal, \(\mathrm{Ind}(E,\mathcal{V})'_c\) the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Ind}(E,\mathcal{V})\) formed from \(\mathcal{V}\)-ind-objects of \(E\) indexed by a preordered set which is large compared to all cardinal \(< c\). Taking \(c = \mathrm{card}\, \mathcal{U}\), show that we have an equivalence of categories \[ \mathrm{Ind}(E,\mathcal{V})'_c \xrightarrow{\ \approx\ } \widetilde{E}_{\mathcal{V}} . \]

This section 9.21 develops technical preliminaries for the demonstration of Theorem 9.22 below, which constitutes the main result of this paragraph 9. Let \[ p : E \to B \tag{9.21.1} \] a fiber functor, where \(B\) is a small category, and where inverse image functors \[ f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha \] associated with the arrows \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\) are accessible (9.2). In particular, the categories fibers \(E_\alpha\) (\(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\)) satisfy the condition L (9.1), therefore, \(B\) being small, there exists a cardinal \(\pi'_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) such that all categories \(E_\alpha\) satisfy the condition \(L_{\pi'_0}\). Either \(\pi_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) an infinite cardinal \(> \pi'_0\), so that we have \[ \pi_0 > \pi'_0 , \quad E_\alpha \text{ satisfait } L_{\pi'_0} \quad \text{pour tout } \alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B . \tag{9.21.2} \] Moreover, the hypotheses made immediately imply the existence of a cardinal \(c \in \mathcal{U}\) satisfying the following conditions: \[ \begin{cases} \text{a)} & c \text{ est infini}, \\ \text{b)} & c \geq \mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, B, \\ \text{c)} & \text{pour toute flèche } f : \alpha \to \beta \text{ dans } B, \text{ le foncteur } f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha \text{ est } c\text{-accessible}. \end{cases} \tag{9.21.3} \]

Let us further suppose that we can find, for each \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), a subcategory full \(C_\alpha\) of \(E_\alpha\), satisfying the following conditions: \[ \begin{cases} \text{a)} & \text{Pour tout } X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_\alpha,\ X \text{ est } c\text{-accessible dans } E_\alpha\ (9.3). \\ \text{b)} & \text{Pour tout } X \in \mathrm{ob}\, E_\alpha,\ \text{on peut trouver un isomorphisme } X \xrightarrow{\sim} \varinjlim_I X_i \text{ dans } E_\alpha, \\ & \text{où les } X_i \text{ sont dans } C_\alpha \text{ et où } I \text{ est un ensemble ordonné grand devant } c. \end{cases} \tag{9.21.4} \] Let \(\pi\) be a cardinal such that we have \[ \pi \geq \pi_0 , \quad \text{donc } \pi > \pi'_0 , \tag{9.21.5} \] and for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), either \[ C_\alpha^\pi \subset E_\alpha \tag{9.21.6} \] formed of objects which can be represented in the form \(\varinjlim_I X_i\), where \(I\) is a set ordered large in front of \(\pi'_0\), such that \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\), and where the \(X_i\) are in \(C_\alpha\). It is clear then, thanks to 7.5.2, that \(C_\alpha^\pi\) is essentially small, i.e. is equivalent to a small category.

Either \[ F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E) \tag{9.21.7} \] the category of sections of \(E\) on \(B\), and either \[ F^\pi \subset F \tag{9.21.8} \] the strictly full subcategory of \(F\) formed of sections \(X : \alpha \mapsto X(\alpha)\) such that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\) we have \[ X(\alpha) \in C_\alpha^\pi . \] It is clear, the \(C_\alpha^\pi\) being essentially small, that the same is true of the category \(F^\pi\). We will show that this category is generative, and more precisely:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.9

Under the conditions and with the previous notations, we have the following:

(i) Any object of \(F\) is accessible (9.3). If \(d\) is a cardinal \(\geq \pi'_0\), and if \(\alpha \mapsto X(\alpha)\) is an element of \(F\) such that for all \(\alpha\), \(X(\alpha)\) is \(d\)-accessible in \(E_\alpha\), then \(X\) is \(d\)-accessible in \(F\).

(ii) Suppose \(\pi \leq c\), or that \(\pi'_0\) is finite (i.e. the \(E_\alpha\) stable by small \(\varinjlim\) filters). Then any object \(X\) of \(F\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(\varinjlim_I X_i\), where the \(X_i\) are in \(F^\pi\) and where \(I\) is large in front of \(\pi\).
To prove (i), note that by virtue of (9.21.4) and 9.9, any object of \(E_\alpha\) is accessible. On the other hand, \(F\) satisfies the condition \(L_{\pi'_0}\), under the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.10

Let \(p : E \to B\) be a fiber functor, \(F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E)\), \(I\) be a category, \(i \mapsto X_i\) a functor from \(I\) into \(F\). So that \(\varinjlim_I X_i\) is representable in \(F\), it is sufficient that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(\varinjlim_I X_i(\alpha)\) is representable in the category fiber \(E_\alpha\); when this is so, then \(\varinjlim_I X_i\) "calculates argument by argument." In particular, if the fiber categories satisfy the condition \(L_{\pi'_0}\) (\(\pi'_0\) being a cardinal given) it is the same for \(F\).
We leave the details of the (easy) demonstration of 9.21.10 to the reader, in we content to note that it is convenient to use the following result, the demonstration of which is immediate:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.21.10.1

With the assumptions and notations of 9.21.10 for \(p : E \to B\), and for \(I\), for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), the inclusion functor \(E_\alpha \to E\) commutes at inductive limits of type \(I\).

Returning then to the general conditions of 9.21.9, i.e. \(X\) an object of \(F\). As for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(X(\alpha)\) is accessible in \(E_\alpha\), there exists a cardinal \(d \in \mathcal{U}\) such that for all \(\alpha\), \(X(\alpha)\) or \(d\)-accessible in \(E_\alpha\). We can choose \(d \geq \pi'_0\), so that \(F\) meets \(L_d\) under 9.21.10. Still using 9.21.10 for calculating limits inductive \(\varinjlim_I Y_i\) in \(F\), with \(I\) large in front of \(d\), we immediately see that \(X\) is \(d\)-accessible. This proves (i).
We will now prove 9.21.9 (ii) in several steps ((9.21.11) to 9.21.16)).

So let \[ X : \alpha \mapsto X(\alpha) \] an object of \(F\). By virtue of (9.21.4 b)), we can, for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), find a set ordered \(I_\alpha\) large in front of \(c\), and an isomorphism \(X(\alpha) = \varinjlim_{i\in I_\alpha} X(\alpha)_i\), where \(i \mapsto X(\alpha)_i\) is an inductive system of type \(I_\alpha\) in \(E_\alpha\), the \(X(\alpha)_i\) in \(C_\alpha\). Consider the ordered set produced \(I = \prod_\alpha I_\alpha\); it is clear that he is big in front \(c\), and that the previous inductive systems give rise to inductive systems in the \(E_\alpha\), \[ i \mapsto X(\alpha)_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_\alpha , \quad i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I , \tag{9.21.11} \] indexed by the same ordered set \(I\), and to isomorphisms \[ X(\alpha) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_I X(\alpha)_i \quad \text{dans } E_\alpha , \tag{9.21.12} \] for everything \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\). We assume that the data (9.21.11) and (9.21.12) are subsequently fixed.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.13

Under the previous conditions, we can find an application \(\varphi : I \to I\) such as \(\varphi(i) \geq i\) for all \(i \in I\), and an application \((i,f) \mapsto \lambda(i,f)\) of \(I\times\mathrm{Fl}\, B\) in \(\mathrm{Fl}\, E\), satisfying the following conditions:

a) For \(i \in I\), \((f : \alpha \to \beta) \in \mathrm{Fl}\, B\), \(\lambda(i,f)\) is a \(f\)-morphism of \(X(\alpha)_i\) in \(X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)}\), making the diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix@R=1em@C=2em{ X(\alpha) \ar[rr]^-{X(f)} & & X(\beta) \\ & & X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[u] \\ X(\alpha)_i \ar[uu] \ar[urr]_(0.35){\lambda(i,f)} & & \\ \alpha \ar[rr]_-{f} & & \beta } \] where the vertical arrows are the canonical morphisms deduced from (9.21.12).

b) For all \(i \in I\), and \((f : \alpha \to \beta) \in \mathrm{Fl}\, B\), we have commutativity in the diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=1em@C=2em{ & & X(\beta)_{\varphi^2(i)} \\ X(\alpha)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[urr]^-{\lambda(\varphi(i),f)} & & X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[u] \\ X(\alpha)_i \ar[u] \ar[urr]_(0.35){\lambda(i,f)} & & \\ \alpha \ar[rr]_-{f} & & \beta } \]

c) For any pair of consecutive arrows \(\alpha \xrightarrow{f} \beta \xrightarrow{g} \gamma\) of \(B\), we have commutativity in the following diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=1em@C=1.6em{ X(\alpha) \ar[r]^-{X(f)} & X(\beta) \ar[r]^-{X(g)} & X(\gamma) \\ & & X(\gamma)_{\varphi^2(i)} \ar[u] \\ & X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[ur]^-{\lambda(\varphi(i),g)} & X(\gamma)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[u] \\ X(\alpha)_i \ar[ur]^-{\lambda(i,f)} \ar[urr]_(0.3){\lambda(i,gf)} & & \\ \alpha \ar[r]_-{f} & \beta \ar[r]_-{g} & \gamma } \] where the vertical arrows are those deduced from (9.21.12).
We will also note that the commutativity of the two upper trapezoids in b) is already contained in a), so that b) actually asserts the commutativity of the lower triangle of envisaged diagram.

Let's prove 9.21.13. Let \(i \in I\), and \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) be an arrow in \(B\). Let's consider the compound \(X(\alpha)_i \to X(\alpha) \xrightarrow{\ X(f)\ } X(\beta) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_j X(\beta)_j\), it defines a morphism in \(E\): \[ X(\alpha)_i \to f^*(X(\beta)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f^*(\varinjlim_j X(\beta)_j) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_j f^*(X(\beta)_j) , \] where the last equality comes from the fact that \(f^*\) is \(c\)-accessible (9.21.3 c)) and that \(I\) is large in front of \(c\). By virtue of (9.21.4 a)), like \(X(\alpha)_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_\alpha\), the morphism envisaged is factors by a \(f^*(X(\beta)_j)\), where \(j\) a priori depends on \(i\) and \(f \in \mathrm{Fl}(B)\). But by virtue of (9.21.3 b)), and as \(I\) is large in front of \(c\)), we can choose \(j\) independent of \(f\), i.e. \(j = \varphi(i)\). \(I\) being filtering, we can assume \(\varphi(i) \geq i\). We thus find, for \(i \in I\) and \(f \in \mathrm{Fl}\, F\), a morphism \[ X(\alpha)_i \to f^*(X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)}) , \] or what amounts to the same thing, a \(f\)-morphism \[ \lambda(i,f) : X(\alpha)_i \to X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} , \] which by construction makes the diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix@R=1em@C=2em{ X(\alpha) \ar[rr]^-{X(f)} & & X(\beta) \\ & & X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[u] \\ X(\alpha)_i \ar[uu] \ar[urr]_(0.35){\lambda(i,f)} & & \\ \alpha \ar[rr]_-{f} & & \beta } \tag{D(f)} \] Let us then consider, for \(i, f, g\) given, the lower triangle of the diagram considered in 9.21.13 c); it is not clear that it is commutative, but both compounds \(X(\alpha)_i \rightrightarrows X(\beta)_{\varphi^2(i)}\) become equal after composition with \(X(\beta)_{\varphi^2(i)} \to X(\beta)\), as it results from the commutativity of the two trapezoids upper, and the global contour trapezoid, which are the diagrams \(D(f), D(g)\) and \(D(gf)\) respectively. So, since \(X(\alpha)_{\varphi^2(i)}\) is \(c\)-accessible (9.21.4 a)) and that \(X(\alpha) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{j\in I} X(\alpha)_j\), with \(I\) large in front of \(c\), it follows that we can find a element \(j \geq \varphi^2(i)\) of \(I\), such that the two arrows considered become equal after composition with \(X(\beta)_{\varphi^2(i)} \to X(\beta)_j\). A priori, \(j\) depends on \(i, f\) and \(g\). But for \(i\) fixed, the set of possible pairs \(f, g\) is of cardinal \(\geq c^2 = c\), by virtue of (9.21.3 a) and b)), therefore, \(I\) being large in front of \(c\), we can choose \(j\) independent of \(f\) and \(g\), i.e. \(j = \varphi'(i) \geq \varphi^2(i) \geq \varphi(i)\). Let then, for all \(i \in I\) and \(f : \alpha \to \beta\), \[ \lambda'(i,f) : X(\alpha)_i \to X(\beta)_{\varphi'(i)} \] the \(f\)-compound morphism \(X(\alpha)_i \to X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)} \to X(\beta)_{\varphi'(i)}\), where the second arrow is the transition morphism. It is then immediate, by construction, that \((\varphi', \lambda')\) satisfies conditions 9.21.13 a) and c), for \((\varphi,\lambda)\). Proceeding the same for the condition b), we see that we can choose \(\varphi'\) in such a way that this condition is also satisfied for \((\varphi',\lambda')\). This completes the proof of 9.21.13).
Let it be now \[ J \subset I \] a part of \(I\) satisfying the following conditions:
a) \(J\) is filtering,
b) for all \(j \in J\), we have \(\varphi(j) \in J\),
c) for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(X_J(\alpha) = \varinjlim_{i\in J} X(\alpha)_i\) is representable in \(E_\alpha\).

Conditions a) and c) are satisfied in particular if \(J\) is large in front of \(\pi'_0\) (9.21.2). It then follows from 9.21.10.1 that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(X_J(\alpha)\) is the inductive limit \(\varinjlim_{i\in J} X(\alpha)_i\) in \(E\). Now for an arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), the arrows \(\lambda(i,f)\), for \(i\) variable in \(J\), define thanks to 9.21.13 b) a morphism of ind-objects of \((X(\alpha)_i)_{i\in J}\) in \((X(\beta)_i)_{i\in J}\), hence a homomorphism \[ X_J(f) : X_J(\alpha) \to X_J(\beta) \] on inductive limits, which is clearly a \(f\)-homomorphism. Using 9.21.13 c), we finds that we have transitivity relations \[ X_J(gf) = X_J(g) X_J(f) , \] so that we defined a section \(X_J \in \mathrm{ob}\, F\) of \(E\) to \(B\). Finally 9.21.13 a) shows us that canonical homomorphisms \[ X_J(\alpha) \to X(\alpha) , \quad \alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B , \] are functorial in \(\alpha\), so that we have a canonical homomorphism \[ u_J : X_J \to X . \]

On the other hand, if \[ J' \supset J \] is another part of \(I\) satisfying conditions a),b),c) above, we find a canonical homomorphism \[ u_{J',J} : X_J \to X_{J'} , \] so that the \(X_J\) form an inductive system in \(F\), parameterized by the set (ordered by inclusion) of the parts \(J\) of \(I\) satisfying the conditions envisaged. Finally, the homomorphisms \(u_J\) above define a homomorphisms of this inductive system in (the constant inductive system defined by) \(X\).

Let now \(K\) be a set of parts \(J\) of \(I\), satisfying the conditions a),b),c) considered in 9.21.14, and suppose that \(K\) is filtering, and of meeting \(I\). So he is clear that we have \[ \varinjlim_{J\in K} X_J \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X , \] using 9.21.10.1 which brings us back to verifying that we have an isomorphism argument by argument.

Take for example for \(K\) the set of all parts \(J\) of \(I\) which are large before \(\pi'_0\), stable by \(\varphi\), and such that \(\mathrm{card}\, J \leq \pi\). Then by definition (9.21.6) of \(C_\alpha^\pi\), we have, for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(X_J(\alpha) \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_\alpha^\pi\), therefore \(X_J \in \mathrm{ob}\, F^\pi\). Subsequently, 9.21.9 (ii) will be proven if we establish that \(K\) is large in front of \(\pi\) (therefore filtering) and of meeting \(I\). It will obviously suffice, for this, to prove that any part \(S\) of \(I\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, S \leq \pi\) is contained in a \(J \in K\). This will indeed result from the hypothesis preliminary set out in 9.21.9 (ii), and

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.16

Let \(I\) be an ordered set, \(\pi\) be an infinite cardinal, \(\varphi : I \to I\) be a application of \(I\) within itself.

a) Suppose \(I\) filtering. Then for any part \(S\) of \(I\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, S \leq \pi\), there exists a filter part \(J \supset S\) of \(I\), such as \(\varphi(J) \subset J\) and \(\mathrm{card}(J) \leq \pi\).

b) Let \(\pi'_0\) be a cardinal \(< \pi\), and suppose \(I\) large in front of \(\pi\). So for any part \(S\) of \(I\) such that \(\mathrm{card}\, S \leq \pi\), there exists a part \(J \supset S\) of \(I\) large in front of \(\pi'_0\), such as \(\varphi(J) \subset J\) and \(\mathrm{card}\, J \leq \pi\).

Let us prove for example b) (the proof of a) being analogous and simpler). Or \(P\) the set of parts of \(I\) of cardinal \(\leq \pi\), and let \(T \mapsto i_T : P \to I\) be an application such that for \(T \in P\), \(i_T\) is an upper bound of \(T\) in \(I\); the existence of this application simply expresses the hypothesis that \(I\) is large \(\pi\). Let \(A\) be a well-ordered set such that \(\mathrm{card}\, A = \pi\), and such that for all \(a \in A\), the set of \(a' \leq a\) is of cardinal \(< \pi\). He follows in particular, as \(\pi'_0 < \pi\), that \(A\) is large in front of \(\pi'_0\). Let's define by transfinite recurrence of applications \[ S \mapsto S_a : P \to P \quad (a \in A) , \] by the following formulas: \[ S_0 = S , \quad S_{a+1} = S_a \cup \varphi(S_a) \cup \{i_{S_a}\} , \quad S_a = \bigcup_{a'<a} S_{a'} \text{ si } a \text{ un ordinal limite}. \] Let us then put, for \(S \in P\), \[ J(S) = \bigcup_{a\in A} S_a . \] It is then clear that \(J(S) \supset S\), that \(J(S)\) is stable by \(\varphi\), that \(\mathrm{card}\, J(S) \leq \pi\) (since \(\mathrm{card}\, A = \pi\)), finally \(J(S)\) is large in front of \(\pi'_0\) (using the fact that \(A\) is large front \(\pi'_0\)).
This demonstrates 9.21.16 and completes the demonstration of 9.21.9.
Also note a variant of 9.21.9:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.17

The notations are those of 9.21.9. We denote by \(F'\) the subcategory full \(\underline{\mathrm{Homcart}}_B(B,E)\) of \(F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E)\) formed of the Cartesian sections of \(E\) on \(F\). So:

(i) Any object of \(F'\) is accessible. More precisely, let \(\alpha \mapsto X(\alpha)\) be an element of \(F'\), and let \(d\) be a cardinal such that \(d \geq \pi'_0\), \(d \geq c\), and such that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \(X(\alpha)\) is a \(d\)-accessible object of \(E\). Then \(X\) is a \(d\)-accessible object of \(F'\).

(ii) Suppose \(\pi \leq c\) and the functors \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) are \(\pi'_0\)-accessible, or that the categories \(E_\alpha\) are stable by small inductive limits filtering, and that the functors \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) commute at the said inductive limits. Then any object \(X\) of \(F'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(\varinjlim_I X_i\), where for all \(i \in I\), \(X_i\) is an object of the full subcategory \(F'^\pi = F'\cap F^\pi\) of \(F'\), and where \(I\) is large front \(\pi\).
The demonstration being entirely analogous to that of 9.21.9, we simply indicate the points where a modification of the latter is necessary. We first note:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.18

Statement 9.21.10 remains valid when replaced \(F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E)\) by \(F' = \underline{\mathrm{Homcart}}_B(B,E)\), provided that we assume that the functors inverse images \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) commute to inductive limits of type \(I\).

If therefore under the conditions of 9.21.17, \(d\) is a cardinal such that \(d \geq c\) and \(d \geq \pi'_0\), it results from the above and from the hypothesis (9.21.3 c)) that for any ordered set \(I\) large in front of \(d\), the inductive limits of type \(I\) are representable in \(F'\) and are calculated argument by argument, hence the conclusion 9.21.17 (i) by an immediate argument.
To prove (ii), we must add a complement to 9.21.13:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.21.19

Under the conditions of 9.21.13, suppose that \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, F'\) i.e. that \(X\) is a Cartesian section of \(E\) on \(B\). Then we can reinforce the conclusion by the assertion that there exists a function \(\mu\) which, to every \(i \in I\) and every arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), associates a morphism \(\mu(i,f) : f^* X(\beta)_i \to X(\alpha)_{\varphi(i)}\) into \(E_\alpha\), such that we have:

d) For all \(i \in I\) and all arrows \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), the following two diagrams are commutative: \[ \vcenter{\xymatrix@R=1.6em@C=1.4em{ X(\alpha)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[r]^-{\lambda(\varphi(i),f)} & f^*X(\beta)_{\varphi^2(i)} \\ f^*X(\beta)_i \ar[u]^-{\mu(i,f)} \ar[ur] & }} \qquad \vcenter{\xymatrix@R=1.6em@C=1.4em{ f^*X(\alpha)_{\varphi(i)} \ar[r]^-{\mu(\varphi(i),f)} & X(\alpha)_{\varphi^2(i)} \\ X(\alpha)_i \ar[u]^-{\lambda(i,f)} \ar[ur] & }} . \]

To prove it, we proceed as in 9.21.3, considering the compound morphism \(f^*(X(\beta))_i \to f^*(X(\beta)) \xrightarrow{\ X(f)^{-1}\ } X(\alpha)\), where (as already in writing the condition d)) we identify in the notations a \(f\)-morphism \(R \to S\) of \(E\) with the morphism corresponding \(R \to f^*(S)\) of \(E_\alpha\). Like \(X(\alpha) = \varinjlim_j X(\alpha)_j\), and \(I\) large in front \(c\), we can factorize the previous morphism by one of \(X(\alpha)_j\), where \(j\) a priori depends on \(i\) and on \(f\), but can be chosen independent of \(f\), i.e. \(\psi(i)\). Even if it means enlarging the function \(\varphi\) of 9.21.13, we can assume that \(\psi = \varphi\). By proceeding as for conditions b) and c) of 9.21.13, we see that, even if it means further enlarging the application \(\varphi\), we can assume that the two diagrams of 9.21.19 d) are commutative.

The application \(\varphi\) being chosen as in 9.21.19, let us return to the argument of 9.21.14, where it must however be assumed that \(J\) satisfies, in addition to the conditions stated a) b) c), on the condition:
d) For any arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) in \(B\), the functor \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) commutes to inductive limits of type \(J\).

I say that, subject to this additional condition, the section \(X_J\) of \(E\) on \(B\) is Cartesian, i.e. for any arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), the morphism \[ X_J(\alpha) \to f^*X_J(\beta) \tag{\(*\)} \] is an isomorphism. Indeed, by virtue of condition d) above, the aim of the morphism considered is identified with \(\varinjlim_{i\in J} f^*(X(\beta)_i)\), and the morphism is obtained by passing to \(\varinjlim_J\) from the morphism of ind-objects in \(E_\alpha\) \[ (X(\alpha)_i)_{i\in J} \to (f^*)(X(\beta)_i)_{i\in J} , \] deduced from the morphisms \(\lambda(i,f) : X(\alpha)_i \to f^*(X(\beta)_{\varphi(i)})\) for \(i \in J\). But the conditions explained in 9.21.19 assure us that the previous homomorphism of ind-objects is in makes an isomorphism, an inverse being obtained by the homomorphism deduced from the system of \(\mu(i,f)\), for \(i \in J\). This proves our assertion that (*) is an isomorphism, therefore that \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, F'\).

We will now assume that the functors \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) are \(\pi'_0\)-accessible. Then the conditions on \(J\) considered in 9.21.20 are satisfied if \(J\) is large in front of \(\pi'_0\), stable by \(\varphi\) and such that \(\mathrm{card}\, J \leq \pi\), and we complete the demonstration of 9.21.17 as in 9.21.15.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 9.22

Let \(p : E \to B\) be a fiber functor, where \(B\) is a category essentially equivalent to a small category, and where for any arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) in \(B\) the inverse image functor \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) is accessible (9.2). Let us further assume that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), the fiber category \(E_\alpha\) admits cardinal filtration (9.12) and that any object of \(E_\alpha\) is accessible (9.3). Then each of the categories \(F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E)\) and \(F' = \underline{\mathrm{Homcart}}_B(B,E)\) admits a small full subcategory generating by epimorphisms strict (7.1), and each of its objects is accessible.

It is immediate that the statement does not essentially change when we replace \(B\) by a full subcategory \(B_0\) such that the inclusion functor \(B_0 \to B\) is an equivalence, and \(E\) by \(E_0 = E\times_B B_0\). This allows us to assume that \(B\) is a small category.

Let, for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), \((\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E_\alpha))_{\pi\geq\pi_\alpha}\) be a cardinal filtration of \(E_\alpha\). Let \(c_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) be a cardinal satisfying the following conditions: \[ \begin{cases} c_0 \geq \underset{\alpha\in\mathrm{ob}\, B}{\mathrm{Sup}}\, \pi_\alpha , \quad c_0 \geq \mathrm{card}\, \mathrm{Fl}\, B ; \\ \text{pour toute flèche } f : \alpha \to \beta \text{ de } B,\ f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha \text{ est } c_0\text{-accessible}; \\ \text{pour toute } \alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B,\ \text{les objets de } \mathrm{Filt}^{\pi_\alpha}(E_\alpha) \text{ sont } c_0\text{-accessibles}. \end{cases} \tag{9.22.1} \] Let's ask \[ c = 2^{c_0} , \] so that \(c > c_0\), and for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), is \[ C_\alpha = \mathrm{Filt}^c(E_\alpha) . \] I say that the preliminary conditions at 9.21.9 and 9.21.17 are verified, by making \(\pi = \pi_0 = c\). This is clear for (9.21.2) and (9.21.3). For (9.21.4 a)), this follows from 9.17, and (9.21.4 b)) results from condition 9.12 c) for cardinal filtration. Note also that condition 9.12 b) of cardinal filtrations implies that for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), we have \(C_\alpha^\pi = C_\alpha = \mathrm{Filt}^c(E_\alpha)\), where \(C_\alpha^\pi\) is defined in (9.21.6). Therefore, 9.22 follows from 9.21.9 and 9.21.17, and more precisely, we proved the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.23

Under the conditions of 9.22, if \(c_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) is a satisfactory cardinal under conditions (9.22.1), and if \(c = 2^{c_0}\), then the subcategory \(F^c\) of \(F\) (resp. \(F'^c\) of \(F'\)) formed from \(X\) such that \(X(\alpha) \in \mathrm{ob}\, \mathrm{Filt}^c(E_\alpha)\) for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\) is essentially small and generative by strict epimorphisms; more precisely, any object \(X\) of \(F\) (resp. \(F'\)) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(\varinjlim_I X_i\), where the \(X_i\) are in \(F^c\) (resp. in \(F'^c\)), and where \(I\) is an ordered set large in front of \(c\).
By means of slightly stronger hypotheses in 9.22, we can also specify considerably 9.22 and 9.23:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.24

Under the conditions of 9.22, suppose that each of the categories \(E_\alpha\) (\(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, E\)) is stable by small filtering inductive limits, and that for all \(\pi \geq \pi_\alpha\), \(\mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E_\alpha)\) is stable by filtering inductive limits indexed by filtering ordered sets \(I\) such as \(\mathrm{card}\, I \leq \pi\) (which slightly reinforces the condition 9.12 b) of cardinal filtrations). In the case where it is \(F'\) that we consider, suppose further that for every arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), the functor \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) switches to small filtering inductive limits. Finally, let \(c_0 \in \mathcal{U}\) be a satisfactory cardinal to the conditions (9.22.1), and consider, for any cardinal \(\pi \geq c = 2^{c_0}\), the subcategory strictly full \(F^\pi\) (resp. \(F'^\pi\)) of \(F\) (resp. of \(F'\)) formed of \(X\) such that we have \(X(\alpha) \in \mathrm{Filt}^\pi(E_\alpha)\) for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\). So the subcategories considered define a cardinal filtration (9.12) of \(F\) (resp. of \(F'\)).

Conditions a), b), c) of 9.12 must be verified. Conditions a) and b) result from analogous conditions for the cardinal filtrations given by \(E_\alpha\), and 9.21.10 (resp. 9.21.18, taking into account the second of the conditions (9.22.1)). It remains to be proven c), whose first part follows immediately from 9.21.9 (resp. 9.21.17), making \(\pi'_0 = 0\), \(\pi_0 = c\). He remains to be proven that if we have two cardinals \(\pi' \geq \pi \geq c\), then for all \(X\) in \(F^{\pi'}\) (resp. \(F'^{\pi'}\)) we have \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{i\in K} X_i\), with the \(X_i\) in \(F^\pi\) (resp. \(F'^\pi\)), \(K\) large front \(\pi\), and card \(K \leq \pi'^\pi\). For this, let's go back to the demonstrations of 9.21.9 (ii) and 9.21.17 (ii). We can assume that each \(I_\alpha\) is of cardinality \(\leq \pi'\) under the condition 9.12 c) on the cardinal filtration of \(E_\alpha\), so we will have \[ \mathrm{card}\, I \leq (\pi'^\pi)^{\mathrm{card}\,\mathrm{ob}\, B} \leq (\pi'^\pi)^c = \pi'^{\pi c} = \pi'^\pi . \] The index set \(K\) used in 9.21.15 resp. 9.21.21 is the set of parts \(J\) of \(I\) which are filtering (i.e. large in front of \(\pi'_0 = 0\)), stable by \(\varphi\) and such as card \(J \leq \pi\). It is then immediate that we have \[ \mathrm{card}\, K \leq (\mathrm{card}\, I)^\pi \leq (\pi'^\pi)^\pi = \pi'^{\pi^2} = \pi'^\pi , \] which completes the demonstration of 9.24.

To conclude, it is appropriate to give a statement freed from hypotheses a little techniques of 9.22, replacing these by the conjunction, for the \(E_\alpha\), of the hypotheses which intervene in 9.11 (which ensures the accessibility of objects of \(E\)) and in 9.13 (which ensure the existence of cardinal filtration in \(E\)):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.25

Let \(p : E \to B\) be a fibering functor, where \(B\) is a category equivalent to a small category, and where for any arrow \(f : \alpha \to \beta\) of \(B\), the functor \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) is accessible (9.2). We further assume that, for all \(\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, B\), the fiber category \(E_\alpha\) meets the following conditions:

a) \(E_\alpha\) admits a small full subcategory, generating by strict epimorphisms (7.1).

b) \(E_\alpha\) is stable by fiber products, by nuclei of double arrows, by sums of two objects and by cokernels of double arrows, finally by small inductive limits filters∗.

c) The functor \(\mathrm{Ker}(u,v)\) on the category of double arrows of \(E_\alpha\) is accessible (by example, switches to small filtering inductive limits).

d) Any strict epimorphism of \(E_\alpha\) (10.2) is a universal strict epimorphism.

Under these conditions, each of the categories \(F = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_B(B,E)\) and \(F' = \underline{\mathrm{Homcart}}_B(B,E)\) admits a small generating subcategory by strict epimorphisms, and all its objects are accessible (9.3).
Also, 9.24 gives us:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.26

Under the conditions of 9.25, and in the case where we consider \(F' = \underline{\mathrm{Homcart}}_B(B,E)\), suppose that the functors \(f^* : E_\beta \to E_\alpha\) commute to small filtering inductive limits. Then \(F\) (resp. \(F'\)) admits a cardinal filtration, which we can be explained using the process of 9.24.

footnotes.html: (∗) This last condition is probably unnecessary, cf. 9.13.2.

blocks.html: 10. Glossary.
For the convenience of the reader, we have collected here the definitions of some terms used in previous issues. We designate by \(C\) a category.
10.1. Cartesian, Cocatesian:

A diagram of \(C\) \[ \xymatrix{ A \ar[r] \ar[d] & B \ar[d] \\ C \ar[r] & D } \] is called Cartesian if it is commutative and if the canonical morphism of \(A\) in the fibered product \(C\times_D B\) is an isomorphism. It is said to be CoCartesian if the corresponding diagram of the category opposite to \(C\) is Cartesian.
10.2. Epimorphism, strict epimorphism etc. See 10.3.
10.3. Epimorphic family, strict epimorphic etc. :

A family \[ f_i : A_i \to B , \quad i \in I , \] of arrows with the same purpose in \(C\) is called an epimorphic family, if for any object \(C\) of \(C\), the application induced by \(f_i\): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(B,C) \to \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(A_i,C) \tag{10.3.1} \] is injective.

We say that the considered family is strict epimorphic if the image of the application (10.3.1) is formed of families \((g_i)\) such that for any object \(R\) of \(C\), any pair of indices \(i, j \in I\) and any pair of arrows \(u : R \to A_i\), \(v : R \to A_j\) with \(f_i u = f_j v\), we also have \(g_i u = g_j v\). When the fiber products \(A_i \times_B A_j\) are representable for \(i, j \in I\), it is the same as saying that the essential image of (10.3.1) is formed of \((g_i)\) such that for any pair of indices \(i, j \in I\), we have \(g_i \mathrm{pr}_1 = g_j \mathrm{pr}_2\), where \(\mathrm{pr}_1, \mathrm{pr}_2\) are both projections of \(A_i \times_B A_j\). The family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\) is said to be anepimorphic family effectiveif the previous condition is verified, i.e. if it is strict epimorphic, and if the fiber products \(A_i \times_B A_j\) are representable.

The family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\) is said to be a universal epimorphic family, (resp. universal effective epimorphic) if the morphisms \(f_i\) are quarable (10.3), and if for any arrow \(B' \to B\), the family of arrows \(f'_i : A'_i \to A_i\times_B B'\), \(i \in I\), deduced from the family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\) by base change \(B' \to B\), is epimorphic (resp. epimorphic effective).

The notion of strict universal epimorphic family will be defined in (II 2.5, 2.6). Note that when the morphisms \(f_i\) are quatable (for example if in \(C\) the products fibers are representable) this notion coincides with that of effective epimorphic family universal (use II 2.4). In the general case, we have between the six variants considered of the notion of epimorphicity the logical implications: \[ \xymatrix@R=1.8em@C=0.8em{ & & \text{épimorphique effective universelle} \ar@{=>}[dll] \ar@{=>}[drr] & & \\ \text{épimorphique effective} \ar@{=>}[ddrrr] & & & & \text{épimorphique stricte universelle} \ar@{=>}[dll] \ar@{=>}[d] \\ & & \text{épimorphique stricte} \ar@{=>}[dr] & & \text{épimorphique universelle} \ar@{=>}[dl] \\ & & & \text{épimorphique} & } \] A morphism \(f : A \to B\) of \(C\) is called an epimorphism (resp. a strict epimorphism, resp. an effective epimorphism, resp. a universal epimorphism, resp. an epimorphism universal workforce, resp. a strict universal epimorphism) if the morphism family reduces to the only element \(f\) is epimorphic (resp. ....).
10.4. Monomorphic family, strict monomorphic etc.

A family of arrows \(f_i : A_i \to B\) of \(C\) is called monomorphic (resp. monomorphic strict, ....) if as a family of arrows of the opposite category \(C^\circ\) it is epimorphic (resp. strict epimorphic, ...). An arrow \(f : A \to B\) of \(C\) is called a monomorphism (resp. a strict monomorphism, ...) if the family reduced to \(f\) is monomorphic (resp. strict monomorphic, ...), i.e. if \(f\) as category arrow opposite \(C^\circ\) is an epimorphism (resp. a strict epimorphism, ....).
10.5. Monomorphism, strict monomorphism etc. See 10.4.
10.6. Projector, image of a projector, direct factor of an object.

Let \(f : X \to X\) be an endomorphism of an object of \(C\). We say that \(f\) is a projector if we have \(f^2 = f\). Then the couple \((f, \mathrm{id}_X)\) admits a representable cokernel \(X'\) if and only if it admits a representable kernel \(X''\), and when this is so, there exists a unique isomorphism \(u : X' \to X''\) in \(C\) such that \(iup = f\), where \(p : X \to X'\) and \(i : X'' \to X\) are the morphisms canonical. We then generally identify \(X'\) and \(X''\), and we call it theprojector image \(f\); we say that the projector \(f\) admits an image if \(\mathrm{Ker}(f, \mathrm{id}_X)\) is representable i.e. \(\mathrm{Coker}(f, \mathrm{id}_X)\) is representable. A sub-object (resp. a quotient) \(Y\) of \(X\) is called a direct factor of \(X\) if we can find a projector \(f\) in \(X\) factoring by \(Y\), and admitting \(Y\) as an image as a sub-object (resp. as a quotient) of \(X\). They say sometimes, by abuse of language, that an object of \(C\) is a direct factor of \(X\), if it is isomorphic like a projector in \(X\).
10.7. Quarable:
An arrow \(f : A \to B\) of \(C\) is said to be quarrable if for any arrow \(B' \to B\) of \(C\), the fiber product \(A\times_B B'\) is representable in \(C\).
10.8. Quotient, strict quotient etc.

Let \(A\) be an object of \(C\). Two epimorphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(f' : A \to B'\) from source \(A\) are called equivalent if there exists an isomorphism \(u : B \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } B'\) such that \(uf = f'\). We obtain thus an equivalence relation in the set of source epimorphisms \(A\), whose classes are called the quotients, or quotient objects, of \(A\). For any quotient of \(A\), we generally supposes chosen an element of this class, i.e. \(f : A \to B\), and we often speak (by misnomer) of the quotient \(B\) of \(A\) (or of the quotient \(f : A \to B\) of \(A\)). It is said that \(B\) is a strict quotient (resp. an effective quotient, resp. a universal quotient, ....) if the morphism \(f : A \to B\) is a strict epimorphism (resp. an effective epimorphism, resp. a universal epimorphism, ...) (10.3).
10.9. Equivalence relationships.

Let \(F, G\) be two pre-sheaves of sets on \(C\). A diagram \(p_1, p_2 : F \rightrightarrows G\) is called an equivalence relation on \(G\) if for any object \(A\) of \(C\), the application \[ (p_1(A), p_2(A)) : F(A) \to G(A)\times G(A) \] induces a bijection of \(F(A)\) on the graph of an equivalence relation on the set \(G(A)\).

A diagram \(p_1, p_2 : B \rightrightarrows C\) in \(C\) is called an equivalence relation on \(C\) if the corresponding pre-sheaf diagram is an equivalence relation. When in \(C\) the finished products and fiber products are representable, a functor \(\widehat{\varphi} : C \to C'\) switching to finite products and fiber products transforms the equivalence relations on \(C\) in equivalence relation on \(\widehat{\varphi}(C)\).

Let \(\pi : C \to D\) be a morphism of \(C\) such that the fiber product \(C\times_D C\) is representable. Then the diagram \(\mathrm{pr}_1, \mathrm{pr}_2 : C\times_D C \rightrightarrows C\) is a relation equivalence on \(C\).
10.10. Effective equivalence relation, universal effective.

An equivalence relation \(p_1, p_2 : R \rightrightarrows A\) on an object \(A\) of \(C\) is called a relation of effective equivalenceif there exists a morphism \(\pi : A \to B\) such as the square \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{p_2} \ar[d]_-{p_1} & A \ar[d]^-{\pi} \\ A \ar[r] & B } \] either Cartesian and Cocatesian. (The morphism \(\pi\) is the cokernel of the pair \((p_1, p_2)\), and is therefore determined up to a single isomorphism). The morphism \(\pi\) is then an epimorphism effective; if it is a universal effective epimorphism (10.3) we say that theequivalence relation is effectively universal.
10.11. Subobject, strict subobject etc.
These are dual notions of those of quotient, strict quotient etc (10.8).
BIBLIOGRAPHY
B. Mitchell, Theory of categories, Academic Press (1965).
Appendix: Universe (by N. Bourbaki∗)

footnotes.html: (∗) We reproduce here, with his agreement, secret papers of N. BOURBAKI. The references in this text relate to his scholarly work.

pages.title: 1. Definition and first properties of universes

blocks.html: 1. Definition and first properties of universes

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

A set \(\mathcal{U}\) is called a universe if it satisfies the conditions:

  1. (\(\mathcal{U}\).I)

    if \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) and if \(y \in x\), SO \(y \in \mathcal{U}\) ;

  2. (\(\mathcal{U}\).II)

    if \(x, y \in \mathcal{U}\), SO \(\{x,y\} \in \mathcal{U}\) ;

  3. (\(\mathcal{U}\).III)

    if \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), SO \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}\) ;

  4. (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV)

    if \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is a family of elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), and if \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), then the meeting \(\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha\) belongs to \(\mathcal{U}\) ;

  5. (\(\mathcal{U}\).?)

    if \(x, y \in \mathcal{U}\), then the couple \((x,y) \in \mathcal{U}\).

N.B. As I believe was decided for the next editions, we define the couple according to Kuratowski by \((x,y) = \{\{x,y\}, \{x\}\}\), the condition (\(\mathcal{U}\).?) is unnecessary because it results from (\(\mathcal{U}\).II).
Examples.
1) The empty set is a universe denoted \(\mathcal{U}_0\).

2) Consider the finite non-empty words formed with the four symbols “\(\{\)”, “\(\}\)”, “,” and “\(\varnothing\)” (see Alg. I). Let us define, by induction over the length \(n\) of such a word, the notion of significant word:
a) For \(n = 1\), only the word \(\varnothing\) is significant;

b) for a word \(A\) of length \(n\) to be significant, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(p\) words distinct significant \(A_1, \ldots, A_p\) (\(p \geq 1\)) of lengths \(< n\) such that \[ A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_p\} . \] For example \(\varnothing, \{\varnothing\}, \{\{\varnothing\}\}, \{\varnothing, \{\varnothing\}\}\) are significant words. It is clear, by induction over length, that every significant word designates a term of the Set Theory; for example, if \(A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_p\}\), \(A\) denotes the set whose elements are \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\), and \(A_p\); these terms are obviously finite sets. Let \(\mathcal{U}_1\) be the set of sets thus obtained. We easily verify that \(\mathcal{U}_1\) satisfies the conditions (\(\mathcal{U}\).I), (\(\mathcal{U}\).II), (\(\mathcal{U}\).III) and (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV) of def. 1 (but no to that idiot (\(\mathcal{U}\).?), which doesn't matter if we want to decannulate the couple). So \(\mathcal{U}_1\) is a universe. Note that the elements of \(\mathcal{U}_1\) are finite, and that \(\mathcal{U}_1\) is countable.
In the following statements, \(\mathcal{U}\) designates a universe.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1

If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) and if \(y \subset x\), then \(y \in \mathcal{U}\).
Indeed, we have \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).III), hence \(y \in \mathfrak{P}(x)\) and \(y \in \mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).I).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), every quotient set \(y\) of \(x\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
Indeed \(y\) is a part of \(\mathfrak{P}(x)\). Hence \(y \in \mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).III) and the prop. 1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2

If \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), we have \(\{x\} \in \mathcal{U}\).
This results from (\(\mathcal{U}\).II) applied for \(y = x\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3

Every pair, every triplet, every quadruplet of elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) is a element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
This is true for couples according to the N. B. or (\(\mathcal{U}\).?). We deduce the case of triples because \((x,y,z) = ((x,y),z)\), then that of quadruplets because \((x,y,z,t) = ((x,y,z),t)\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4

If \(X, Y \in \mathcal{U}\), then \(X \times Y \in \mathcal{U}\).

Indeed, for \(x \in X\), \(\{x\} \times Y\) is the reunion of the \(\{(x,y)\}_{y\in Y}\) family; as we have \(\{(x,y)\} \in \mathcal{U}\) according to (\(\mathcal{U}\).I), (\(\mathcal{U}\).II) and prop. 3, we have \(\{x\} \times Y \in \mathcal{U}\) according to (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV). Finally \(X \times Y\) is the family reunion \((\{x\} \times Y)_{x\in X}\); it is therefore an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) according to (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV) again.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

If \(X, Y, Z, \ldots\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), all sets of the scale built on \(X, Y, Z, \ldots\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (see chap. IV, § ).
This indeed results from successive applications of the prop. 4 and (\(\mathcal{U}\).III).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

If \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is an element family of \(\mathcal{U}\) and if \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), the sum set \(\sum_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Indeed, this sum set is a part of the product \((\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha) \times I\) (chap. II), product whose two factors are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (by (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV) and the hypothesis). We apply then the props. 4 and 1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5

If \(X\) and \(Y\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), any match between \(X\) and \(Y\) (in particular any application of \(X\) in \(Y\)) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Indeed, such a match \(C\) is a triple \((X, Y, \Gamma)\) where \(\Gamma\) is a part of \(X \times Y\) (the graph of \(C\)) (chap. II, § ). We have \(\Gamma \in \mathcal{U}\) according to the props. 4 and 1. Hence \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) by prop. 3.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6

If \(X, Y \in \mathcal{U}\), any set \(Z\) of matches between \(X\) and \(Y\) (in particular applications of \(X\) in \(Y\)) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Indeed, \(Z\) is a part of \(\{X\} \times \{Y\} \times \mathfrak{P}(X \times Y)\). But this product is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) according to prop. 2 and the horn. to prop. 4. We therefore have \(Z \in \mathcal{U}\) by prop. 1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is a family of elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), and if \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), we have \(\prod_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha \in \mathcal{U}\).
Indeed, this product is a set of applications of \(I\) in \(\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha\), and this meeting is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7

If \(X\) is a part of \(\mathcal{U}\) whose cardinality is at most that of one element of \(\mathcal{U}\), then \(X\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Let \(I\) be an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(\mathrm{card}(X) \leq \mathrm{card}(I)\). We have a surjection \(i \mapsto x_i\) of a part \(I'\) of \(I\) on \(X\). Then \(X\) is the family reunion \((\{x_i\})_{i\in I'}\); but this meeting is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by prop. 1, prop. 2 and (\(\mathcal{U}\).IV).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If \(\mathcal{U}\) is non-empty any finite part of \(\mathcal{U}\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) has elements of arbitrary finite cardinality.
On the other hand, if \(\mathcal{U} = \varnothing\), \(\varnothing\) is a finite part of \(\varnothing\) but not an element of \(\varnothing\).

Indeed, if \(\mathcal{U}\) is non-empty, the prop. 2 shows that a one-element set \(x_0\) belongs to \(\mathcal{U}\). By induction on \(n\), let us set \(x_{n+1} = \mathfrak{P}(x_n)\). We have \(x_n \in \mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).III), and \(\mathrm{card}(x_{n+1}) = 2^{\mathrm{card}(x_n)}\), so that \(\mathcal{U}\) has arbitrarily finite cardinal elements large.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

It follows from prop. 2 and horn. to prop. 7 that any non-empty universe \(\mathcal{U}\) contains the universe \(\mathcal{U}_1\) of ex. 2; in fact we have \(\varnothing \in \mathcal{U}\) according to the prop. 1. So \(\mathcal{U}_1\) is the intersection of all non-empty universes. More generally:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8

If \((\mathcal{U}_\lambda)_{\lambda\in L}\) is a non-empty universe family, then \(\mathcal{U} = \bigcap_{\lambda\in L} \mathcal{U}_\lambda\) is a universe.
This immediately follows from the def. 1.
2. Universe and types of structures

Let \(\mathcal{E}\) be a kind of structure; suppose, to fix the ideas and lighten the presentation, that each species structure \(\mathcal{E}\) is defined on a basis set. Let \((X,S)\) be a structure of species \(\mathcal{E}\) (\(X\) being the basic set, and \(S\) the structure), and let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. If \(X \in \mathcal{U}\), then all the objects constituting the structure \(S\) on \(X\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (by the horn. 1 of prop. 4, n° 1 and by (\(\mathcal{U}\).I)) so that the structure \((X,S)\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Now suppose that \(\mathcal{E}\) is a kind of structure with morphisms. \(X\) and \(X'\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) provided with species structures \(\mathcal{E}\), then the set of morphisms of \(X\) in \(X'\) is still an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) (n° 1, prop. 6).

Let us then consider the category \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) defined in § 1, no. 2, ex. c). Like the objects and the arrows of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) is a pair of parts of \(\mathcal{U}\), equipped with a quadruplet of applications; it is not, in general, an element of \(\mathcal{U}\). The notation \(X \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) will mean that \(X\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) with a species structure \(\mathcal{E}\).
The \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) category is stable for many operations:
a) If \(X \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\), and if \(X'\) is a part of \(X\) which admits an induced structure, then \(X' \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\). This follows from prop. 1, no. 1.
b) If \(X \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\), and if \(X''\) is a quotient set of \(X\) which admits a structure quotient, then \(X'' \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) (cor. of prop. 1, n°1).

c) If \(X, Y \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\), and if \(X \times Y\) admits a product structure, then \(X \times Y \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) (prop. 4, n°1). More generally, if \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) is a family of elements of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\), if we have \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), and if \(\prod_{i\in I} X_i\) admits a product structure, then \(\prod_{i\in I} X_i \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) (cor. of prop. 6). Analogous assertions for sum structures (cor. 2 of prop. 4).

d) Let \(I\) be a preordered set, and \((X_i, f_{ij})_{i,j\in I}\) be a projective system (resp. inductive) of sets with species structures \(\mathcal{E}\) and morphisms; or \(L\) the limit of this system, within the meaning of chap. III. If \(X_i \in \mathcal{U}\) for all \(i\), if \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), and if \(L\) admits a projective limit structure (resp. inductive), then we have \(L \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\): in fact \(L\) is a part of \(\prod_{i\in I} X_i\) (resp. a quotient set of \(\sum_{i\in I} X_i\)), and is therefore an element by \(\mathcal{U}\) from n° 1.
Let's give a few more specific examples:

1) Let \(X, Y \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Top})\) be two locally compact spaces. So the whole \(\mathcal{C}(X,Y)\) continuous applications of \(X\) in \(Y\), provided with the convergence topology compact (Top. Gen. X), is an element of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Top})\). Indeed we have \(\mathcal{C}(X,Y) \in \mathcal{U}\) according to the prop. 6 of No. 1.

2) Let \(X, Y \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Ab})\). Then the group \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}}(X,Y)\) is an element of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Ab})\) by prop. 6 of n° 1. If we further suppose that we have \(\mathbb{Z} \in \mathcal{U}\), the tensor product \(X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} Y\) is an element of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Ab})\); in fact this tensor product is a quotient of \(\mathbb{Z}^{(X \times Y)}\), which is a part of \(\mathbb{Z}^{X \times Y}\), which itself is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by the props. 4 and 6 of n° 1.

3) Let \(X \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Unif})\) be a uniform space. Then its completed \(\widehat{X}\) is an element of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Unif})\). Indeed \(\widehat{X}\) is a set of equivalence classes of Cauchy filters on \(X\); or a filter on \(X\) is an element of \(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(X))\), therefore an equivalence class of filters is an element of \(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(X)))\), so that \(\widehat{X}\) is an element of \(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(X))))\), therefore an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).III) and (\(\mathcal{U}\).I).

N.B. Morality, Bourbaki must ensure that his constructions are “canonized”. By example, in those where we add an element \(\infty\) (compactified of Alexandroff, body projective), it will be interesting to take \(\infty = \varnothing\) (because \(\varnothing\) is an element of any universe not empty) and to form the sum set of \(\{\varnothing\}\) and the given set. Of course it would be necessary also “canonify” the two-element set used to construct sum sets; thus \(\{\varnothing, \{\varnothing\}\}\) seems to me a good candidate because it belongs to any non-empty universe.
3. Universe and categories

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(C\) be a category. We write \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) if we have \(\mathrm{Ob}(C) \in \mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}(C) \in \mathcal{U}\). This writing is justified by the fact that \(C\) is the sextuplet formed of \(\mathrm{Ob}(C)\), of \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)\) and the four structural applications; so if \(\mathrm{Ob}(C) \in \mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}(C) \in \mathcal{U}\), these four applications are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (n° 1, cor. 1 of prop. 4), hence also the sextuplet \(C\) (No. 1, prop. 3, cum grano salis). This is also a special case of No. 2, if we consider the category “cat” structure species. With the notations of #2, the relation \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) is also written \(C \in (\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{cat})\).

Note that a category like \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Ens})\), \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Ord})\), \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Top})\) or \((\mathcal{U} - \mathrm{Gr})\) is not, in general, an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).

Let \(C, D\) be two categories and \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe such that \(C, D \in \mathcal{U}\). If \(C'\) is a subcategory of \(C\), we have \(C' \in \mathcal{U}\); the product category \(C \times D\), the sum category of \(C\) and \(D\), the opposite categories \(C^\circ\) and \(D^\circ\) are also elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) (see n° 2). The functor category \(E = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(C,D)\) is also an element of \(\mathcal{U}\): indeed \(\mathrm{Ob}(E)\) is a set of pairs of applications \(\mathrm{Ob}(C) \to \mathrm{Ob}(D)\), \(\mathrm{Fl}(C) \to \mathrm{Fl}(D)\), so \(\mathrm{Ob}(E) \in \mathcal{U}\) by #1; as for \(\mathrm{Fl}(E)\), it is a set of functorial morphisms, that is to say applications \(\mathrm{Ob}(C) \to \mathrm{Fl}(D)\), hence \(\mathrm{Fl}(E) \in \mathcal{U}\) by prop. 6 of No. 1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9

Let \(\mathcal{E}\) be one kind of structure with morphisms, and \(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{U}'\) two universes such as \(\mathcal{U}' \subset \mathcal{U}\). Then \((\mathcal{U}' - \mathcal{E})\) is a full subcategory of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\).

Indeed, if \(x\) and \(y\) are objects of \((\mathcal{U}' - \mathcal{E})\), they are by definition objects of \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\). As for \(\mathrm{Hom}(x,y)\), it is the same set in both categories (cf. §1, n°2, e.g. c)).
N.B. Note that the hypothesis that \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(\mathcal{U}'\) are universes is useless, so that the prop. 9 would advantageously go back to § 1, no. 4. But the Tribe asked that it be here.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

It happens that the axioms of the kind of structure \(\mathcal{E}\) imply that the cardinals of sets with species structures \(\mathcal{E}\) are bounded by a cardinal fixed, i.e. \(c\) (for example the kind of structure of finite group, of group of finite type, of finite type module on a fixed ring \(A\), or finite type algebra on \(A\)). Suppose then that there exists an element \(z\) of \(\mathcal{U}'\) such that \(c \leq \mathrm{card}(z)\). So, for any set \(x\) equipped of a species structure \(\mathcal{E}\), there exists an element \(x'\) of \(\mathcal{U}'\) equipotent to \(x\), for example a part of \(z\); equip \(x'\) with the structure deduced from that of \(x\) by structure transport; we thus obtain an element of \((\mathcal{U}' - \mathcal{E})\). It follows that the inclusion functor of \((\mathcal{U}' - \mathcal{E})\) in \((\mathcal{U} - \mathcal{E})\) is then essentially surjective (§4, n°2, def.2) and is therefore an equivalence of category (§4, n°3, th.1).
4. The axiom of universes

The very pleasant stability results of No. 2 are only of interest if they can be apply to something other than the two small universes \(\mathcal{U}_0\) and \(\mathcal{U}_1\) described in No. 1. We We will therefore add to the axioms of Set Theory the following axiom:
(A.6) For every set \(x\), there exists a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(x \in \mathcal{U}\).

This axiom implies that, if \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is a family of sets, there exists a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(x_\alpha \in \mathcal{U}\) for all \(\alpha \in I\): it is sufficient, in fact, to apply (A.6) to \(x = \bigcup_{\alpha\in I} x_\alpha\) and apply prop. 1 of n°1.

In particular, given a category \(C\), there exists a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(C \in \mathcal{U}\) at meaning of n°3: we apply the above to the family \((\mathrm{Ob}(C), \mathrm{Fl}(C))\). This applies to categories of the form \((V - \mathcal{E})\) where \(\mathcal{E}\) is a kind of structure with morphisms and \(V\) a together, a universe for example; in general we have \(V \neq \mathcal{U}\).

For example, if \(V\) is a universe, we have \(\mathrm{Ob}(V - \mathrm{Ens}) = V\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}(V - \mathrm{Ens}) \subset V\) (n° 1, prop. 5). The universes \(\mathcal{U}\) such as \((V - \mathrm{Ens}) \in \mathcal{U}\) are therefore those such as \(V \in \mathcal{U}\). But the relation \(V \in V\) is impossible for a universe: in fact, for any part \(A\) of \(V\), we would have \(A \in V\) (n° 1, prop. 1), hence \(\mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{P}(V)) \leq \mathrm{card}(V)\), which is impossible.
5. Highly inaccessible universe and cardinals

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. According to the condition (\(\mathcal{U}\).I) of the def. 1 any element \(x\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) is part of \(\mathcal{U}\); we therefore have \(\mathrm{card}(x) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\). Like the cardinals of the parts of \(\mathcal{U}\) form a well-ordered whole, the cardinal \[ \underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) = \sup_{x\in\mathcal{U}} \mathrm{card}(x) \tag{1} \] exists. Note that, for any cardinal \(\underline{c} < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\), there exists an element \(x\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(\mathrm{card}(x) = \underline{c}\); indeed, there exists by definition \(y \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(\underline{c} \leq \mathrm{card}(y) \leq \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\), and we take for \(x\) a suitable part of \(y\). Conversely, if \(x \in \mathcal{U}\), we have \(\mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\); in fact we have \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}\) by (\(\mathcal{U}\).III), hence \(2^{\mathrm{card}(x)} \leq \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\). The cardinal \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) therefore has the following properties:

1) If \(\underline{c}\) is a cardinal \(< \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\), we have \(2^{\underline{c}} < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\); indeed, if \(x\) is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\) of cardinal \(\underline{c}\), we have \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}\) by application of (\(\mathcal{U}\).III), hence \(2^{\underline{c}} < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\).
