2) If \((\underline{c}_\lambda)_{\lambda\in I}\) is a family of cardinals such that \(\underline{c}_\lambda < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) for all \(\lambda \in I\) and \(\mathrm{card}(I) < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\), then the cardinal sum \(\sum_{\lambda\in I} \underline{c}_\lambda\) is \(< \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\). Indeed let \(x_\lambda \in \mathcal{U}\) such that \(\mathrm{card}(x_\lambda) = \underline{c}_\lambda\); even if it means replacing \(I\) by a set of equipotent indices, we can assume that we have \(I \in \mathcal{U}\); then the whole sum of \(x_\lambda\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) (cor. 2 of prop. 4 of n°1), which demonstrates our assertion.
Let's make the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2

A cardinal \(\underline{d}\) is said to be highly inaccessible if:

(FI.1) If \(\underline{c}\) is a cardinal such that \(\underline{c} < \underline{d}\), we have \(2^{\underline{c}} < \underline{d}\).

(FI.2) If \((\underline{c}_\lambda)_{\lambda\in I}\) is a family of cardinals such that \(\underline{c}_\lambda < \underline{d}\) for all \(\lambda \in I\) and if \(\mathrm{card}(I) < \underline{d}\), then \(\sum_{\lambda\in I} \underline{c}_\lambda < \underline{d}\).

Examples. Cardinal 0 and countably infinite cardinal are strongly inaccessible. No finite non-zero cardinal is strongly inaccessible. Thus we have just demonstrated that the axiom (A.6) of universes implies the relation:
(A'.6) Every cardinal is strictly increased by a strongly inaccessible cardinal.
Conversely:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

The relation (A'.6) implies the axiom (A.6) of the universes.

Indeed, let \(A\) be a set. It is about building a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) of which \(A\) is an element. Let us define by induction a sequence \((A_n)_{n\geq 0}\) of sets using: \[ A_0 = A , \quad A_{n+1} = \text{réunion des éléments de } A_n = \text{ensemble des éléments de } A_n . \tag{2} \] Let's set \(B = \bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n\). Let, by (A'.6), \(\underline{c}\) be a strongly inaccessible cardinal such that \(\mathrm{card}(B) < \underline{c}\).

There exists a well-ordered set \(I\) such that \(\mathrm{card}(I) = \underline{c}\). Even if it means replacing \(I\) with its smallest segment of cardinal \(\underline{c}\), we can assume thatany segment of \(I\) distinct from \(I\) has a cardinal \(< \underline{c}\).

We will note \(\varepsilon\) the smallest element of \(I\). It results from the hypothesis on the segments that \(I\) has no largest element (otherwise it would be removed), so that any element of \(I\) admits a successor; for \(\alpha \in I\), we will note \(s(\alpha)\) the successor of \(\alpha\).

This being said, let us define, by transfinite recurrence, a family \((B_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) of sets at means of: \[ \begin{cases} B_\varepsilon = B \\ B_{s(\alpha)} = B_\alpha \cup \mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha) \\ B_\alpha = \bigcup_{\beta<\alpha} B_\beta \text{ si } \alpha \text{ n'a pas de prédécesseur}. \end{cases} \tag{3} \] Let's set \(\mathcal{U} = \bigcup_{\alpha\in I} B_\alpha\). We will show that \(\mathcal{U}\) is the universe sought.
We first have \(A \in \mathcal{U}\), because \(A = A_0\) is a part of \(B = B_\varepsilon\), therefore an element of \(\mathfrak{P}(B_\varepsilon) \subset B_{s(\varepsilon)}\).

Let us then note that, for all \(\alpha \in I\), we have: \[ \mathrm{card}(B_\alpha) < \underline{c} . \tag{4} \] Let us proceed by transfinite induction on \(\alpha\). This is true for \(\alpha = \varepsilon\) by hypothesis. From (4) we deduce \(\mathrm{card}(B_{s(\alpha)}) \leq \mathrm{card}(B_\alpha) + 2^{\mathrm{card}(B_\alpha)} \leq \underline{c} + \underline{c}\) (by (FI.1)) \(= \underline{c}\) (because \(\underline{c}\) is infinite). Finally, if \(\alpha\) has no predecessor, the set \(I'\) of \(\beta < \alpha\) has a cardinal \(< \underline{c}\); hence (if \(\mathrm{card}(B_\beta) < \underline{c}\) for all \(\beta < \alpha\)), \(\mathrm{card}(B_\alpha) = \mathrm{card}(\bigcup_{\beta\in I'} B_\beta) \leq \sum_{\beta\in I'} \mathrm{card}(B_\beta) < \underline{c}\) (by (FI.2)). This demonstrates (4).

Let us now show that we have \[ \mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c} \text{ pour tout } x \in \mathcal{U} . \tag{5} \] Indeed, it suffices to show that, for all \(\alpha \in I\), we have “\(\mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c}\) for all \(x \in B_\alpha\)”. Let us again proceed by transfinite induction on \(\alpha\). This is true for \(\alpha = \varepsilon\), because, if \(x \in B_\varepsilon = B\), there exists \(n \geq 0\) such that \(x \in A_n\); hence \(x \subset A_{n+1}\) by (2), \(x \subset B\) and \(\mathrm{card}(x) \leq \mathrm{card}(B) < \underline{c}\). The case where \(\alpha\) has no predecessor is obvious. Finally, if \(x \in B_\alpha\) and if \(\alpha = s(\beta)\), we have either \(x \in B_\beta\) and the assertion “\(\mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c}\)” is true by induction, i.e. \(x \in \mathfrak{P}(B_\beta)\), hence \(x \subset B_\beta\) and the assertion “\(\mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c}\)” is true by (4).
We are now able to demonstrate that \(\mathcal{U}\) is indeed a universe:

(\(\mathcal{U}\).I) Let \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) and \(y \in x\). This is about showing that we \(y \in \mathcal{U}\). In other words he is to show that, for all \(\alpha \in I\), we have the relation: \[ \text{«}\, x \in B_\alpha \text{ et } y \in x \implies y \in B_\alpha \,\text{»} . \] We still proceed by transfinite induction on \(\alpha\). This is true for \(\alpha = \varepsilon\) because \(x \in B_\varepsilon = B\) implies \(x \in A_n\) for some \(n\); therefore, if \(y \in x\), we have \(y \in A_{n+1}\), hence \(y \in B = B_\varepsilon\). The transition to an element \(\alpha\) without a predecessor is obvious. Finally let's move from \(\alpha\) to \(s(\alpha)\): if \(x \in B_{s(\alpha)}\) and if \(y \in x\), we have, either \(x \in B_\alpha\), hence \(y \in B_\alpha \subset B_{s(\alpha)}\) by recurrence, i.e. \(x \in \mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha)\), hence again \(y \in B_\alpha \subset B_{s(\alpha)}\).

(\(\mathcal{U}\).II) Let \(x, y \in \mathcal{U}\). As the family \((B_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is increasing by (3), there exists \(\alpha \in I\) such as \(x, y \in B_\alpha\). So \(\{x, y\} \in \mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha) \subset B_{s(\alpha)} \subset \mathcal{U}\).

(\(\mathcal{U}\).III) Or \(x \in \mathcal{U}\). There then exists \(\alpha \in I\) such that \(x \in B_\alpha\). It is therefore enough to show that, for all \(\alpha \in I\), we have the relation: \[ \text{«}\, x \in B_\alpha \implies \mathfrak{P}(x) \in B_{s(s(\alpha))} \,\text{»} . \] We still proceed by transfinite induction on \(\alpha\). If \(\alpha = \varepsilon\), we have \(x \in A_n\) for a certain \(n\), hence \(y \in A_{n+1} \subset B = B_\varepsilon\) for any part \(y\) of \(x\); thus we have \(y \in \mathfrak{P}(B_\varepsilon) \subset B_{s(\varepsilon)}\) for all \(y \in \mathfrak{P}(x)\), hence \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \subset B_{s(\varepsilon)}\) and \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathfrak{P}(B_{s(\varepsilon)}) \subset B_{s(s(\varepsilon))}\), so our assertion is true for \(\alpha = \varepsilon\). The transition to an element \(\alpha\) without a predecessor is immediate, because \(\beta < \alpha\) then implies \(s(s(\beta)) < \alpha\). Finally let's move from \(\alpha\) to \(s(\alpha)\); either \(x \in B_{s(\alpha)}\); if \(x \in B_\alpha\), we have \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in B_{s(s(\alpha))} \subset B_{s(s(s(\alpha)))}\) by recurrence; if \(x \in \mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha)\), we have \(x \subset B_\alpha\), hence \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \subset \mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha)\) and \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathfrak{P}(\mathfrak{P}(B_\alpha)) \subset B_{s(s(s(\alpha)))}\).

(\(\mathcal{U}\).IV) Let \((x_\lambda)_{\lambda\in K}\) be a family of elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(K \in \mathcal{U}\). It is about show that the meeting \(x = \bigcup_{\lambda\in K} x_\lambda\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\). For all \(\lambda \in K\), let's choose a \(\alpha(\lambda) \in I\) such that \(x_\lambda \in B_{\alpha(\lambda)}\). Let us show that the set \(\alpha(K)\) of \(\alpha(\lambda)\) is increased in \(I\): in fact, if it were not, we would have \[ I = \bigcup_{\lambda\in K} [\varepsilon, \alpha(\lambda)] , \] which would contradict (FI.2) because \(\mathrm{card}(K) < \underline{c}\) and \(\mathrm{card}([\varepsilon, \alpha(\lambda)]) < \underline{c}\) for all \(\lambda \in K\). Let \(\beta\) be an upper bound of \(\alpha(K)\); we have \(x_\lambda \in B_\beta\) for everything \(\lambda \in K\) because the family \((B_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is increasing. We therefore have \(x_\lambda \subset B_\beta\) according to what we saw in the demonstration of (\(\mathcal{U}\).I), hence \(x = \bigcup_{\lambda\in K} x_\lambda \subset B_\beta\). By (3) it follows that we have \(x \in B_{s(\beta)}\), hence \(x \in \mathcal{U}\). C.Q.F.D.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3

Let \(x, y\) be two sets and \(n\) be an integer \(\geq 0\). We say that \(y\) is a order component \(n\) of \(x\) if there exists a sequence \((x_j)_{j=0,\ldots,n}\) such as \(x_0 = x\), \(x_n = y\), and \(x_{j+1} \in x_j\) for \(j = 0, \ldots, n-1\).

Thus \(x\) is the only component of order 0 of \(x\). The order 1 (resp. 2) components of \(x\) are the elements of \(x\) (resp. the elements of the elements of \(x\)). We say that \(y\) is a component of \(x\) if there exists \(n \geq 0\) such that \(y\) is component of order \(n\) of \(x\). The relationship “\(y\) is a component of \(x\)” is a preorder relation. According to the diagram of selection-meeting (chap. II, ...) the relation “\(y\) is a component of \(x\)” is collectivizing by relative to \(y\), so that the components of \(x\) form a set.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4

Let \(\underline{c}\) be a cardinal. We say that a set \(x\) is of type \(\underline{c}\) (resp. of strict type \(\underline{c}\), of finite type) if all the components of \(x\) have cardinals \(\leq \underline{c}\) (resp. \(< \underline{c}\), finished).

Examples. The elements of the universe \(\mathcal{U}_1\) (n° 1, ex. 2) are all of finite type. If \(\underline{c}\) is a cardinal, and if \(x\) is of type \(\underline{c}\) (resp. strict type \(\underline{c}\), finite type), then any component of \(x\) and any part of \(x\) are of type \(\underline{c}\) (resp. strict type \(\underline{c}\), finite type); likewise \(\mathfrak{P}(x)\) is of type \(2^{\underline{c}}\) (resp. strict type \(2^{\underline{c}}\), finite type). If \(x\) is of type \(\underline{c}\) and if \(\underline{c} \leq \underline{c}'\), then \(x\) is of type \(\underline{c}'\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma

Let \(\underline{c}\) be a highly inaccessible non-countable cardinal, and \(X\) be a strict type set \(\underline{c}\). Then there exists a cardinal \(\underline{d} < \underline{c}\) such that \(X\) is of type \(\underline{d}\).

Indeed, for any integer \(n\), let us denote \(\underline{d}_n\) the cardinality of the set \(X_n\) of the components of order \(n\) of \(X\). We have \(\underline{d}_0 = 1\), \(\underline{d}_1 = \mathrm{card}(X) < \underline{c}\), and, like \(X_n = \bigcup_{y\in X_{n-1}} y\), we deduce, by induction on \(n\) and use of (FI.2), that we have \(\underline{d}_n < \underline{c}\) for all \(n\). So the \(\underline{d}_n\) are increased by the cardinal \(\underline{d} = \sum_{j\geq 0} \underline{d}_j\), which is \(< \underline{c}\) by (FI.2) and the non-countability hypothesis of \(\underline{c}\). Then, if \(Y\) is a component of order \(n\) of \(X\), we have \(\mathrm{card}(Y) \leq \mathrm{card}(X_{n+1}) \leq \underline{d}\). C.Q.F.D.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 10

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(\underline{c}\) be a strongly inaccessible cardinal. So the set \(\mathcal{U}'\) of \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) which are of strict type \(\underline{c}\) is a universe.

Indeed, if \(x, y \in \mathcal{U}'\) and if \(z \in x\), we obviously have \(z \in \mathcal{U}'\) and \(\{x, y\} \in \mathcal{U}'\). If \(x \in \mathcal{U}'\), we have \(\mathrm{card}(x) < \underline{c}\), hence \(\mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{P}(x)) < \underline{c}\) by (FI.1); as a component of \(\mathfrak{P}(x)\) is either \(\mathfrak{P}(x)\), or a part of \(x\), or a component of \(x\), we have \(\mathfrak{P}(x) \in \mathcal{U}'\). Finally if \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) is a family of elements of \(\mathcal{U}'\) such that \(I \in \mathcal{U}'\), we have \(\mathrm{card}(\bigcup_\alpha x_\alpha) < \underline{c}\) by (FI.2); as any component of order \(> 0\) of \(\bigcup_\alpha x_\alpha\) is a component of a \(x_\alpha\), we have \(\bigcup_\alpha x_\alpha \in \mathcal{U}'\). C.Q.F.D.

Remark on the cardinal \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\). Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) be the cardinal defined by (1), i.e. \[ \underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) = \sup_{x\in\mathcal{U}} \mathrm{card}(x) . \] We obviously have \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\), because, remember, \(x \in \mathcal{U}\) implies \(x \subset \mathcal{U}\). But equality \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) = \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\) is not always true. For example, let \((x_\alpha)_{\alpha\in I}\) be a family uncountable of symbols with the axioms: \[ \text{«}\, x_\alpha = \{x_\alpha\} \text{ pour tout } \alpha \in I \,\text{»} , \quad \text{«}\, x_\alpha \neq x_\beta \text{ si } \alpha \neq \beta \,\text{»} \] (in other words \(x_\alpha\) is a set with only one element, namely itself). Let's form, like in the ex. 2 of No. 1, the “significant words” formed with the symbols \(\varnothing, x_\alpha, \{, \}\) and , . Each designates a finite set. Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be all of these. It is easy to verify that the conditions of the def. 1 are satisfied, so that \(\mathcal{U}\) is a universe. Like the words significant are finite sequences of elements of a set of cardinal \(\mathrm{card}(I) + 4 = \mathrm{card}(I)\); we have \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}) = \mathrm{card}(I)\) (chap. III; in fact \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}) \geq \mathrm{card}(I)\) is enough, and it is obvious). On the other hand, \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) is the countable cardinal, hence \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) < \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\).

The strict inequality \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) < \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\) is due to the fact that we introduced sets \(x_\alpha\) here. such as \(x_\alpha \in x_\alpha\). If we ban horrors like this, we get \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}) = \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) for any universe \(\mathcal{U}\), as well as other very nice results "that cannot be of no use.” That's what we're going to do in the next issue.
6. Artinian sets and universes

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5

We say that a set \(x\) is Artinian if there exists no infinite sequence \((x_n)_{n\geq 0}\) such that \(x_0 = x\) and that \(x_{n+1} \in x_n\) for all \(n \geq 0\).
Examples. The sets \(\varnothing, \{\varnothing\}, \{\varnothing, \{\varnothing\}\}\), more generally the elements of the universe \(\mathcal{U}_1\) of n° 1, are artinian.

In pictorial terms, if \(x\) is Artinian, and if we take an element \(x_1\) from \(x\), then an element \(x_2\) of \(x_1\), etc., the process must stop, and we arrive at a component \(x_n\) of \(x\) which is empty. In other words, the Artinian sets are “constructed from \(\varnothing\)”; this will be specified later (cf. (*) in the demonstration of th. 2).
Artinian sets obviously enjoy the following properties:
(AR.I) If \(x\) is Artinian, any part of \(x\) and any component of \(x\) are Artinian. So that \(x\) is Artinian, it is necessary and sufficient that any element of \(x\) be Artinian.
(AR.II) If \(x\) and \(y\) are Artinian, then \(\{x,y\}\) is Artinian.
(AR.III) If \(x\) is Artinian, \(\mathfrak{P}(x)\) is Artinian.
(AR.IV) Any meeting of Artinian sets is an Artinian set.
These properties immediately show that we have the

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 11

If \(\mathcal{U}\) is a universe, the set of Artinian elements of \(\mathcal{U}\) is a universe, necessarily Artinian.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

If \(x\) is an Artinian set, there exists an Artinian universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(x \in \mathcal{U}\).
Indeed, by axiom (A.6), \(x\) is an element of a universe \(V\); we take for \(\mathcal{U}\) the whole Artinian elements of \(V\).
The following proposition is even less useful than the rest of the issue:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 12

Let \(A\) be an Artinian set. So:

a) For all \(x \in A\), we have \(x \notin x\);

b) If \(x, y \in A\), we cannot have both \(x \in y\) and \(y \in x\);

c) For all \(x \in A\), the relation "\(y\) is a component of \(z\)" between components \(y, z\) of \(x\) is an order relation;

d) For any non-empty element \(x\) of \(A\), there exists \(y \in x\) such that \(x \cap y = \varnothing\).

Indeed the negation of a) (resp. of b)) leads to the existence of an infinite sequence \((x_n)_{n\geq 0}\) contradicting the def. 5, namely \((x, x, x, \ldots)\) (resp. \((x, y, x, y, x, y, \ldots)\)). The negation of c) means that there exist \(x \in A\), distinct \(y, z\) components of \(x\), and sequences memberships: \[ y \in y_1 \in \ldots \in y_q \in z , \quad z \in z_1 \in \ldots \in z_r \in y ; \] hence, as in b), an infinite sequence \((x_n)_{n\geq 0}\) contradicting the def. 5. Finally, if d) is false, there exists a non-empty element \(x\) of \(A\) such that \(x \cap y \neq \varnothing\) for all \(y \in x\); we poses \(x_0 = x\), and we take for \(x_1\) an element of \(x\); like \(x \cap x_1 \neq \varnothing\), we take for \(x_2\) an element of \(x \cap x_1\) etcetera; more formally we define by recurrence a sequence infinite \((x_n)_{n\geq 1}\) of elements of \(x\) using \(x_1 \in x\), \(x_{n+1} \in x_n \cap x\) for \(n \geq 1\); then the continuation \((x_n)_{n\geq 0}\) contradicts the def. 5 .

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

Let \(B\) be a set. For \(B\) to be Artinian, it is necessary and sufficient that everything set \(A\) of sets of components of \(B\) satisfies condition d) of prop. 12. In indeed the necessity results from (AR.I) and from prop. 12. Conversely, if \(B\) is not Artinian, there exists an infinite sequence \((x_n)_{n\geq 0}\) with \(x_0 = B\) and \(x_{n+1} \in x_n\) for all \(n \geq 0\); we then takes for \(A\) the part reduced to the set \(X\) of \(x_n\); so \(A\) contains an element non-empty \(X\) such that, for any element \(y\) of \(X\), we have \(y \cap X \neq \varnothing\) (indeed \(y\) is the form \(x_n\), and we have \(x_{n+1} \in y \cap X\)).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2

Let \(\underline{c}\) be an infinite cardinal. So:

a) The relation “\(x\) is an Artinian set of type \(\underline{c}\) (resp. of strict type \(\underline{c}\))” is collectivizing in relation to \(x\); the set \(A_{\underline{c}}\) of Artinian sets of type \(\underline{c}\) has for cardinal \(2^{\underline{c}}\).

b) If \(\underline{c}\) is strongly inaccessible, the set \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) of Artinian sets of type strict \(\underline{c}\) is a universe of cardinal \(\underline{c}\); the cardinal \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}) = \sup_{x\in\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}} \mathrm{card}(x)\) is \(\underline{c}\).

c) If a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) admits an element of cardinal \(\underline{c}\), any Artinian set of type \(\underline{c}\) belongs to \(\mathcal{U}\) (in other words \(A_{\underline{c}} \subset \mathcal{U}\)).

c') If a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) is non-empty, any Artinian set of finite type is an element of \(\mathcal{U}\).
Before demonstrating th. 2, let us deduce in some illuminating corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1

If a universe \(\mathcal{U}\) is Artinian, then \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\) is strongly inaccessible, and \(\mathcal{U}\) is the set of Artinian sets of strict type \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\).

Indeed let's set \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) = \sup_{x\in\mathcal{U}} \mathrm{card}(x)\). He is a highly inaccessible cardinal (beginning of No. 5). Let us first assume it is uncountable; then, for any infinite cardinal \(\underline{c} < \underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\), any Artinian set of type \(\underline{c}\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by c); so all together artinian of strict type \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) is element of \(\mathcal{U}\) by lemma of n° 5. The latter assertion remains valid if \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\) is countable by c'). Conversely, according to (\(\mathcal{U}\).I), all element of \(\mathcal{U}\) is of strict type \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})\). So \(\mathcal{U}\) is the universe \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}(\mathcal{U})}\) of b), hence \(\underline{c}(\mathcal{U}) = \mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U})\) by b).

It results from the horn. 1 that an Artinian universe is determined uniquely by its cardinal (also highly inaccessible). We therefore have a “one-to-one correspondence” between universes artinians and cardinals highly inaccessible. In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

The inclusion relation \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{U}'\) between Artinian universes is a good order relationship.

Indeed the relation \(\underline{c} \leq \underline{c}'\) between cardinals is a relation of good order (chap. III) and, with the notations of b) of th. 2, the relations \(\underline{c} \leq \underline{c}'\) and \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}} \subset \mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}'}\) are equivalent.
Note that th. 2, b) gives a second demonstration of th. 1 (No. 5).

Let's move on to the demonstration of th. 2. Given a set \(A\), we will call string of \(A\) any finite sequence \((x_j)_{j=0,\ldots,n}\) such that \(x_0 = A\) and that \(x_{j+1} \in x_j\) for \(j = 0, \ldots, n-1\). The strings of \(A\) form a set, according to the schema of selection-meeting; note the \(G(A)\). Given a string \(X = (x_j)_{j=0,\ldots,n}\), the strings of the form \((x_i)_{i=0,\ldots,q}\) with \(q \leq n\) will be said to be smaller than \(X\); we thus obtain, on \(G(A)\), an ordered set structure. For \(A\) to be Artinian, it is necessary and sufficient that \(G(A)\) is a “Noetherian” ordered set (i.e. satisfying the conditions equivalents of Chap. III, § 6, n° 5).
We will show that:
(*) If \(A\) is Artinian, it is uniquely determined by the isomorphism class of the ordered set \(G(A)\).

Indeed, given an ordered set \(G\) and an element \(g \in G\), we will note \(S(g)\) the set of \(g' \in G\) such that \(g < g'\) and that \(g \leq h \leq g'\) implies \(h = g\) or \(h = g'\) (in other words all of the “immediate successors” of \(g\)). Consider the application \(\theta\) which any string \(X = (x_0, \ldots, x_n)\) of \(G(A)\) matches the set \(x_n\); we then have, for all \(X \in G(A)\): \[ \theta(X) = \{ \theta(X') \mid X' \in S(X) \} . \] As \(A\) is the image by \(\theta\) of the smallest element \(X_0 = (A)\) of \(G(A)\), we will just have to show that \(\theta\) is uniquely determined by the isomorphism class of the set ordered \(G(A)\). Now this follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1

Let \(G\) be a Noetherian ordered set and \(\varphi\) be a map of \(G\) such that, for all \(g \in G\), we have: \[ \varphi(g) = \{ \varphi(g') \mid g' \in S(g) \} . \tag{1} \] Then \(\varphi\) is uniquely determined. Moreover, if \(\mathcal{U}\) is a universe containing an element equipotent to \(G\), \(\varphi\) takes its values in \(\mathcal{U}\).
The hypothesis implies that we have \(\varphi(g) = \varnothing\) if \(g\) is a maximal element of \(G\).

Let, in fact, \(\varphi\) and \(\varphi'\) be two maps such that (1) is true; if \(\varphi \neq \varphi'\), the set of \(g \in G\) such that \(\varphi(g) \neq \varphi'(g)\) is non-empty, therefore admits one element maximal \(h\) because \(G\) is Noetherian; we then have \(\varphi(g) = \varphi'(g)\) for all \(g > h\), in particular for any "successor" \(g \in S(h)\); hence \(\varphi(h) = \varphi'(h)\) by (1), which is a contradiction; we therefore have \(\varphi = \varphi'\). Now let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe containing an element \(x\) equipotent to \(G\); let us show that \(\varphi\) takes its values ​​in \(\mathcal{U}\); otherwise let \(h\) be an element maximum among \(g \in G\) such as \(\varphi(g) \notin \mathcal{U}\); we have \(\varphi(g') \in \mathcal{U}\) for all \(g' \in S(h)\) so that \[ \varphi(h) = \{ \varphi(g') \mid g' \in S(h) \} \] is part of \(\mathcal{U}\); now, as its cardinal is less than \(\mathrm{card}(G)\) therefore to the cardinal of a element of \(\mathcal{U}\), we have \(\varphi(h) \in \mathcal{U}\) (n° 1, prop. 7); this contradiction shows that \(\varphi\) takes its values in \(\mathcal{U}\).

That being said, let's demonstrate a) of th. 2. We can limit ourselves to the assertion not fulfilled, because the other follows immediately. Let \(\underline{c}\) be an infinite cardinal. If \(A\) is a set of type \(\underline{c}\) we a: \[ \mathrm{card}(G(A)) \leq \underline{c} . \tag{2} \] Indeed, if we note \(A_n\) the set of order components \(n\) of \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{card}(A_1) \leq \underline{c}\) and \(\mathrm{card}(A_{n+1}) \leq \underline{c} \cdot \mathrm{card}(A_n)\), hence \(\mathrm{card}(A_n) \leq \underline{c}^n\) by recursion; or \(\underline{c}^n = \underline{c}\) because \(\underline{c}\) is infinite (chap. III); hence \(\mathrm{card}(\bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathbb{N})\cdot\underline{c} = \underline{c}\) (chap. III); or \(G(A)\) is a set of finite sequences of elements of \(\bigcup_n A_n\), so that we have inequality (2) (chap. III). This being said, if \(E\) is a set of cardinal \(\underline{c}\), the data of a order structure on a part \(E'\) of \(E\) is equivalent to the data of the part of \(E \times E\) formed \((x,y)\) such as \(x \in E'\), \(y \in E'\) and \(x \leq y\). So, by virtue of (2), the classes isomorphism of ordered sets \(G(A)\) (where \(A\) is of type \(\underline{c}\)) form a set \(\mathfrak{G}_{\underline{c}}\), and we have \(\mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{G}_{\underline{c}}) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{P}(E \times E)) = 2^{\underline{c}}\). Let \(\mathfrak{G}'_{\underline{c}}\) be the part of \(\mathfrak{G}_{\underline{c}}\) formed from classes of Noetherian ordered sets having a smallest element; if, to all \(G \in \mathfrak{G}'_{\underline{c}}\), we match the value of the function \(\varphi\) of lemma 1 to the most small element of \(G\), we obtain an application \(\theta\) of \(\mathfrak{G}'_{\underline{c}}\) whose image contains all Artinian sets of type \(\underline{c}\). These latter therefore form a set \(A_{\underline{c}}\), and we have \(\mathrm{card}(A_{\underline{c}}) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{G}'_{\underline{c}}) \leq \mathrm{card}(\mathfrak{G}_{\underline{c}}) \leq 2^{\underline{c}}\).

It remains to be seen that we have \(\mathrm{card}(A_{\underline{c}}) = 2^{\underline{c}}\). For this it is enough to see that there exists a artinian set \(B\) of type \(\underline{c}\) and cardinal \(\underline{c}\), because the parts of \(B\) will then be elements of \(A_{\underline{c}}\). This existence results from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2

For any cardinal \(\underline{c}\), there exists an Artinian set \(B\) of type \(\underline{c}\) and of cardinal \(\underline{c}\).

We proceed by transfinite induction on \(\underline{c}\). For \(\underline{c} = 0\) we have no choice, and we take \(B = \varnothing\). If \(\underline{c}\) has a predecessor \(\underline{c}'\), let \(B'\) be an Artinian set of type \(\underline{c}'\) and cardinal \(\underline{c}'\); we then have \(\underline{c} \leq 2^{\underline{c}'}\), so that there exists a part \(B\) of \(\mathfrak{P}(B')\) of cardinal \(\underline{c}\); elements of \(B\) are parts of \(B'\) and therefore have cardinals \(\leq \underline{c}' \leq \underline{c}\); the Higher order components of \(B\) are components of \(B'\), and therefore also have cardinals \(\leq \underline{c}' \leq \underline{c}\). Finally, if \(\underline{c}\) has no predecessor, we choose, for any cardinal \(\underline{c}_\lambda < \underline{c}\), an artinian set \(B_\lambda\) of type \(\underline{c}_\lambda\) and cardinal \(\underline{c}_\lambda\); then \(B = \bigcup_\lambda B_\lambda\) answers the question. This proves Lemma 2, and completes the proof of part a).

Let's move on to b). Let \(\underline{c}\) be a strongly inaccessible cardinal. We already know, by a), that the Artinian sets of strict type \(\underline{c}\) form a set \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\). The fact that \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) is a universe results immediately from the properties (AR.I) to (AR.IV) of the Artinian sets (beginning of n°), and increases of cardinals similar to those of prop. 10 of No. 5. The relationship \(\sup_{x\in\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}} \mathrm{card}(x) = \underline{c}\) results from Lemma 2, applied to the cardinals \(< \underline{c}\). Finally, for show that \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}) = \underline{c}\), suppose first \(\underline{c}\) uncountable; according to the lemma of No. 5, \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) is the union \(\bigcup_{\underline{d}<\underline{c}} A_{\underline{d}}\), where \(A_{\underline{d}}\) designates the set of sets artinian type \(\underline{d}\); or we have \(\mathrm{card}(A_{\underline{d}}) = 2^{\underline{d}} < \underline{c}\) (by a)); on the other hand all of the cardinals \(\underline{d} < \underline{c}\) has a cardinal \(\leq \underline{c}\) (chap. III); hence \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}) \leq \underline{c} \cdot \underline{c} = \underline{c}\), and also \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}) \geq \underline{c}\) because \(\mathrm{card}(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}) \geq \mathrm{card}(A_{\underline{d}}) = 2^{\underline{d}}\) for all \(\underline{d} < \underline{c}\).

The case \(\underline{c} = 0\) being trivial, remains the case where \(\underline{c}\) is the countably infinite cardinal. In this case \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) is the set of Artinian sets of finite type (i.e. finite as well as all their components), and we use a nice combinatorial result:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3

(D. König?). Consider two infinite sequences \((E_n)_{n\geq 1}\), \((\varphi_n)_{n\geq 1}\) of finite sets \(E_n\) and maps \(f_n : E_{n+1} \to E_n\). If there is no infinite sequence \((x_n)_{n\geq 1}\) such that \(x_n \in E_n\) and that \(f_n(x_{n+1}) = x_n\) for all \(n\), then \(E_n\) is empty for \(n\) large enough.

In other words, if all sequences \((x_n)\) such as \(f_n(x_{n+1}) = x_n\) are finite, their lengths are bounded. This can be expressed in terms of projective limits: a limit projective of non-empty finite sets is non-empty (cf. Top. Gén., Chap. I, 2nd ed. §9, n°6, prop.8, 2°)).

Let us reason, in fact, through the absurd. If there are non-empty \(E_n\) for \(n\) arbitrarily large, no \(E_n\) is empty (because \(E_n = \varnothing\) results in \(E_{n+1} = \varnothing\) given the existence of \(f_n : E_{n+1} \to E_n\)). Let us call finite sequences \((x_j)_{1\leq j\leq n}\) “consistent” such that \(f_j(x_{j+1}) = x_j\) for \(j = 1, \ldots, n-1\). Let us demonstrate, by induction on \(n\), the existence of a coherent sequence \((a_1, \ldots, a_n)\) (\(a_i \in E_i\)) which, for all \(q \geq n\), can be extended into one coherent sequence \((a_1, \ldots, a_n, x_{n+1}, \ldots, x_q)\) of length \(q\). This is obvious for \(n = 0\), because no \(E_q\) is empty. Let's move from \(n\) to \(n + 1\). If, for all \(x \in f_n^{-1}(\{a_n\}) \subset E_{n+1}\), all coherent sequences extending \((a_1, \ldots, a_n, x)\) had lengths bounded by an integer \(q(x)\), then all coherent sequences extending \((a_1, \ldots, a_n)\) would be lengths bounded (by \(\sup_x q(x)\)) because \(E_{n+1}\) is finite; therefore there exists \(a_{n+1} \in f_n^{-1}(\{a_n\})\) such that the coherent sequence \((a_1, \ldots, a_n, a_{n+1})\) admits extensions of arbitrary length. This being the case, we have an infinite sequence \((a_n)_{n\geq 1}\) which contradicts the hypothesis.

It follows from Lemma 3 that if \(A\) is an Artinian set of finite type, then the set ordered \(G(A)\) of its strings is finite: we take, in fact, for \(E_n\) the set of strings \((x_n \in x_{n-1} \in \ldots \in x_1 \in A)\) to \(n + 1\) terms (which is finite because the components of \(A\) of order \(\leq n + 1\) are finite in number and are all finite), and for \(f_n\) the application \((x_{n+1} \in x_n \in x_{n+1} \ldots) \mapsto (x_n \in x_{n-1} \in \ldots)\). But all classes of isomorphism of finite ordered sets is countable: in fact, the data of a order structure on a finite part of \(\mathbb{N}\) is equivalent to that of its graph, which is a part finished of \(\mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}\); on the other hand the set of finite parts of a countable set is countable (chap. III). It follows from (*) and Lemma 1, that the set \(G\) of sets artinians of finite type is countable. It is infinite by Lemma 2 (or, more simply, by use of the sequence \((z_n)_{n\geq 0}\) defined using \(z_0 = \varnothing\), \(z_{n+1} = \{z_n\}\)). This ends the demonstration of b).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

We have just demonstrated that, if \(A\) is an Artinian set of finite type, it has only a finite number of components. There therefore exists an integer \(n\) such that \(A\) is of type \(n\).

Let's move on to the proof of c). Let \(\underline{c}\) be an infinite cardinal, \(\mathcal{U}\) a universe admitting a cardinal element \(\underline{c}\), and \(A\) an Artinian set of type \(\underline{c}\). Then the ordered set \(G(A)\) has a cardinal \(\leq \underline{c}\) (formula (2) above). The second assertion of Lemma 1, applied to \(G(A)\), shows that the map \((x_0, \ldots, x_n) \mapsto x_n\) of \(G(A)\) takes its values in \(\mathcal{U}\). In other words, all the components of \(A\) are elements of \(\mathcal{U}\). This demonstrates c). The demonstration of c') is analogous: if \(A\) is an Artinian set of finite type, we have just seen that \(G(A)\) is finished; as \(\mathcal{U}\) is non-empty, it contains an element equipotent to \(G(A)\) by the horn. to prop. 7 (No. 1). C.Q.F.D.
7. Muddy metamathematical remarks
a) The axiom “everything together is Artinian” is harmless. Indeed, if we have a model \(M\) of Set Theory, the set \(M'\) of the artinian elements of \(M\) is also a model (see prop. 11).

b) The axiom (A.6) of universes (and the equivalent axiom (A'.6) of cardinals strongly inaccessible) is independent of the rest of Set Theory. Indeed either \(\underline{c}\) the first highly inaccessible non-countable cardinal. The universe \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) sets strict type artinians \(\underline{c}\) (th. 2, b)) is a model of Set Theory without (A.6): we call “sets” the elements of \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\), the “relation of belonging” is the restriction to \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) ordinary, etc. The “universes” of the model are therefore the universes ordinary which are elements of \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\). Now we have seen that the only universes which are elements of \(\mathcal{U}_{\underline{c}}\) are the two pequeños \(\mathcal{U}_0 = \varnothing\) And \(\mathcal{U}_1\). So \(\mathcal{U}_1\) is a “set” which is not an element of any “universe”. We therefore have a model of Set Theory where (A.6) is false.

c) Bourbaki was too cautious in simply “assuming” that the axiom of infinity (A.5) is independent of the previous axioms and diagrams. It actually is, because the universe countable \(\mathcal{U}_1\) of Artinian sets of finite type is a model where (A.5) is false, and where the preceding axioms and diagrams are true.
d) It would be very interesting to demonstrate that the axiom (A.6) of the universes is harmless. This seems difficult and it is even unprovable, says Paul Cohen.

The adjective "silty" in the title means that we didn't take the trouble, in building models, to cannulate the symbol \(\tau\) so that it does not bring out any. The key of that, if we consider a model \(M\), is to replace the ordinary \(\tau_x(R(x))\) by: \[ \tau_x(R(x) \text{ et } x \in M) . \] It is still necessary to verify that this transforms ordinary quantifiers into quantifiers formerly called “typical”: \[ (\forall x \in M) \text{ et } (\exists x \in M) . \]
Exercises
1) Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geq 1\). Show that the set of Artinian sets of type \(n\) is infinite (the sets \(z_0 = \varnothing\), \(z_1 = \{\varnothing\}\), \(z_{q+1} = \{z_q\}\) are of type 1).

2) Let's call height of a set \(A\) the upper bound (finite or infinite) of the integers \(n\) such that there exists a sequence \(x_n \in x_{n-1} \in \ldots \in x_0 = A\). Show that the sets of height \(\leq n\) are finite and form a finite set, whose cardinality \(p_n\) is calculated using of \(p_0 = 1\), \(p_{n+1} = 2^{p_n}\) (proceed by induction on \(n\), noting that the elements of a set \(A\) of height \(\leq n\) are sets of height \(\leq n - 1\)).
3) Let \(G\) be a Noetherian ordered set admitting a smallest element \(g_0\) and such that for all \(h \in G\), the set of \(g \leq h\) are finite and totally ordered.
a) Show that every element \(h \neq g_0\) of \(G\) admits one and only one predecessor.

b) Let \(\varphi\) be the application of \(G\) defined in Lemma 1 (i.e. \(\varphi(g)\) is the set of \(\varphi(g')\) where \(g'\) iterates through the set of successors of \(g\)). We set \(A = \varphi(g_0)\). Show that \(A\) is an Artinian set. For \(g \in G\), let \(g_0 < g_1 < \ldots < g_n = g\) be the sequence of elements \(\leq g\); let's set \(f(g) = (\varphi(g_0), \varphi(g_1), \ldots, \varphi(g_n))\); show that \(f\) is an application increasing and surjective of \(G\) on the ordered set \(G(A)\) of the text. Show that, if \(f\) is injective, it is an isomorphism of \(G\) on \(G(A)\).

c) For \(g \in G\), let \(M_g\) be the set of upper bounds of \(g\). We assume that, for all pair of distinct elements \(g, g'\) having same predecessor \(p\), ordered sets \(M_g\) and \(M_{g'}\) are non-isomorphic. Show that the map \(f\) of b) is then an isomorphism of \(G\) on \(G(A)\).

[If \(f(g) = f(g')\) with \(g \neq g'\) and if \(g_0 < g_1 < \ldots < g_n = g\) and \(g'_0 < g'_1 < \ldots < g'_{n'} = g'\) are the sequence of elements \(\leq g\) and those of elements \(\leq g'\), show that \(n = n'\), and that we can assume that \(g\) and \(g'\) have the same predecessor \(p\); then consider the set of \(p \in G\) such that there exist two distinct successors \(g, g'\) of \(p\) such as \(f(g) = f(g')\), a maximal element \(q\) of this set, and two distinct successors \(h, h'\) of \(q\) such as \(f(h) = f(h')\); note that the restrictions of \(f\) to \(M_h\) and \(M_{h'}\) are injectives, therefore (by b)) are isomorphisms of \(M_h\) on \(G(\varphi(h))\) and of \(M_{h'}\) on \(G(\varphi(h'))\); deduce from the hypothesis of non-isomorphism of \(M_h\) and \(M_{h'}\), that we have \(\varphi(h) \neq \varphi(h')\), which contradicts \(f(h) = f(h')\)].

N.B. Exercise 3) gives very precise information on how “made” the Artinian sets. We already knew that such a set \(A\) is determined by the isomorphism class of the ordered set \(G(A)\) (lemma 1). We now know how to characterize ordered sets \(G\) isomorphic to \(G(A)\):
α) \(G\) is Noetherian and admits a smaller element;
β) For all \(g \in G\), the set of \(h \leq g\) is totally ordered and finite (hence the existence and the uniqueness of the predecessor of \(g\));
γ) If \(g, g'\) are distinct elements having the same predecessor, the set \(M_g\) of upper bounds of \(g\) and that of \(M_{g'}\) of the upper bounds of \(g'\) are not isomorphic.

4) Let \((A_n)_{n\geq 0}\) be the sequence of finite ordinals, defined by \(A_0 = \varnothing\), \(A_{n+1} = A_n \cup \{A_n\}\). Show that the ordered set \(G(A_n)\) has \(2^n\) elements, and that the number of its height elements \(q\) (in the sense of exercise 2)) is \(\binom{n}{q}\).
BIBLIOGRAPHY
[1] B. Mitchell, Theory of categories, Academic Press (1965).
